review_text: Carmageddon is a vehicular combat 'racing' game by UK developer Stainless Software released in 1997 to much acclaim for its advanced car damage model and sandbox style freedom and plenty of controversy for its depictions of gory carnage against defenseless pedestrians. This lead to an initially censored release for the UK version with zombies that bled green, the only way it was allowed to be sold until a patch was released later on to restore the original palette. The German version didn't fare much better however and was given robots instead that bled oil. Carmageddon's main inspiration comes from the 1975 film Death Race 2000 and was originally going to be a tie-in game but licensing fell through on more than one occasion, to the point Stainless said stuff it let's just get it out.  Carmageddon's a free-for-all race where you're encouraged to do whatever you want whenever you like and there is no restrictions on where you can go or what is considered fair or cheating. Your opponents will often be boring enough to race around the circuit and collect their weak paychecks from the checkpoints scattered about. Your opponents can't actually win anything however and there is no position of your standing in the race on your HUD. That's because you don't have one, and it doesn't matter anyway. What does matter is causing as much chaos as possible by either running down pedestrians, and in creative and gorier ways to net additional credits, and ramming the other cars. Wasting an opponent will give you a lot of credits, and more time than any other activity.  There's a timer that counts down at the beginning of each event and all manner of shenanigans will give you extra to allow you to explore the environment at your heart's content. For all manner of juicy bonuses and hidden bonus dosh/time and various PUPs (power-ups in Carma slang) litter the circuit and the outskirts of the race. These can be pretty gosh darned handy, for example complete invulnerability, acme damage magnifier for really putting a dent in your opponents, or pedestrian electro ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ray for 30 seconds. The wacky power-downs however come in a sizable variety and are usually harmless enough, but make your vehicle harder to drive for a short period of time. Pinball mode though is a worthy mention as its especially nasty and you'll want to recover ASAP if you're flung into orbit to prevent reparing serious damage.  You cannot actually be wasted yourself (at least in this first installment) but credits earned during the race are often used to repair your car, and repairing and recovering when you're on your roof or side will be happening a lot. So it's crucial to scouring and looting the race as much as possible to get more money to have enough left over at the end of a race. If you lose more than you make during a race and you didn't have much left over to begin with when you began the race your bank will go into a screaming fit and pull the plug, and this constitutes as 'game over' here. Failing a race by running out of time isn't penalizing though and you can simply jump back in and try again. You use credits earned between races to upgrade your car's abilities via APO (armor, power, offensive), which are bare-bones as upgrades come and simply make you more resistant to impacts, more horsepower, and dish out extra abuse when colliding into your opponents.  All races are won in three Carma fashions: Kill all pedestrians, waste all opponents, or complete all laps. The latter is reserved for ponces however and won't really give you a whole lot of credits but it's there to sate your particular needs, you weirdo. Murdering every ped is no easy feat however, but most levels usually have a Pedestrian Respawn or two (or three) somewhere and memorizing the locations of these after you wipe out a quarter or two of the population and going back for it can give you an extra edge. Participating in races is also done by a ranking system (which was brought back in Reincarnation/Max Damage) and you start at a lowly 99 with three races to choose from and the penultimate goal is to be number one, along the way will be more challenging environments with more unhinged opponents (and cops in APCs who just refuse to get out of the way). Getting hundreds of thousands of credits an event will move the rank much more (up to 5 increases max or none whatsoever if you're absolute tripe).  The player can choose between two drivers at the start and a difficulty determining starting time in a race, credits and bonus time earned from activities and how aggressive your opponents are. It's best to start on 'normal' and see how you fare, and if you're the kind of person who hides under the table during thunderstorms then easy mode should be right up your alley. Max Damage and Die Anna are the main 'heroes' of the Death Race and drive a similar model of car. Max's all-round great Eagle is better suited to rammin' and slammin' however whereas Anna's Hawk is a faster but less strong car. Nevertheless Carmageddon is an early supporter of equality, and all manner of men and women are subjected to the same treatment. Be it behind the wheel or roadkill in the street. Sometimes when you waste a specific opponent you have a chance of stealing and adding it to your collection. Some of these can be very powerful, others not so much. Beating the game will unlock every single one, including those labeled as 'cheat', your reward for being the baddest dude.  Visually the game is competent, with adequate detail for the environments, early 3D models for vehicles and props that are well designed and pedestrians are rendered as 2D sprites via digitized actors. Their deaths are often extremely gruesome, easily making this the goriest entry of the series even with later installments featuring 3D peds that can lose their limbs. Sound design is loud and crunchy, and impacts with the environment and opponents feel very raw, and the peds incredibly nasty and satisfying. The soundtrack also features four tracks from Fear Factory's 1995 album Demanufacture in instrumental only form, most fitting choice given the game's militant gameplay in a pseudo-apocalyptic near future world.  I have very few complaints with the way this game plays, maybe that it goes on for far too long (36 races) especially if you're trying to do the best you can on each race which can run anywhere from 45 minutes to an hour on average. Late game opponents become real damage sponges if you don't nick a better set of wheels (the Eagle/Hawk just won't cut it even at max stats) and cops are extremely difficult to waste and become very common near the end but if you manage to steal an especially hard to acquire ride in the event 'Blood on the Rooftops' you will know sweet, sweet revenge for all the trouble they were giving you early on and finish the last handful of events with pure bloody malice.  Carmageddon is decidedly British in its portrayal of humor, with plenty of tongue-in-cheek jokes, British slang, and raunchiness suited for a mature audience. Which only makes sense given the dev is UK based. There's plenty of pop culture references from the era, akin to games like Duke Nukem 3D (even has a reference to OJ Simpson here too!). This should've been more than enough proof that Carmageddon's intentions were never meant to be mean-spirited or shock jockery (a suggestion to add pregnant women peds was flat out no'd), just the kind of things that make the devs ♥♥♥♥ themselves laughing. And you can tell they had a great time putting this game together, a real labor of love. Carmageddon also has an equally humorous and interesting development history if you look into it more. If you think most racing games are too dull and traditional and you want to insert more mayhem in to your driving experience than this game comes as highly recommended! And if you still can't get enough the Splat Pack expansion has even more content to offer. The antidote to all racers.  4.0/5

review_text: I enjoyed this game for about 3 our of the 4 hours it took me to finish it, the visuals are phenominal, and the storytelling is alright. That being said... There are so many different things to say and routes to take that it would only make sense that your choices would matter in the end, right? Wrong. No matter what you say, the ending is always the same (with one other mediocre,ridiculous ending that is not even remotely satisfying) with the slight exception of conversational elements throughout.  The most intriguing part of this game for me was the mystery. And as the mystery unfolded, it pulled me deeper into the story! This was amazing. Until... there is actually no resolution whatsoever. There are implied resolutions, and all of it is just that    the 'emotional lifeline' on the other end of your radio was screwing you over the whole time after taking you on an emotional roller coaster.     Albiet thought provoking, beautiful, and relatively entertaining the first time, there is little to no reason to replay this game, making the 4 hours and the lackluster ending not really worth anything over $10 for me.

review_text: Game of Thrones is developed by Cyanide Studios as an action RPG following the adventures of a Night Watch’s brother and a priest of R’hllor. As a huge Game of Thrones fan, I love content related to the seven kingdoms as well as the anything below the narrow sea, and yet somehow I could not get into Game of Thrones game.  At first glance, it seems like an amazing experience. You see the prologue which is pretty amazing narrated by Varys, you can customize your character and pick one of the three classes in the game each with different abilities, customize your attributes, skills and traits. As a big bonus you also get a unique tree for each of the characters, in the case of the Night watch it is your very own hound and a skin changer tree while the R’hllor priest gets a new set of skills devoted to the holy fire, such as setting your opponents on fire. You can also pick your preferred weapons, such as maces, swords, axes, bows or crossbows. The game also has other positive points such as amazing sound, a decent storyline and the ability to make a game based on game of thrones while completely avoiding any spoilers from the books and the tv show. The game essentially has its own story.   On the other hand, the game suffers from quite some big issues. For instance, the combat feels sluggish and boring, the graphics are quite low quality and the worst of all, the voice acting in some cases along with some plot areas are cringe-worthy. The voicing at some point felt as Christian Bale’s imitation of the Batman instead of the R’hllor priest or the brother of the Night Watch. It does not feel natural and it ruins a part of the game’s ambiance. Another point that can frustrate some players is DLC skins in the store. The game has weapon sets sold in the store as well as dog skins which can annoy some people considering it is a single-player game.  Overall, I would give Game of Thrones a 5 out of 10. I do not personally recommend it as an avid Game of Thrones fan but if you can get past all the quirks, you will enjoy the game quite a bit. review_text: This is most likely going to be one of the most negative recommendations you will ever read, but I tend to criticize heavily the things I enjoy and despite my criticisms I do enjoy Planetside 2, as you may have guess with the amount of hours logged. But this game has serious issues bugging it and they will ensure the demise of this game sooner before later, as a shooter it is fun but as an MMO it will not be able to sustain itself much longer. And it’s up to you to determine if putting up with these issues is worth the rush of being in massive scale battles that no other game is capable of.    The first issue I have with the game is when many of the game’s individual elements put together it seems to fall apart. It’s like each mechanic of gameplay was designed in a different room with no knowledge of what the final product was supposed to even look like. Either that or the development team was so intent on copying popular shooters that focus on small 4-64 player battles they gave no thought what so ever to how much of an impact it would have with hundreds of players. One example of this is that the developers gave people the ability to plant ammo packs without restraint, giving infinite ammo and unlimited healing (seriously you can revive people in less time than it takes to kill them). The best strategy for winning an evenly matched fight is to simply bore the enemy until they give up and leave. With unlimited healing and ammo on both sides many battles result in a standstill with my squad leader saying something along the lines of “let’s go someplace else.”   Another flaw of Planetside 2 is the level design. The bases look good aesthetically but function poorly. I think the developers are slowly becoming aware of this is constant changes are being made, however I think if you took the words “Cram as many people as you can into a choke point” and copy pasted it across 10 pages you would have the entire design document. This choke point obsession combined with the mechanics of infinite healing/ammo creates the ‘bore the enemy into submission’ battles. You either have the population at the base to steamroll it, or you have the population to get steamrolled. If you and your enemies have even remotely even forces chances are you just shoot at each other until someone falls asleep at the keyboard. If you are defending a base you can always go after the mobile spawn points, but since those are in infinite supply as well they can always bring more. With lackluster stage design and small scale shooter mechanics pretending they can work in an MMO are evidence of the chief issue of Planetside 2; that many of its ideas just don’t work well together. As long as it continues to function with these ideas it will be nothing but a source of constant issues.   The other issue I will address is the system of economics. Basic economics tell us that when there is an abundance of something it has no value. It is because of this reason many people consider Planetside 2’s predecessor to be superior. In the original Planetside not everyone could do everything, therefore what you chose to do had value because not everyone would be capable of it. If you decided to drive a tank, it was meaningful because not everyone could. As a result tanks were able to shake up the gameplay, add variety to the battlefield and make things interesting. In Planetside 2 the developers gave everyone the ability to drive a tank and now they are worthless, instagibable by practically anyone (including medics). It is because everyone has access to everything that the gameplay gets boiled down into a gray homogenous amoeba like mass labeled zerg, anything that is not zerg will be consumed and nerfed down until it is. This cripples the gameplay significantly. Any attempt to introduce anything new and interesting will be passed through the consideration that everyone will be doing it and therefore it must be absorbed as part of the zergy mass, and offer no real variety to that mass. Most recently they introduced a few new weapons for the liberator bomber, and to be honest, they suck. And they must suck, because if they were actually any good everyone would be using them and nothing else. Essentially this system only backs the developers into a corner of delivering nothing but piles of mediocrity.    The final issue to bring up is the grind. If you give an assault rifle to a private 1st class and have him ordered to shoot a hole in the wall, he will shoot a hole in the wall. If you then give that weapon to a staff sergeant and have him ordered to shoot a hole in the wall, he will shoot a hole in the wall. The hole will not be any deeper, the weapon will not be more powerful nor will his armor protect him anymore, a magic pixie does not come along and sprinkle its dust on your weapons and armor because you advance in rank. A good competitive shooter at its core is a game that relies on skill, strategy, tactics and teamwork. Magic pixie dust to make weapons and armor more powerful in the hands of higher ranked people betrays these very concepts. It has been the chief failing behind many games (APB, Brink, Huxley, and I’m sure you can name a few others). And while Planetside 2 tries to avoid magic pixie dust, it fails. Many times people had my heavy assault dead to rights with solid out maneuvering, but because I put a lot of magic pixie dust on my shield and armor they stand no chance no matter how skilled or strategic they are, and don’t even get me started on how bad magic pixie dust determines the victor in vehicle combat. The grind always fails shooters. Many modern shooters have a grind, and there is a reason they are often referred to as “flavor of the month” shooters. I have no idea why SOE thought that “flavor of the month” shooter mechanics would work in an MMO that needs to be sustainable over years. This is another reason why many people consider the first Planetside superior, because the developers understood this. A Gauss rifle in the hands of a newbie was the same in the hands of a veteran, no magic pixie dust, just the player’s skill with the weapon. I believe the proof of this lies in Steam’s wonderful statistics. There is a reason Team Fortress 2, and Counterstrike in its many flavors retain more people over time than Planetside 2 or any of the Call of Duty games, simply because they are not nearly as much the grind fest. For an MMO to succeed you need to establish long term success, not short term popularity.    As I said before I enjoy Planetside 2, but I don’t see it lasting much longer. The way much of the gameplay doesn’t seem to go hand in hand, the poor economic decision to give everything to everyone and the constant grinding just to hopefully someday stand on even ground (which most good shooters immediately start you out at) make it wear thin quickly. It’s like a mosh pit at a speed metal concert. It’s fun, it’s fast, it’s furious, and can be a blast at times. But when you’re done there isn’t a real driving incentive to go back to it anytime soon. If you’re late getting into it you’re going to get your butt kicked to the point you might just leave early, and you know anything new they add is just going to get lost in the moshing.

review_text: So far I am close to 70 hours into it. This game is in no way GOTY worthy.   The story feels so disconnected and only at the end of ACT1 the missions really connect and tell a coherent story.   The core gameplay and feel of the game is great but sadly if your missions are 95.3% the same it gets repetitive and boring after a while. I mean you can only scout and infiltrate so many outposts until it gets boring.   The antagonist Skullface gets way too little character development and I wish we would have seen more of him and got to fight him in a boss fight. Furthermore his Skulls unit was used way too little and I think the missions that feature them are the best in the game because those ♥♥♥♥ers are just so creepy and interesting.   Recycling missions from Act1 in Act2 is a no go imo, should have just given you the ability to choose a higher difficulty when you want to play through them again.   The weapon development feels lackluster, I really hoped for a more complex system. Same goes for weapon customizations. it feels like 70% of the customization is just visuals that do not impact the performance of your weapon at all.   The lack of a good fast travel method is annoying. Shipping yourself to another outpost is a cool way on paper but implemented into the game it's really annoying. Furthermore making deployment take so long is annoying as well. I wish I had the option to skip that sequence cause after seeing it the 50th time it just feels like a waste of time and subtracts from the fun experience.   The graphics look good. I really can't complain about them but I also need to admit that my standards on that front are fairly low.

review_text: Early Access Review

Pros:  + Plenty of tracks with their own set of challenges to learn and practice on + Different speed classes that can be used for practice or to add challenege + Visuals are unique, not cartoony but not realistic either, nice mid point.  + Responsive controls, I play with a PS4 pad using DS4Windows + Really fast gameplay that requires practice + Recent Europa DLC was free conent, adding a new area with five tracks + Recent updates added more customization options + Active/Passive/Upgrades makes tuning ships simple and does help in balancing multiplayer   Cons:   - AI are either the perfect racer that mocks you or the idiot that loses control and rams you, resulting in you spining to a near stop pretty much killing any chance you had - Only six teams for now, though they do race different enough to feel different - Basic Upgrade system, four total, one per stat for each ship - Would prefer a point system where you can add/remove to tune a ship instead of passives and upgrades - You can only used assigned active power ups, making racing less interesting, although this mitigates RNG.  - Basic Customization, no RGB slider, set colors. - Further Customization options tied to a drop system - No option to import files to make custom skin, texture or pattern options  Notes:  I really enjoy the game, but it does feel simplefied to some extent. The only issue I really have is while it is cool to have added customization options added to the game for free, I do frown upon the drop system.   - I do not like the feeling have having to farm until I see a drop - I do not like how options for Liveries (patterns) are for individual ships but color schemes unlock for all ships - I do not like one drop a day - When it was just color schemes, duplicates were frequent and many people had the same duplicates, so limted trading.  - I do not like one day having a drop in five minutes and then having the next day take a two hour session to see a drop - I would rather have something like a loayalty system where racing for a team earns points to unlock team exclusive options.   I will admit, it is nice to see something besides paid DLC but considering the limited drop rate and sheer amount of drops due to liveries being added, it is a little disheartening when you see a fourth Deep Sky Blue or have a sweet pattern drop for a team you never race with. Also, if it meant supporting the game, I would not mind throwing a few dollars at color schemes or patterns. At least anything drop related is merely cosmetic.

review_text: THis game took me 19 hours to complete. And I felt every last second of that. There are games, that aren't really good but are still worth checking and spending time on to see all the good ideas, all the good things the game may have. And sometimes, even while knowing that overall the game is not good, you will enjoy a game like that. Lords of Shadow is not a game like that, simply because it gives *way* too little for what time and patience it asks in return. It does have an interesting story, an interesting take on the famous series. It does look stylish and pretty, although artistically, not technologically (pretty low res textures and pre-renered movies, rather poor optimization, other small issues). But still - really pretty. It also has a *few* really cool fights, which are inventive, exciting and challenging enough, without feeling unfair or broken. So, that're the good things. There are bad things, like overabundance of QTEs, with very outdated approach to the concept as well - they're used just for the sake of it, and in the unlikeliest moments. Like, say, opening some doors requires timed QTEs, not even God of War-like button mashing. Yet those are also not *that* notable. And this is the main problem with the game - it's a bit of good, a bit of bad and about 80% of boredom. Majority of the game feels like padding, something inserted just for the sake of wasting more of the player's time. In fact, there's even a special enemy/creature in the game, who's sole purpose is to waste player's time and you meet that creature 4 times, even though you will be fed up with it the first time. There's almost never any payoff to anything. You might hear of a cool enemy/location several chapters of 3-5 levels before you finally get to it, and nothing really happens during those levels... And the ex-DLC levels, which give this edition 'Ultimate' name, are absolutely horrible. Again, there are few nice ideas, but they're so not worth it. The story, which could've been a proper epilogue to the main story, setting up the sequel and ex-portable midquel, is a complete throwaway and degrades into farce in the last two levels, where Gabriel plays hide and seek with a powerful demon set on destroying the world. With the same elements, a much MUCH better game could've been made. And, I'm sure, MercurySteam could've done that. Maybe, the sequel will be that much better game... P.S. It's worth noticing several visual similarities to a classic game called Blade of Darkness, which was developed by another spanish development company, which then dissolved and the majority of it's team created MercurySteam - the developers of this game.

review_text: Despite the enjoyment I got throughout the first 30 hours of gameplay, the rest of the game just got more and more disappointing the more I looked into it.  Pros -Great shooting mechanics, way more improved over New Vegas and 3 -'Decent' graphics that beat the last installments by a landslide -Fun companions, some of the best I've seen in Fallout -Nicely designed world for the most part, lots of detail has been put into the world like the last games -Very good weapon crafting system that I fell in love with  Cons -Lack of Karma.   Karma basically determined what your character was, be it good or bad. This companion-based-only system of reputation is dumb and doesn't really matter to the world around you. See that red text on an item? Don't steal it, you'll lose karm- ohhhhh not anymore, it's just that Piper over there will hate you and not speak to you. Now you just play as the dad or mom that thinks they're bad/good, but nobody really cares because you were not programmed to be truly bad or good.  -Lack of Skills.   Remember when you were able to be a survivalist in the wasteland who had experience with guns, repair and lockpick in order to survive? Yeah, that's not here. Now you're just limited to your S.P.E.C.I.A.L. which does provide perks, but very little engagement as to what you want to be. You want to be able to build a sneaky guy? Oh just build all that up into agility now. It's more compressed and lacking compared to their original system. You know, lack of RPG elements like this just takes the whole roleplay idea out of this Fallout. Now you're forced to be the parent of a son who was taken away... Wanna be the badass veteran who woke up 210 years later in the middle of a battle, needing to choose sides or wanting to stay neutral for yourself? SORRY, GO SAVE YOUR SON AND ((((SPOILERS))) GET THE ENDING WHERE NOTHING MATTERED BECAUSE ALL YOU GET IS A SPEECH BY YOUR OWN CHARACTER THAT TALKS FOR SOME REASON. Sorry... that just really bothered me.  -Mass Effect style dialogue  Back in 3 and New Vegas your character was able to say all sorts of things to NPCs. This meant you had this long list of choices that you could read through and pick to respond to the NPC. These choices actually mattered as well, nowadays you just have this four-choice layout that makes you think you're playing a Bioware game, except the wording is now dumbed down to just 'Yes. No. Be ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Maybe.' And gone are the days of actually needing a speech skill to get past a requirement, now you can just cheese the dialogue by failing the red text choice again and again until RNG just grants you mercy that one time and you complete it.  -Dumb, boring, repetitive quests.  Where as Fallout 3 and New Vegas provided more memorable and meaningful quests, 4 provides waaaaay too many 'Hey, go here, kill stuff. Okay thanks here's your caps.' type of quests.   Tl:dr  Overall Fallout 4 C̶A̶N̶ ̶B̶E̶ ̶A̶ ̶G̶O̶O̶D̶ ̶G̶A̶M̶E̶, IS A GOOD GAME, just not a good FALLOUT game. If you cared little for the previous Fallout games, you'd definitely find some enjoyment in it. It certainly has some Fallout feel to it. It's got nice shooting, making it a good FPS. However, the lack of RPG elements, lackluster story and the watered down dialogue just kills it for me and, as you can probably tell, many diehard Fallout fans. I don't recommend this to any major Fallout fan, it'd just be a big disappointment that should not have been bought in the first place.  Nowadays I go back to it every now and then because I appreciate the work put into it like the environment, cinematic conversations and gunplay. I like to treat it more as a sandbox rather than an RPG story-adventure. Seriously, I legitimately love exploring post apocalyptic Massachusetts, but the stuff I mentioned above are just not there, and I'm just sad about that.  If Obsidian plans on making another Fallout, I dearly hope they bring back the good stuff I mentioned in the Cons, but right now, I suggest you play 3 or New Vegas if you truly want the RPG feel. New Vegas especially since it is IMO, the best Fallout.

Where do I start? I got gifted PAYDAY 2: GOTY at the start of 2015, and I've enjoyed a lot of my time with it. I had a lot of gripes that I was willing to write off because the game was still fun. Crimefest has made me look back on those gripes and make me wonder 'Is it worth returning?'  Music’s pretty damn good – the “release” tracks remind me of Wip3out, and they fit into a lot of heists without issue. Later tracks became leitmotifs for their specific heists (eg “Shoutout” for Meltdown), so whilst they can be played in other heists, they fit their accompanying heist best.  The game's Diesel engine has led to a lot of curious little idiosyncrasies; some as basic as a single world animation for melee weapons, and game-affecting things like enemy cops having no arm/leg hitboxes. OVK likely sees these as marginal errors, but people have shown that these errors CAN be fixed.  That leads me to the biggest problem of the game: the netcode. Dear god . When you’re in a server, you come to realise that everyone operates on separate time-frames. And these localised time-frames exist about a couple of seconds after the host's game. So you'll hear Bain telling you that someone's gone down, but they're still running around on your screen with an orange outline. Only when your game's caught up will you be allowed to help them up.  These netcode issues are at their worst during stealth missions. When every second of visibility counts, you don't want to be acting on a different time-frame to the game. Sooner or later, you'll end up standing still for a second to hide somewhere, and realise that a cameraman saw you. You might have moved, but the server saw you, and there’s jack that you can do about it. And this isn't to mention that ragdolls act differently on client and host games; I won't forget the time where a host's kill slid into a river on his side, but stayed ashore for me, allowing me to save the mission.   Why this?

review_text: A good roguelike is a game that features randomness, but also a tough-but-fair mentality. They let you understand the randomness, prepare for it, and stack the deck and load the dice in your favor. Good roguelikes are all about taking the time to get a good feel for the mechanics, and how best to manipulate a complex, nuanced world around you to the maximum effect, rather than just relying upon dumb luck.  This game isn't a good roguelike.  Prepare to experience the boredom of constantly restarting, hoping to get a good weapon which, if obtained, will ensure easy domination of the game, but instead losing most campaigns in the first or second sector because the RNG hates you.   Prepare for the 'adventure' of trying to achieve long-term goals like unlocking other ships through events that are absolutely random, and require just plain craking the slot machine arm time after time after time until the RNG forgets to spite you.

review_text: I successfully collected my 3 cards in less than an hour :/

This is only a first look review after about four hours playtime. I might update it at some point.   All in all a great roguelike game. Great atmosphere, superb sound design, fitting (if somewhat minimalistic) graphics, creepy enemies and lots of equipment to research and outfit your Deadnauts with. The game does a great job making you feel like a remote mission planner.. you feel very distant and disconnected from the life and death struggle of your Deadnauts on a strange alien ship. Yet, at the same time, you become more and more attached to these sensor blips on your screen after each (usually harrowing) mission.  But there are also a few things I find lacking. While I do like the challenging difficulty, the game just sometimes grabs you by the b***s and doesn't let go until you're dead. And that can happen in the very first mission, which makes this game rather frustrating for newcomers. If you can't handle RNG and missions going from everything is dandy straight to hell in a matter of seconds, you should stay away from this game. But that goes for any roguelike, really.   Another complaint I have, is that you can't seem to hire new Deadnauts during a campaign. You can only clone your existing five Deadnauts.. which makes playing the game with randomly generated characters completely unappealing, because you can just create five very good minmaxed Deadnauts since you're stuck with the same guys for the entirety of a campagin anyway. Which makes the game's claim that Deadnauts are expendable (says so in the intro) untrue.   Another thing I found annoying is that you can't abort a mission. You have to complete the objective, before you can evacuate your Deadnauts from the alien ship.. why? It would be cooler, if you could abandon missions, xcom style. Also, the mission objectives themselves seem to be lacking in variety. In about four hours of playtime and probably 10 - 15 missions I only ever had two objectives: to either find the ship's log or to destroy mysterious artifacts. But maybe I have been only unlucky sofar, since the missions are randomly generated. And the missions themselves (layouts) have been fairly varied.  It would also be nice, if the game would allow you to carry over researched equipment from one campaign to another. Starting at zero every single time you start a new campaign can become quite tiresome.  Despite those flaws (well, they are flaws only in my opinion ofc.) I still think this is a great atmospheric and in style unique game which deserves a closer look. There is a demo available on Screwflie's Deadnaut page, as far as I am aware.  In it's current state, I would give this game an HONEST 7/10 (honest as in 5 is average and everything above that is good). A great, but not perfect, game!  UPDATE: It took me about 7.5 hours to complete my first campaign (including character creation, so actual playtime was probably closer to seven hours). Sooner than I expected.. but then again, the game does offer high replay value. Mission objectives have become more varied since I started over, so that's good.   But I did notice another annoying thing: you perform actions by clicking on ingame popup texts (such as investigate, hack, etc...). The problem is that your Deadnauts can sometimes only interact with those, if you rotate your camera to a different angle.. which can be really infuriating in situations where every second counts. Luckily this bug happened only infrequently. I can still recommend this game to roguelike fans who don't mind the other stuff I have listed.

review_text: First of all, there are a lot of Space games that are coming to us all soon and I'll think many of them will be great...  But so far Star Conflict is, for now (and for me), the only game that is fully released and Free to play! Free to play is really free to play. I know - cause I started to play that way!  You can however choose to pay more or less real money to get more from the game - but it's up to you! For me, the most important argument to pay - is to support the developers!  I think they have made a perfect score in this: 1. Play the game for free 2. Don't like it? Erase it and you haven't spend your money!   Compare that to buying a game, play it for an hour and realize that the game is not for you. Your money is gone...  3. Like it? Continue play for free if you want. Yes, you can play this game forever without spending any money!  I'll have seen many pilots in this game who have played for long without spending any money. If I meet them in a fight it's the skill, ships level and how many we are against each other who wins! 2 against 1 is always a better start...  So why should you pay? First of all (I say it again) - to support the developers! Again - instead of beeing forced to pay $X to get a game or pay $X every month to play a game you have now the chance to pay what you think the game is worth or what you can afford!  So what do you get in the game if you pay? (I'll skip the details) - Faster ship-leveling = ships get stronger and faster (you can still level up a ship but it takes more time)  (Faster ship-leveling also means that you can access newer ships faster and access more parts of the game faster) - More loot (you can still loot a lot if you win a PvP game)  So the only positive effect is that, instead of playing for hours, you can direct get what you want.  Buying a DLC ship: The other way to spend money in this game is buy a DLC ship. This is the only way to get those ship. There are 50 of them but there are also 135(!) ships you can work your way to get (without spending any money).  Are those DLC ships better than the normal ships? Yes and no... If you level up a normal ship to maximum that ship is balanced to be equal to the DLC ship beacuse the DLC ship is maxed leveled from start. That is also a week point for the DLC ships. One of the most fun things in the game is to level up a ship.  So, for me I only use DLC ships in the Invasion mode (MMO part of the game). They are great cause they don't have to be rapaired and the have more slots for things you find.  The review then? Here it is...  I love playing Battlefield 4 with keyboard+mouse. It's easy to understand, kill all enemies and capture the flag, and the matches are short. In Star Conflict you have the same game type - but in space and it is working great! But here you also have the Invasion mode MMO. Fly to sectors just to explore it, shoot some bad guys or find some valuable stuff - if you have more time over.  The controls works perfect (I use keyboard and mouse), the graphics are truly awesome, the balance is not 100% but the developers is trying hard with it and are releasing updates often.  The only really bad thing about this game is when starting a PvP game. Often it takes around a minute to get started but sometimes it can take several minutes. A tip here is to select 'Any region' instead of your region.  The game needs this time to find other gamers with your (ship)rank so the match will be fair. And because there is not so many players this takes time...  I'll do hope that more space-liking-gamers finds this game!  Oculus Rift I play this game using a Oculus Rift.   I can't in words explain how incredible fantastic the game is when using Oculus Rift.   When you are in a dogfight and the enemy fly by you, I'll guess you have some problem to follow him when using a monitor. In Oculus Rift you just turn your head and follow him and then turn your ship after him without loosing him from sight!  That's really neat...   And the world in Star Conflict is great and made in '3D'... It looks fantastic when you look at it on your monitor... Yes? ...but when you use the Oculus Rift... fantastic goes to fantastic X 10! It's hard to explain but you are truly in that world! You know when you play you see the world from behind your ship. In Oculus Rift you 'hang' just above your ship. You can look up, to the sides and when you look down you see the ship and the 3D-deep is awesome. Your ship is so close and far away you see the asteroids and even farther away you see a planet. Words can never explain that feeling - you have to try it to understand it!  So, if I rate Star Conflict a 5 of 5 when playing it with a normal monitor I need to raise that to a 10 of 5 if I play with Oculus Rift. It's still the same game - but with Oculus Rift you ARE IN THE GAME!

review_text: It stinks.

10 friggin' bucks!??!   Asking too much! So, I have to take points off for that. Other than that, I don't have a whole lot to gripe about. The game does what they want it to do, and it's pretty fun if you're good at this sort of thing. Unfortunately, I'm not that great at...this...sort of thing.    Revisiting...   I decided to revisit this after taking a break from it for, uh, forever. If you can't tell, I got a little bit frustrated with trying to play to a ridiculous standard of speed. I don't know what I expected, given the fact that the title does mention 10 seconds, which is exactly how much time you have to do anything before you just fail miserably. However, I can't fathom why this has such high markings from other people.    It works!   Yes, it does work. Immediately, this puts it in a class of its own amongst other similarly-rated Steam games that flat out don't work at all. If the difficulty setting weren't on, 'Screw you and your ability to enjoy yourself!' I might have played it more. It accomplishes making you think quickly to get all the kills you need, but falls short of being entirely fun due to this.    Graphics and music blahblahblah blah   Both are good and they do what they accomplish. The pseudo-oriental theme music and robot nazi ideas are cute, but it wears thin after about 30 seconds, which is exactly how long it will take you to play through 5 levels of the game. Seriously, once you get through 3 you run out of things to expect from it - so you really only need about a half hour of playtime to get everything out of it that you need.   A hesitant recommendation   Look, the game says what it is, and it works well at doing it. The graphics are cute, there's robot zombies, there's ninjas, you can figure it out. This isn't exactly a game that many people will put hours upon hours upon hours of gameplay into unless they have some sort of spectrum disorder. I will, however, give it a 7/10 for doing  EXACTLY  what it wants to do, and doing it as hard as it possibly can until any respective player wants to cap themselves in the eyeball.

review_text: i once had a poptart but it fell into a puddle and it got soggy...

Unfortunately, I simply cannot recommend this game, even if I enjoyed most of it. It's quite fun to play, looks very pretty, and all that, but like a hot, crappy boyfriend, it makes a bunch of big promises, and doesn't even try to keep them. Rage is one of those quintessential examples of games not living up their full potential. This game does an amzing job of making you want to explore every square inch of the world presented to you, and it is at this point you realize how little of it is actually available to you.  The world appears large, and dense with lore, but it is merely a facade to look nice in the background while you blast mutants. Every little piece of the world you can get get your hands on and immerse yourself in is quickly gobbled up, and you ask for more, always more. For a while, it gives you just enough meat even as you finish with the last morsel, but eventually the table is empty, and there is no more depth and immersion to be had. The world feels hollow now, the last taste of it bittersweet in your mouth.   Characters are all very well voiced, and many have some great charm to them, but they are all pretty inconsequential to the plot. There may as well not even be any other people as far as the story is concerned. There's not many named characters in the game, and I can't even remember any of their names, only that Ron Perlman voiced one early in the game, but that character is very quickly forgotten entirely.   The other thing that strikes me most about this game is just how poorly the main story developed; you're set up with an incredibly cool plot to unravel, and then it's pretty well forgotten about until the last 10 minutes when, apprently, the developers thought 'Wait, are we forgetting something big, important, and that's supposed to be the focus of the whole ♥♥♥♥ing game? OH ♥♥♥♥, WE FORGOT THE PLOT.'   This game has one of the WORST endings I have ever seen in a videogame in my entire life. You get sent into evil government comlplex, shoot some storm troopers push a button, and then pop a bottle of champagne as the end cutscene plays(for about 27 seconds, without even some captions to pat you on the back). Wait, what the ♥♥♥♥? No boss fight? Nothing? I didn't even use this ultimate gun thing ONCE and I just picked it up less than 20 minutes ago!   The game ends so abruptly I waited through the whole credits expecting a big 'haha, fooled you! here's the rest of the game.' but no, back to the main menu. Would you like to play again? How would you like to return the favor, Rage, because I just spent 18 hours jerking you off without anything resembling a climax.  6/10, and that's being pretty generous. Don't pay more than 10 bucks for it if you really must have it.

Game is absolutely fantastic.  As someone that was a HUGE fan of the first Borderlands, I was somewhat disappointed with Borderlands 2 and was happy to see the changes they made with the pre sequel that has redeemed me as a Borderlands fan.  Pros:  - Better loot drops than Borderlands 2 - The Grinder: let's you grind guns and equipment you don't want to get better gear - Increased mobility - The slam feature - Laser weapons and the new cryo element. Cryo is super useful and lasers are quite versatile. Some shoot out multiple projectiles, like a shotgun, and some do high damage and are super precise, like sniper rifles. - More expansive and interesting environments to explore and fight in - The humor. It's hard for me to find games genuinely funny but some parts of this game had me cracking up. - No more slag. Found that mechanic of slagging and switching weapons to be very tedious.  Cons:  - The constantly draining 02: it's not a deal breaker, like I thought it would be, as 02 is very easy to find, but it's still annoying and I feel like the game would be better off without that meter to keep an eye on. It's so trivial to the point that it just doesn't make sense to me why it's even still in there.  - Non-instanced loot: people just pick up anything nice, even if it's not a good gun for their character or build. Would be really nice if they could implement a 'Need or Greed' system or even a rating system to keep people honest.  Altogether, if you like loot-based games like Diablo, and you like first-person shooters, I can't see you not liking this game. Most of the issues I had with BL2, like terrible loot and constant knockdowns, seems to have been addressed with the pre-sequel. If you are really concerned about the oxygen mechanic, I really recommend looking past it and giving the game a try. It bothered me at first but then I realized it's hardly an issue whatsoever.   5 stars! Gearbox, you have done a great job with this game and I hope you continue on this path!

I'm going to give a thumbs up only because the game shows potential and what future turn based tactical shooters 'could' be. To be honest though I'm quite disappointed.  I hope full control can keep working on it and keep enough momentum going to make it better but we will see, time will tell. At this stage personally I consider JA Back In Action to be a far superior JA game, especially with the rebalancing mod which has taken it to the next level.  The game has several very annoying flaws, the most annoying for me right now is the camera rotation. It needs to be freely rotatable. There is nothing more annoying than having to spend time constantly mucking around with the camera to be able to see whats happening behind buildings and trees. There are also major issues with the interrupt system, cover system and LOS. The AI could do with a lot more work as well.  Put these together with a few seemingly easy to fix interface and feedback issues and you have a game that is unpolished and difficult to become immersed in.  I just started a new campaign on JA:BIA with the rebalancing mod. Oh where do the hours go? JA:BIA 10/10

review_text: 6/10, Ether Vapor is a pretty good little SHMUP that I still consider to be very enjoyable despite its problems.  GOOD  The weapons are all basic, yet very useful. Each weapon also has a charge part to it. Making the weapon system a bit like a combination of R-type and Radiant Silvergun. The unlockable ships all have variations on the same weapon types but still manage to be useful. Movement feels great and bullets are abundant enough that you feel there's enough going on without being too overwelmed. In this regard the game isn't really considered a Bullet hell.  BAD  Unfortunately there are quite a few problems with the game as a whole. The scoring system is something that I think could have used some more work to make it more interesting for Super plays. And another problem is the graphics... No. The quality isn't bad. The issue stems from identifying bullets admist the chaos and knowing what can and can't hurt you. In most SHMUPs enemy bullets are on the highest layer and are typically a bright colour that you can identify easier. Some bullets that aren't of the pink ball variety tends to blend in with the explosions and your own bullets. Leading to moments where you question what just hit you. The story is there but nothing too special. I watched the cutscenes once then turned them off personally.

review_text: Recently finished, so here's my 2 cents:  Girlfriend Rescue is your standard issue RPG Maker game, both developed and published by Aldorlea Games (notorious developer of many RPG Maker games). Its a JRPG that handles similar to old school Dragon Warrior or Final Fantasy, but with a bit more room for strategy in both building your party and combat. The story itself takes place in a modern, urban setting which is a breath of fresh air (ironically) as opposed to your typical medieval fantasies. There's an interesting variety of characters to construct your party with, to which you will use to trudge through the 8 different scenarios. Explore a bit, loot things, fight baddies, and continue onwards. At the end of each scenario you get a little piece of story that serves as a reminder in case you forgot what the game was all about (see: title), and then you go back to walking and mashing the X button.  Its not the most fun I've had with one of these kinds of games, in fact I'm a bit disappointed in Aldorlea Games. After playing Deadly Sin 2 and Skyborn, I really expected more from this title. I only recommend it if you have absolutely no other RPGs to play.. or just really need that old school RPG fix, even then wait for a sale.  Pros:  + Interesting variety of characters + Fresh, urban setting + Fast paced combat + Colorful visuals  Cons:  - Mediocre story - Poorly written dialogue - Repetitive battles - Ugly enemy artwork - Vague character/skill descriptions - Messed up controls with my X360 gamepad - Lack of settings (volume, controls, etc)  5/10

review_text: TL;DR: For grinders and people who want to keep their hands busy.  Rogue Legacy is a grindy game. I have played a touch on PC and quite a bit on Vita and it has proven to be a slog in a lot of ways.   This is a game that has good ideas but at every turn it asks you to invest time over and over to get over specific walls. Damage is heavily scaled so later areas are disproportionately hard for early runs which means that you have to die and restart just to get the stats to make your attempts easier. Bosses favor specific character traits and classes which means when you finally start running bosses, you can sometimes get stuck dying repeatedly till you pick up a better character to make things work.   It also lacks meaningful variation to break up this monotony. Tile sets can get rote and enemies don't have interesting attack patterns to master. Change in locals also brings reskinned enemies most of the time with some exceptions.  I can recommend Rogue Legacy but only to a specific crowd, those who appreciate a long grind or need a brainless game. Other than that you may want to steer clear.

Where do I start? Well, if I'm gonna be honest, I'll start right where many people start.  SUPERHOT is quite the shooter, and it certainly likes to hang up the banner of 'most innovative', which at times, I'm inclined to agree with. Many times. The game really is fun all around - if you can get past some frustrations that may occur during some levels. But when you do pull off a really cool one? A really awesome one? Let me tell you just like everybody else.  It's. So. Dang. Satisfying.   To the point where, when given the option to save a replay, you don't mind sitting around for a few minutes waiting for it to finish uploading, because you can watch that badass replay over and over and over again and still foam at the mouth at how awesome it was. Even if you got lucky. Even if you weren't even trying. The experience was still well worth it.  The price tag on the other hand? Not so satisfying and well worth it.   And here we get to the part of the review where many, many others get to. Which is totally understandable. Because it's so easily agreeable.   The simple fact is: SUPERHOT is not worth the money. It's not worth the standard $25 price tag.   Do not be deceived by the endless mode layered with multiple challenges. I personally haven't played that much of it, but from I have played, I can tell you it is quite fun - for a while. Eventually, you just get bored, as with every game. And quite frankly, the repetitious gameplay of SUPERHOT, even with a good amount of variation here and there, does start to inadvertantly pester you. In some cases, you just feel that it's getting really old, really fast.   The story, particularly, and I'm afraid to say, ONLY in your first playthrough is enough to keep you intrigued with a drive to keep going and see it through. Unfortunately, it's just too short. And that's not entirely a fault of the devs, the main point of this game is to experience the new innovative game mechanic. The point is to experience time only moving when you move. The point is to make yourself feel like a professional badass action hero. At least when you pull it off. No joke, I beat the main game in 2 hours. And do not mistake me, that's with plenty of restarting levels, plenty of endless but vain attempts at being cocky, and plenty of anger induced rampages, usually unseccesful. That's on me though.   So, while it is disappointing that the story didn't have any great length, it was never the reason to play the game in the first place. Still though, if it was expanded upon, that'd be quite welcome, and pretty interesting.   Overall, I DO recommend SUPERHOT. But not for a price of $25. It simply isn't worth that. The cost for this game really does come at a cost, pun intended, for a player's experience. All of your cool experiences and momentes of undeniable badass-ery, are entirely undermined with the knowledge of the outrageous price tag.   So, do yourself a favor, and buy this game! Buy SUPERHOT! SUPER HOT SUPER HOT SUPER HOT ...............................  ....................... Just make sure you buy it on sale at a reasonable price. Don't spend anything over $20 if you can manage.

review_text: Early Access Review

This is a Neutral review, but due to Steam's 'Love It Or Hate It' voting system, I'm selecting against it due to how the end game works.  The game itself has four acts, with each act consisting of a major plot arc as the main character (you) explores the land to unite with survivors and repel an otherworldly invasion. I mostly enjoyed exploring every nook and cranny during the first act. Then that feeling died quick as the game placed valuable upgrades throughout the area, making the exploring portion mandatory to stand a chance at later difficulties. I spent 40 hours with my latest Hardcore character (not dead as of this review), and clocked 27 hours of those 40 exploring the map for shiny shrines and attributes gained through side quests. It was a struggle to beat the game the first time by act 3 and took several breaks to get the urge to finish the rest of the game. Overall, the 'Normal' difficulty version of the game is worth checking out.  My main gripe with the game is it relies on difficulty being resistance based. In the game, there are 8 major resistances and 3 other important damage types not covered by the major 8. If a player is able to get a resistance stat above 50%, the game allows for a struggle in the fight, where that player can take a hit while delivering damage and not instantly run at the sight of taking damage. Coupled that by having only 1 healing potion to use at a time, a person playing Hardcore has to grind previous difficulties to get their resistances up or they will die, no exceptions. It made it very boring because I couldn't progress the story for the later difficulties at all, at least safely.  The game further chooses to disrespect the player's time by not providing a reliable means to improve the most common 4 types of damage: Vitality, Aetherial, Chton, and Poison. Elemental Resistance is plenty in the game, so much so, it almost shouldn't exist where I got a shoulder plate to drop that gave +30% to all elements on top of the 40% element resist from Shrines. For those four I mentioned, there are almost no significant ways to improve them through Components (items you socket into items) and Augments (items you earn through factions that apply on top of the components), meaning you need to get lucky and hope an item drops with what you need.  Damage bonuses are flat multipliers, so damage quickly becomes fixed if one doesn't use multipliers through curses or items that decrease resistances or defenses. The game doesn't support glass cannons properly because of that and teaches over time to just focus on defense because your damage will be good enough for almost all the fights. Only when my resistances weren't high against the damage type I was facing was I concerned about needing to do damage in a quick manner.  I could probably write about 3 pages worth of complaints in design decisions with information to prove those points, but to save your time I recommend playing the game through Normal difficulty and stopping there, even though the game gimps most characters through their Shrine system having the rest of the Devotion points be available in later difficulties. It'll be a massive time sink gearing up a character to handle the later difficulties with no progression in completing those higher challenges.

review_text: An unusual 'Conquer everyone' sort of game, where you are the last survivor of your civilization, all by yourself - and thus unable to conquer anyone! Instead, you are trying to convince every other civilization to form together into one Federation. Unfortunately, not everyone can get along by themselves, so it is up to you to manipulate things behind the scenes.  Beg, bribe, or threaten races to act more friendly to each other.  Rival empires forming a hostile political pact? A little sabotage, some planted false evidence, and a couple of false flag operations can drive a wedge between enemies, leaving them open to manipulation by you.  Finally, for those utter recalcitrant races, you can just arrange to have them killed off.  The game is simple in concept (make everyone friendly with each other) but deep in possibility, as you have many options to accomplish your goals. The learning curve is somewhat steep, but there is a lively and friendly community able to help. Overall, an unusual game with an enjoyable premise and interesting mechanics. Do not expect a Paradox-style Grand Strategy game, and you'll be fine.

review_text: In short, meh.  With such fond memories of the first game, I honestly feel as though no sequel yet has matched it, problems;  The music, Alex Brandon did an amazing job on the soundtrack of the original which really added to the depth and immersion, since then the music has become dull, repetitive and very lacklustre.   Linearity, now. The first game was linear, there were a few sidequests, but you knew what the game was about, and why you were doing what you were doing. There is no sense of urgency when you can stop saving the world for a minute to save a damsel in distress.   Lack of locations, it took me a minute in Prague to realise that there are only 2 parts, not 4... I just completed the game, and it's only in the last minute it feels like Deus Ex.  Progression, remember that first robot at the statue of liberty, remember so many more at versalife, and how they were difficult to overcome... Hell, remember actually travelling to different places achieving things, seeking alternative routes to not raise alarms, or literally just bashing the same part of the game over and over to not die. Remember slowly unraveling a story, choosing allegiances, even Human Revolution had bosses, I spent the whole game gearing up for what?  I'm annoyed at myself for spamming everything right from the very start, I had raided the bank before the bank mission, why was I allowed to, almost every aspect of the story I'd already completed before it became relevant...  Where is the futurism, where are the riots, where is the action, why so many short stories and no real big one?  The game is VERY SHORT, I know the series is about exploration, reading about the story etc, but the actual gameplay was just samey and boring, i feel as though if I rush played it I could get it complete in an evening and still get a good nights sleep.  I personally feel like a naughty private detective robocop playing this game, fingers crossed (I'm at mission 14) it gets better. Hard to believe that what, 16 years after, and with god knows how many multiples of the budget and additional cast, a sequel can disappoint so much... Sorry to moan but... Wah!

The game also feels very dated, which for me is a good thing however for more newer people to the genre or who have played more recent MMO’s you may be heavily put off by it. review_text: Don't take my thumbs-up to this game as a go-for-it in regards to making a purchase. Read my review before considering! Remember to make informed decisions when it comes to buying video games!  First of all, I don't think the 'this is not a game, because it has no obstacles or objectives' notion is true. The game requires you to weave your way through a maze-like terrain, which in and of it self is an obstacle requiring the application of perceptive skills. Albeit, that is a rather meek gameplay mechanic to base a game upon. That is, however, not what Dear Esther strives to achieve - but from my point of view, I'd say this is indeed a game.  As for the story - I can not say, I am thoroughly impressed. Even with the symbolic and analogous meaning the narrative has, it did somewhat elude me as a result of lack of interest. It just didn't compell me. Be that as it may, the narrators british voice is a soothing background layer to the lone, cumbersome and slightly idyllic setting and ambience.  Speaking of the setting, I am reasonably impressed with the world in which the story takes place. Every step of the way is carefully sculptured and ornamented with both natural adornments or very mystic decals. All of this with immersive efficacy. The graphics aren't exactly cutting-edge but offers adequate possibility for immersion.  The game doesn't run too smoothly, but it runs well at lower settings. The character does not at all move at the pace you might wish. Even though this is probably intentional to make room for walls of narrated text, it does sometime mean that you will be walking in circles, looking for the next place to go at tortoise-pace. I never thought I'd say this, but for a game, which relies almost entirely on the narrative, Dear Esther doesn't have quite the amount of handholding, I felt necessary for the game to not become a drudge trudge.  Personally, I did somewhat enjoy the game - however short it might have been. At the current 7.99€ pricetag I am having a hard time recommending the game. Unless you are a fan of the purely explorative and immersive genres, I would not pick up this game unless it's on sale. If you are, however heavily into narration and immersion based games, Dear Esther is a possible hit.

review_text: Early Access Review

So after starting this game in 2012 I finally came around to finishing it in 2016... This is because playing a big open world game like this feels like a rather big undertaking for me. Not that the games are bad, just that they demand alot of time and I rather not get into it unless I feel I have that time without feeling stressed about it. Once I started up again I played it almost every day until I finished though.  I'm gonna review this in the aspect of it being the last part of the Ezio Trilogy. I was delighted to spend more time with Ezio Auditore, and get to some sort of conclusion with him and Desmond.  Compaired to the other two, I have to say this is my least favourite though. The settings are not as varied or beautiful as in AC II, nor are the missions. Constantinople is the only city you see (and a rather bland one at that) apart from a dreary underground cave town, and a bit of the boring Masyaf by the end of the game. I miss the horse riding from previous installments (though I seem to be in the minority there), colors, the warmth of characters like Leonardo Da Vinci, & more humour. There are no set pieces that stand out like before, apart from SPOILER! running along burning ships in the harbour. SPOILER END! I don't mind playing an older Ezio, that suits the story progression fine, and he is a wonderful man when he is more wheathered & experienced. But I did not feel as emotionally involved as I did in II or even Brotherhood. The supporting voice cast are also less stellar than in past efforts, with exception of John De Lancie.  In II, we got to experience Ezio grow from a young scoundrel to a avenger of his family, and to a defender of the people and freedom in general. The story moved at a steady pace, and I shed a tear over the tragedies in his life, as well as stood in awe over the beauty of the scenery at times, accompanied by the exquisite music by Jesper Kyd. It was close to a perfect game in my opinion.  So it was a tough act to follow, naturally. Brotherhood did well though, almost as well.  All 3 are well worth your time and effort, but as they progressed they became less effective in their storytelling, and bugs and glitches increased as Ubisoft released them with what felt like increasing haste. Sure, one should ride on popularity and try and respond to fans and consumers thirst for more, but not at the expense of quality control.  I suspect also that the writers didn't know how to end Desmonds, Ezios or Altairs stories, and just made it up as they went along, which is an oportunity lost. For me the ending of the trilogy left me wanting, but not for more or for clearer answers, but rather to replay AC II, to remember the series when it was at it's best.

review_text: Early Access Review

This game has gone a long way. Totally awesome, but with a flaw. See cons. Pros- Modding Support: Not necessairly mods, but specific server only mods/tweaks that you can install on your own dedicated server. Also, you can mod your characters. Want to run around shooting zeds with a character from the other game/anime/movie? sure! Weapon skins and other things are also supported. Don't like the look of a certain weapon? Change it to a more detailed one or completely change it! Not survival centered/Arcade style:* Abandon your common senses! Shoot zeds in the head! This game is completely not a survival game(you can still die though), which means that you can have hours of fun without having to worry about your character dying because of dehydration or starvarion. Classes: Keeps you playing the game for months(unless you farm)! You can choose the role of your character. Want to go insane and blow everything you find? Sure! Play demolitions! Can't fight properly but still want to contribute by healing? Play medic! Want to go insane and decapitate every zed you find with an AXE? Play berserker! Classes are capped at lv.6, but it's hard to level. This will keep you playing for ages. Classes can be changed at the start of a game and between waves. Weapons: Weapons are unique to each classes, but you can still use weapons from other classes? Play demo but still want to carry a dwarf axe just for fun? You can buy! You can find some weapons on the floor too! Levels: There are lot's of prepackaged levels, but if you want more, you can download some from the workshop! Cons: Grindy: This game can be grindy and stressful if you want quick levels, which you can still play casually and earn levels later.   *not to bash other survival games. some people might think this is a con. It's totally up to you.

This is a good game, hands down. I’m just going to list a couple of issues I find disturbing.  1. UPlay. Ubisoft’s habit of integrating all of its newest games with its online gaming hub. Did I mention that Left Ctrl is for going prone and C is for crouching? 3. Lack of customization. No really, for costume wise, you only get to change the headgear, and most of the other “options” are just palette swaps, with only the occasional distinct-looking headgear. As for weapons, the customizations are the same for similar weapon types across all operators. i.e. you have the “silenced” barrel, the “less recoil” barrel, no matter what they name them. 4. The players. This is actually more funny than an actual issue. You tell the team “I’m going to plant a breaching charge” and they run right into it when it blows up. You and your team back up at a corner during defense and you ask your teammate “have you reinforced the wall behind us?” and he says “I thought you did” before getting blown up by breaching charges. 5. The horrible network code. The matchmaking system isn’t too bad, but often you either get disconnected halfway through a game or see players getting disconnected if you’re host. It happens so frequently that any more often this game would be a disconnection simulator.  This recommendation is with hopes that the game improves on the issues, then it’s worth getting at a sale price. Let’s see how it goes.

review_text: School of Dragons, at first, seemed like a promising game. And being a fan of MMORPG games and the how to train your dragon franchise made this particular game stand out to me personally. However, it could be so much better. On a whole it is NOT a BAD GAME. It really needs a good few changes.  Once a character is created, it cant be deleated without having a certain amount of gems. And gems, from what i can see, cost ACTUAL money. This also goes back to the character creation. Its lazy, sloppily put together and it lacks the actual CREATE YOUR OWN CHARACTER element. Your character is either Hiccup or Astrid, with different sleeves, shoes, and hair.   The loading times are decent, but theres too many loading screens. You can pass into one area after seing a loading screen, then within seconds be taken to another.   almost everything costs gems, meaning if you want more of a personalised character, you need to feed the developers money. Dragons cost money, as do masks, shoes, food. All the basic things require money. Fair enough, the developers need money so they can continue to keep up the servers, update the game (this game needs some serious updates honestly) but that doesnt mean they should make EVERYTHING cost actual money.  Character animations could be a little bit better, as could the dragons. Their flying animations are decent, but on the ground it gets a little hectic. Their flying speeds could be faster, or slower depending on the type of dragon. i.e having a night fury faster than a gronckle.  'MMORPG based on DreamWorks Animation’s How to Train Your Dragon franchise. Players raise and train their own dragon, interact with characters from the HTTYD franchise, join clans and embark upon journeys and quests alongside their Viking pals in-game.' is a promising description, and these things are done within the game. Just not well.   updates are needed, patches too. But on a whole this is not the worst game ive played. And with a few changes, it could easily become much better.   Im torn between recommending it and not. I recommend it if you are not a big a fan of the franchise than most, as you could possibly be disappointed if you have your heart set on an 'epic httyd experience'

review_text: Not enough aeiou

Been playing civ since the second one. This is the most complex civ to date as far as micromanaging all of your bonuses/tiles/production choices, and that's compared to even Alpha Centauri. If you like micromanagement, you'll love it (I mostly do). If you don't, you're going to probably feel like a previous title was a better game for you.  The AI is pretty damn terrible. It's probably not hard to tweak and be significantly improved, but as of this writing, a couple of months after release, I still see the AI do incredibly silly things such as city states surrounded by 25 warriors (clogging up all traffic through the area, maybe that's the point?) when everyone else is sporting swordsmen. Positive diplomacy with the other AI civilizations is a chore. Yes, you can be friendly with most civs, but you've got to take care of them and their interests like they're some kind of super-needy girlfriend with deep daddy issues you're afraid to dump because you know they'll go total psycho on you and your stuff.  Needless to say, it's all pretty much forced my hand into being a heartless warmonger every single game. Even when you know another civ's pet peeves, oftentimes there's just nothing you can do to avoid ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ them off to the point of them completely loathing you, because half of the time the thing they like is 'the human player not winning'. Well ♥♥♥♥ you, too, AI civilization.  Back to the AI being actually procedurally terrible: I know decent AI for a game like this is an _immense_ undertaking, but that's what triple-A game studios are for. When you sell tons of copies for a game at 60$ a pop, you're expected to be able to hire that extra person to make sure your AI doesn't have to be (extremely) cheaty to overcome its own weaknesses (which are many). The AI literally does not partake in the entire aspect of gold in this game. They have an infinite, or what may as well be, amount of income, which you can prove by trading with them. Seriously. Google it.  But let's talk about some clearly good things:  The tech tree being split into tech/civics, the religion system (after the last update to lessen religious unit walls), the archaeology, the great works, all of that is on the whole a positive shift. The creation of armies/armadas out of multiple units, I haven't used much (games I play usually end right at the end of the industrial era). The new city state system I am pretty sure I adore.  The first week of release I put in 8+ hours of multiplayer with someone in the same room and there were zero hiccups or problems. Like Civ V and the rest before it, this game is best played with some humans, and then it shines.  All in all, strong entry in the series. I haven't yet figured out whether I merely like this game or love it, but it's one of the two.   Last note: the two recent DLCs are an overt cash grab by the publisher. I am very disappointed by that. If it continues, this review will be changed to a downvote.

review_text: Early Access Review
Early Access Review

'Party Hard' is an ultra violent murder simulator set to delightfully upbeat techno dance music. It's 'Hotline Miami' with a goofy detective noir storyline. The goal is straightforward and the retro looking pixelation fits. I can't complain about the difficulty because it scratches the keep-banging-one's-head-against-the-wall-futility itch that makes 'Hotline Miami' so endearing.   My biggest complaint is not one of story but of game coding: the pathing mechanics for the computer. Your job is to murder the partygoers; patience and luck is required. I get that. The perception ability of guests (and especially police officers) is almost psychic at times, declaring my guilt to a murder that they were not witness to nor am I nearby as a suspect. Paired with the ultra linear level designs, the computer has a tendancy to wander into the same traps. Picking a spot near a body disposal area and killing the guests one by one isn't a challenge, it's a fetch quest. If the designers are willing to open levels up a bit (preferably by adding doors or removing obstacles), I will consider changing my views. Until then, prepare for a great deal of frustration above and beyond what is expected for the game.   Also, in a game about creative murder, why is the environment only selectably interactive? 'Dead Rising' took the philosophy that everything is a weapon. Knock things off the wall, pick up small furniture, and especially allow more randomness into the available room options. With a limited supply of traps, the game feels like it's telling you to only have fun in a pre-approved way. I don't think that was the intent, but it came out as such.   The game isn't bad by any stretch of the imagination. I simply can't give it a thumbs up at the current price point. Considering this developer gave the world 'No Time to Explain', I'm disappointed. Pick it up on a deep sale price.

There are several designs of building and are always found in one of a handful of combinations. There are always the same things to interact with inside those buildings. Ship designs are sometimes quite cool but their function is always the same barring upgrades. Of course that's the whole point of upgrades but I was expecting actual classes of ships. I think that would have added much more depth.  I get that this is a survival and exploration game, so it is expected that you would have to repeat certain activities to ensure your survival. But I just wasn't expecting it to get boring so fast. This is just my view. I am happy that some people are enjoying it and I am sad that others (myself included) are not. I would rate the game 3/5 and sadly, I personally would not (currently) recommend this game.  Perhaps in time with updates, this game will improve. And I will gladly change my review if my opinion of the game changes. I will keep checking back to see what updates they have done and fingers crossed, things get better.
