review_text: Early Access Review

If nothing else, please read my TL:DR at the end of this review.  I first played this game in July of 2013, not long after it was released. I was initially turned off due its free-to-play model and gameplay that I found... lacking. I was a huge fan of the X-Men Legends/Marvel Ultimate Alliance franchises which took a page from from the Diablo series gameplay and mixed in the Marvel universe. I had hoped that Marvel Heroes could be what I wanted in a successor to Ultimate Alliance but it felt quite unpolished in terms of gameplay and its business model. It also didn't help that Hawkeye was my initial choice who wasn't the best designed heroe at that time (and still unfortunately has some conflicting reviews).  During the recent Steam Winter Sale, I saw some pretty good deals on the game and also saw that the developer had some decent in-game deals as well. I decided to give it another chance and purchased Nightcrawler. After a few hours of playing him (quite a bit in Midtown Madness) I realized that a lot of time and effort had been taken to improve the game that I had once written off as another free-to-play cash grab based on a franchise I liked (looking at you Avengers Alliance). A lot of content and characters had been added and it really started to feel like the type of game I was hoping to find when I first tried it out.  It still has its imperfections, but the developers are quite publicly candid about how they are working to improve the game. They have (rightfully) made a big deal about their level 52 review process of reviewing/changing characters to better fit with the changes in the game and take into account player feedback. The have greatly improved on the initial free-to-play model with the usage of eternity splinters as a way to buy characters. On the 3rd login day, you receive enough to purchase another character (and have some left over if you choose a random drop). Some it may seem tedious at first but now it really only pay-walls people with regards to inventory stashes. That is a practice I can't defend and wish they would provide some sort of in-game collectible currency to purchase or at least give you a character stash upon beating all three difficulties with a particular character (or some other similar feat).  TL;DR: This game had a very, very rough start in 2013 and I initially uninstalled it in disappointment. I gave it a second chance last month and I've been quite surprised at how much it has improved and become the Marvel themed action RPG I hoped it would be. Still some room for improvement, but the developers' apparent commitment to interacting with the player community and care for creating a better game gives me a lot of hope for the future of this game. If you like Diablo-esque action RPGs and Marvel characters, it's definitely a game worth checking out.

He who stands with me shall be my brother.  Ok. So all glory for the Emperor aside, and all dark secrets of the 1st overlooked, this game is incredibly busted.  I love 40k, I adore terminators and I am not regretting my purchase in the slightest. There are some days when you get back from work or have a day off and just want to smash some nids with an assault cannon. This is the game for that. Rather, this could be the PERFECT game for that.  If it weren't for a few major errors. Particularly regarding the multiplayer (single player campaign is fun, immersive but does not warrant $45AUD itself).  We want to discover the secrets of the First and Sar Luther with our battle-brothers. Which is made very difficult through the myriad of following issues;  - You never know how much damage you take. Yes your armour glows varying colours when you've been hit. But your right arm could be red, everything else fine, a genestealer jumps you from behind and suddenly you're dead. This comes down to a poor depiction of how healthy you are. Running co-op missions with friends is a constant stream of 'you're on half health, watch out for that stalker....oh'.  - There are no checkpoints in multi. You can get to the absolute end of the mission, not see a mob of stalkers (easy to do when it's just 2 of you playing on hard), become another skull for the skull throne and wind up listening to Belial talk for another unskippable minute before repeating the entire mission from the start.  - There are two game modes - normal rules and codex rules. Normal rules allow inventory changes in the mission by pressing 'I', useful for Assault and Gunner classes. Just make sure that the host doesn't check their inventory because then the entire game crashes and boots the rest of the squad. No saves remember. Back to Belial.  - Additionally, normal mode insinuates you can respawn and progress through levels and get new equipment. Your healer dies to a broodlord, you get swamped.  Two options appear; 'Fix It' (believe me we'd love to) or 'Continue'  'Click fix it,' my mate says to me.  We click it. Yup, no respawn. Back to the start and back to the same spiel from Belial (unskippable remember).  Another broodlord....  'Click continue this time.'  No respawn...back to Belial.  We get our sh** sorted, get our classes right and jump into another boarding action. We only play as a 2-man (the cruel reality of 40k fans :P). Oh hey the difficulty doesn't scale. The struggle of a broodlord is hectic with a 2-man.  We beat it. We actually beat it.  'Oooh, awesome I got a level 5 ability I didn't know i could.'  Yup, presses 'I' to check the ability. Game crashes, we lose ALL progression.  So yes. Terminators are great. Belial's voice is stuck in my head. The environments are amazing and Tyranids are actually the least of your concerns when it comes to bugs.  When they fix it, if the fix it, definitely buy the game. Until then, steer clear and just play Dawn of War for a little bit longer, or do ANOTHER run through of Space Marine.  Hope is the first step on the road to disappointment.

review_text: Okay really, if you don't buy this now I will end you.

This game is far from perfect, but since it's free I'd recommend it to people who have about an hour to kill. BUT. Just in case the name and the description aren't clear enough, I'll say it here: THE CONTENT OF THE GAME MIGHT BE TRIGGERING TO PEOPLE WITH DEPRESSION. Also, I STRONGLY disagree with the reviewers who said that the protagonist is just 'sad' and not 'really depressed'. There isn't just ONE valid way to experience depression and it's really crappy to invalidate other people's experiences just because they don't match your own.  So, anyway, it took me a while to get into it, but once my choices actually started mattering I found the game to be enjoyable. I also liked the way that the static on the pictures changed depending on your current status; it's a minor detail but something that I think really added to the mood. The number of choices you could make were also affected by your status, which I thought was a nice gameplay touch even though I was super frustrated that it kept me from doing some of the things I wanted to do (which, of course, is the point of the game mechanic). While I would have liked more choices that were ambiguous (ie. not clear which the 'good' one was), I did like that I was able to make similar choices in-game as I have done with my own life (and that the outcomes mirrored what happened in my situation as well). For me, playing through the game helped remind me of the struggles I've gone through, what I've overcome, and that it's okay that I still struggle with things.  That said, engine wise, I've played better Twine games. I am not sure if the devs were using an old version of Twine or what, but I didn't like how the three statuses (depression, therapy, and medication) were at the bottom of the page since that meant I couldn't just look at them whenever I wanted. I also found the writing to be clunky. In particular there was too much 'telling' and not enough 'showing', and also in the beginning your 'girlfriend' was randomly written as 'significant other' and in the middle of the sentence the pronouns would switch from being 'she/her' to 'they/them'. I am pretty sure that it was just something missed in the editing process, but it did highlight for me that the game failed to ask me what my SO's gender and preferred pronouns were, which is something that all Twine games I've played have done.  Anyway, this game is not going to be everyone's cup of tea. And I definitely wouldn't recommend it for someone who thinks they might be triggered by playing as someone who's depressed. But it's free and only 30 mins to an hour of gameplay for one playthrough, so if you're bored and in the right frame of mind why NOT play it?

review_text: I reluctantly have to give the ol thumbs down to this game. I was really looking forward to it and was completely ready to be wowed by the game play but unfortunately the cons outweigh the pros.   Firstly the devs got a lot of things right with the game. Players can absolutely play the game the way they want to. Whether it be stealth and non-lethal (like myself) or as a guns blazing tank the game provides plenty of lee way. Stealth is challenging at times but then it should be. The few times I went head to head with a group of bad guys I found that even with my stealth build I had plenty of options to do a beat down. The graphics, on the whole, are outstanding. When I made it to London I spent a good while just standing on top of the building staring out at the city. Prague is well detailed and the dirt and grime of the fallen city are well captured as is the dispair of its inhabitants.  To me the Deus Ex series is about two things: story and customizing your character and while both are there in spades it is also where the game actually fails for me.   The story is typical for the series, a number of shadowy (and not so shadowy) organizations are manipulating world events and Jensen has to figure out what the Hell is going on and try to make the least wrong decisions. And this is fine, its what I signed up for. But the Jensen character is so badly delivered and wooden that it was off putting. The character barely moved during conversations and cinematics and the face had virtually no emotion. This is doubly disturbing as I had the opportunity to meet Elias Toufexis, the actor who does both the voice and mo cap for Jensen, at a convention. He was very excited about the game and about what he tried to do with the character. He wanted badly for Jensen to show more emotion and he said that he asked the devs to not have the dark lenses deployed as much so that his emoting would show thru more. I was unimpressed with facial animation. L.A. Noire did a better job of it and it is a number of years old. Apparently, Toufexis was expecting more also, per his comment about wanting to see more of Jensen's eyes. But I don't think it would have made any difference if we had seen more of his eyes as the facial animation was so wooden (particularly for the main character) that it didn't matter. The several other characters were better captured than Jensen and that is unforgiveable.   Lastly, the upgrades...the Praxis kits... or Praxis points... or whatever. Praxis kits themselves are very rare in the game and for good reason. But you don't really need them as you get kits/points via an experience point system. You are told by one character that you will have to relearn to use your augs and I suppose the XP reflects that but it doesn't seem to make sense within the game setting. Further more, Praxis kits (as well as credits) are purchaseable via microtransaction. While Deus Ex is a solo game and people can spend their money however they want having microtransaction on a AAA $60 game is pushing the limits. Praxis points are also available by playing Deus Ex Go, which is a fine game unto itself and well worth the $5.   My advise? Wait for a sale to play the game. Deus Ex is a story driven game with a protagonist that (this time) I had no connection with due to poor animation and an upgrade system that didn't know what it wanted to be. My negative review is meant as a message to the devs as much as anything. No matter how pretty and cool the game is, if it is story driven the main character had better rock. And this time Jensen didn't.

review_text: The very short and simple version is this: If you didn't like the previous Arkham games, you won't like this one.   That said, this game seems to have had a shoddy release on PC. Not that I'd know, I only recently upgraded to a point of being able to play the game. And 48 hours later after 100%ing the story (not newgame+ or DLC or whatever), I'd say this is a damn fine game. There's one specific factor that keeps it from surpassing Arkham City as my favourite Arkham game, but it is a very solid second. Performance-wise, I was expecting this to have some issues. I didn't expect that the game had been fixed entirely. I  might  be right with that. Three specific areas in the game caused the game to freeze and practically crash multiple times. Made the Riddler challenges within them annoying because they froze, they wouldn't have saved and I'd have to do them again. Made me paranoid about hunting them in big rooms. The areas were {SPOILERS}   Ace Chemicals building, Stagg Airships and the movie studios.   When I say 'might', I mean that I'm also running the Windows 10 Anniversary version, notorious for messing with a hell of a lot of programs, and I hear Arkham Knight has issues in this version of Windows 10. So whether it is to blame, or the game itself, I don't know. It seems strange that some sections of the game within enclosed buildings would crash instead of the big open world where everything is happening. Besides those, I had no issues with the game at all. No glitches, no defomities, no frame drops, no blurry textures... The weirdest thing I saw was probably just a physics issue in which Batman dropped from the sky and beat up a Riddler informant, all with his cape stuff over his head covering his face. Everything besides that seems to work as it should.  But onto gameplay. Loved it. I was practically prepared for some poor showings, like say any and all the sections with the Batmobil. I continuously see how that got a lot of complaints. But honestly, I thought those were fine. The controls were easy enough, they weren't terribly difficult or annoying (and I played the game on Hard first go), there's DEFINITELY been far worse driving controls/mechanics in AAA titles, and there's something I found enjoyable about driving in a sneaky way around the streets to take out the larger tank drones. I guess the only downside I have for it is just how many missions needed the Batmobil. Plenty of which just take the bad guy back to the GCPD. But besides that, I spent majority of the game just grappling and gliding around everywhere. That was the thing I originally saw with the game that got me hyped. I ran everything on the highest settings I could get it and it was smooth, 60+ frames at all times. It made Gotham look gorgeous to fly around in. All I wanted to do since that first trailer was just fly around for a while. I got plenty of that and not once did I get tired of it. Everything else with the gameplay is pretty much what you'd expect. More of the same, but improved in some areas. Still plenty of gadgets to use, more combo options, more takedown options. Plenty to learn, difficult to master.  The one thing I see as the annoying factor for people are the Riddler stuff to hunt around for. It's no better or worse in this game than the previous ones. If you liked it then, you'll like it now. Some are stupidly easy, some are hard enough that you'll look them up on how to do. The bottom line with it is I personally enjoy things that make me go out and explore this open world, particularly in places I probably wouldn't go otherwise. Really have me get familiar with all the layout. The payoff for finding them all is better than Asylum and City at least, so there is that.  The only thing that stops this game from being my favourite is the lack of boss fights.  Actual  boss fights. Remember Mr. Freeze in Arkham City? Or Deathstroke in Arkham Origins? None of those here. Some of them turn into almost standard Predator challenges with one of the bad guys in the mix as well, hardly doing anything to make them significant, or they'll just be there after beating down many of their thugs. Or be reduced to Batmobil tank battles. Either way, majority of the time it boiled down to 'Press Button to Win'. Two characters got a semi reasonable encounter, but they somewhat blended in with the multiple minions around them. They stood out and had special attacks needed for them, but they ultimately still flowed in the same combat and combos and such. Nothing felt terrifically epic as previous game encounters, sadly.  All in all, solid 8/10. I enjoyed it, will replay it several times. Hell, I still got New Game + and DLC stuff to sift through, but as of this, I thought the main game was fantastic.

I bought this game on sale for ~$25. I then bought both DLC for $30. I would buy it again for full price. Fantastic game. Would definetly recommend.  Pros: - Fantastic story - Great skill tree and skill customization - Absolutely beautiful graphics - Well optimized. I get about 60-80 fps on Ultra, 1080p, with an R9 390 - Good combat. HINT: DON'T lock on to enemies. It works well with 1 enemy, but with anything over 1 enemy, it completely screws things up. Good thing it isn't automatic, and not at all required. I never use it. - Can be quite difficult. Difficulty can be changed at any time. - SO MUCH CONTENT. I'm 120 hours in and still finding out new things about the game. A huge number of quest, treasure hunts, and witcher contracts. - SO many more. Too many to list.  Cons:  - Riding on Roach (your horse) is quite glitchy (at least on pc). On flat roads or plains it's fine, but if there are any obstacles, or tight turns, it doesn't work well. - A few graphical glitches, but nothing that affects gameplay, and usually they are very small, like misplaced objects, or clipping issues. - Loading times can be a little long at times, unless installed on an SSD, where loading times are better. Comparable to Skyrim, maybe a bit longer.  9.7/10. My new favorite game.  Edit: Nominated for the 'Game Inside A Game' award because Gwent is the bomb.gov

There are two major let downs in this game though.  1) The health regen system is based off of quick-time-event (QTE) kills that you can perform on stunned enemies. I swear this same story takes place in every Warhammer 40K video game. Every 'plot twist' can be seen coming a mile off and there are a few things left unresolved at the end of the game (possibly for a sequel).  Other than that, it's a fairly good game. Worth the time if you like Warhammer 40K or God of War style melee combat.

review_text: Alright, im gonna keep this as short and as informational as possible with not much experience with the game, so bear with me. First of all, what the hell is this game about? Well, basically, it's about this developer that needs to upload a game before his publishing licesce expires. However, as luck would have it, it fails epically. And his licesce expires tomorrow. But, lucky for him, there are five other developers in his apartment building, and they all have developed one game. His mission now is to source hack all of thier games and put something on the app store.  Alright, so what's the gameplay like? Well, the camera follows your character as if its on a wall. Its sorta hard to describe, but you'll know what i mean if you download it. Your character (his name is warren, so we'll use that from now on) travels on this grid, sorta like an old Pokemon game. You use either WASD or the arrow keys to control Warren, E to inspect, and R to interact.  Now for the main part of the game: the minigames. When Warren hacks a game, all you have to do is play a short minigame. I'm not gonna spoil anything, but the minigames are actually very short. The longest minigame is about 15-20 minutes, assuming it's your first time. That's really it. It is a very simplistic game; some would even call it humble.  This leads me to the next category: Graphics, Sound, Interface, and Stability (GSI&S) The graphics are , as i said before, simplistic. not much else to say. The sound also is simplistic, but it has a cartoonish sort of ring to it. The interface is... AGAIN, simplistic. It's simply drawn out, and the hud only appears when you can interact with an item (e.g, hacking a game, etc.). The game is extremely stable; i havent run into any problems yet, not even lag. Overall, it has a very simple feel to it. Games today (Especially freemium clash of clans ripoffs) try to overexaggerate, both in ads and in the game. I mean, seriously, when some chick with triple G's in metal lengere (nailed that spelling) pops on your screen and starts yakking about how you must RISE TO POWAR AND CONCAR YAR FOES FOR THA GLARY OF YAR KINDAM and all that shtuff, its... overwhelming. This game, however, dosen't shove triple G's in your face (execpt for one part but thats not the point). It offers what it has, nothing more and nothing less. But, im ranting too much. Lemme talk about the cons. What are the cons? Well, there arent many cons i can think of that shtick out. I mean, some of the dialouge needs some proofreading, but, aside from that and a couple other things, it dosent really have any cons. A simple game dosent have room for cons. Big ones, that is. So, ive listened to you up to here. What do you think? Well, if you have 35 minutes to spare, get it. It's a neat little game that has some humor sprinkled in there with simplicity for all. So, I say get it. Before you leave, i just wanna thank you for putting up with my B.S. for however many lines this is. I really hope this review helped you choose to download this game. Now, i hate begging like a betch, buut imma beg like a betch. If you though this was funny and/or helpful, please gimme a thumbs up and/or a smiley face. Thank you, and stay away from chicks with triple G's with metal lengere.

I don't know what Bloodbath Kavkaz is trying to be. It's definitely from Eastern Europe, all right. But I know that, gamewise, it's a tremendous ♥♥♥♥ing mess, and it's definitely not as good as the game it is trying to imitate. Is it trying to filter the experience of Hotline Miami through half-empty vodka bottles and angry screeds against Vladimir Putin? It certainly feels that way - the camera physically makes me want to throw up, and the design overall feels like trying to crash a party blasted out of my mind, not quite over the peak of drunkenness that seperates the great buzz from several alcoholic beverages from the regret of having several alcoholic beverages.  But here's the thing: Hotline Miami works because it is designed so ♥♥♥♥ing tightly that the experience is seamless, anarchic, and bloody, like getting into a fight in the mosh pit at a great concert from a punk band that knows their ♥♥♥♥ and capitulates on their effort and practice. By comparison, Bloodbath Kavkaz is a drunk ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who gets up on stage twenty minutes after the concert's over and tries to pull off that same lick on the lead guitarist's guitar, immediately realizing it's not on the same chord and it doesn't even know what strings to play in what order and fashion. And almost immediately, that drunk ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ is taken backstage by the punk band's roadies to be given constructive criticism via a swift beating.  If you're going to rip something off, rip it off well. Or at the very least, take careful, careful notes on the thing you're ripping off. Don't be the drunk ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who gets up on stage to play guitar.  (Side note: I was gifted this game through my friend randee newmen, who was really excited to show me this game knowing I'd love and then hate it. You ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.)

review_text: This game is well worth the price tag, as well as the deluxe edition. I look forward to the DLC that will improve this game. First I will start this review with the negatives  Cons  - AI turns are VERY slow. My first game was on a small map and as I got to late game, the AI turns last longer and longer getting up to 5 minutes per turn. (And yes, I have turned on the quick movement and combat. This is a HUGE annoyance as it prolongs the game to the point where you will waste hours.  I like to play huge maps with extra AI players to give me a crazy unpredictable game, but in my current playthrough of this, I am at the point where turns are getting over 5 minutes long, and im only in 1500AD. This makes the game unplayable in my opinion.   From my playthrough so far, This is really the only negative I have about this game. If they can patch this and speed up the AI turns, this game will be so great.    The Positives  + The new tile system and disctricts are a gamechanger, and for the better in my opinion. This system while wasy to implement, adds a layer of tactics and strategy that makes the game so much more intense. It also adds a ton of replay value. The geography you start on, truly dictates the path you will follow. Each playthough I open, I have to adapt a different playstyle.   + Combat has improved. Although the system hasnt changed much from Civ V, The addition of corps and army, as well as escorts have greatley imporved on an already good system.   +Diplomacy has slighlty improved, however I still find myself not getting along with anyone, and pretty much all the civs tend to hate each other. They also still are very stingy and dont like to trade on fair terms. This, I believe is a flaw from the system giving so many reasons for a civ to dislike you, that they will refuse to trade with you.  Overall, the game is so much more fun than previous Civ's, and really makes you think hard on higher difficulties. My first play on level 6 (Emperor), I slacked a bit on reaseach, as I did not have moutains on my island, and Germany got to the combustion engine by 1450 AD. I couldnt start any wars as I was not very advanced in tech.

review_text: Tears everywhere.
Early Access Review

First and foremost, because I would like to point this out first, because most of the people reading this probably will not want to read the whole massive explanation as to why I like this game. So to summarize, first, this game is an incredibly excellent game that suffers from some bugs and semi-regular crashes, but you can get unoffical patches and mods that help fix that a little bit. I gotta point out though, this game can potentially be balls-to-the-wall hard. So... Expect to play this game methodically, and not rushing through Call of Duty style hoping you won't get hosed down will bullets or surrounded by mutants and mauled to death. Because, spoiler alert, you will. And now, on to the big explanation!  S.T.A.L.K.E.R.: Shadow of Chernobyl is a game ahead of its time. Running off of a system where you need to conserve bullets, monitor your weapons' and armor's conditions, and be very conservative with materials. All of this is mostly thanks to the weight system. In most games that incorporate a weight system, like Elder Scrolls titles and Fallout games, ammunition (Usually) weighs nothing. Not in S.T.A.L.K.E.R., no, in S.T.A.L.K.E.R., bullets weigh a devent amount. So this brings an interesting system to the fore. In most games, I would have main firearms, one as my preferred gun, and the other as my backup, and load up on ammo for both of those. You can do that in S.T.A.L.K.E.R., but it's not optimal. Rather, you should probably just load up on ammo for your main weapons (Pistol and assault weapon), otherwise you'll be over-encumbered. S.T.A.L.K.E.R. also has a bleeding system. If you get shot in a non-vital spot, you bleed, and you should bandage up or you're gonna slowly bleed out. If it's a lesser wound, you don't need to worry about it, it won't bleed much. It's color coded, if it's red, you'll bleed out if you don't treat it, if it's green, it'll most likely heal in a moment or two. If it's yellow, it'll heal, it will just take a while, and you're probably better off bandaging it. This is the first game (Regarding it's time of release, in 2007) that I have seen a game make the bleeding system pertain to enemy AI as well. You hit and enemy with a few shots to the chest, he'll probably either die in a few moments, or curl up on the ground and eventually die like that. It's still very impressive. Everything about the weapons, armor, and gameplay, generally is extremely impressive. But that doesn't mean I don't have my gripes with the game. There are a few problems:  A: this game can be buggy as ♥♥♥♥. F6 oddly is the default quick save button. I set it to F5, becuase that's what it is in most games. That button is your friend. Use it a lot. B: I feel that a lot of the mutants take waaaaaaaay too mant rounds to put down. Unless you manage to hit them in the head, which is hard. Hell, even the blind dogs take like, at least a clip and a half from my Vintar BC to drop if I don't hit them in theh ead. There's a point where it starts to feel ridiculous, considering that this game pretty much forces you to ration off your ammo supplies. C: This game has an INSANE difficulty jump at the end of the game. It would be fine, if it didn't break all the rules that this game makes you obey. It forces you to move fast, and to act fast, while not dying. Up until that point, you essentially have to take everything slow and planned out. This ending just... It threw me off. ---- Quick save is your friend. Remember this. ----  Anyway, altogether, this game is awesome. There's a whole lot to do in it, between the warring factions, Duty and Freedom, and the side missions, there's a surprising lot to do. I would strongly suggest picking this game up if you haven't yet, and pick upt the STALKER: Complete mod for Shadow of Chernobyl. Makes it look a lot better, and tends to fix a lot of the issues the game has.

review_text: First off: If you are looking for actual gameplay, this is not for you. It's a walking simulator that has you piecing together a story from the clues around the house; clues, not puzzles. So don't buy this and then complain that 'it's not challenging'.  That said, I loved Gone Home. It was pretty short, even for a walking simulator, just shy of two hours, so just about the duration of a feature film. And frankly, that's a good thing. Much longer would have hurt the story and its impact. In fact, as it is, the truth was revealed the tiniest bit too early. Or maybe it's just that I write myself and know how these stories work. Maybe someone who doesn't know the mechanics of these stories would be more surprised than I was. That's not to say I wasn't moved by the story. It's short, but it feels incredibly real. The main plot is 'told' by Sam, the protagonist's little sister through clues and a diary, and the subplot can be found by looking at the little things Kaitlin's parents left behind. What all that amounts to is heartwarming and -breaking at the same time.  I'd recommend Gone Home to anyone who really just wants an interactive story in a sliiightly creepy setting (thunder and lightning and an empty house, brrr). If that's you, you should take a look, though maybe wait for another sale. Because while I enjoyed the game, I would've regretted paying 20€ for two hours. As it is, 8€ is what I'd pay for a ticket at the cinema, and that's a fair price for the same 'amount' of entertainment (and a better story than most films have at the moment).  (Also, I did actually play through the whole game, just in offline mode, so I let it run in online mode just long enough that it would let me write this review. Silly Steam.)

Blood Rayne : Betrayal is the latest installment in the BloodRayne Franchise.   Mechanically, it is a competant side scroller, with fairly responsive controls and a complex and satifisying moves list.  There are some hickups with the Dashes and the Backflips, which can be difficult to pulloff, but they are mostly manageable. Whilst it is true that the game demands a high skill cap, that is only if one wishes to get the perfect score on each level, and it ultimately comes down to simple pattern recognition. Other wise its not actually all that difficult, with the exception of some of the later levels.   The art direction and music is fairly well done; the soundtrack is fast paced with gothic and orchestral undertones, and the animation quality and level design is for the most part good.  Time wise the game should take about 7-9 hours to complete, depending on how often you die. There is a considerable amount of replayability though, as there is a level rating system and collectibles.  As a Bloodrayne game there are noticible differances from the previous entries; for starters the Rayne in Betrayal has a considerably different design; she looks younger, possesses much smaller armblades and does not wear her iconic black and red leather outfit, instead wearing a predominately black gothic-inspired outfit.  In addition, she is missing most of her powers and equipment - She no longer has her rage move, her harpoon, her carpathian dragons, nor her signature feeding method. However, despite this Rayne still feels like Rayne; though minimal, she does have her sense of wit, she is still a tough character, and she still fights with acrobat ferocity, just like in the previous games.   Story wise Betrayal has no relation to the previous games; Kagan is still alive, the Shroud never happened, there is no mention of the GGG. So if you were hoping to a resolution to BloodRayne 2's cliff hanger, you are not getting it. It does introduce a new character, however he's only really there to give Rayne a new gimmicky power.  For the most part Betrayal does feel like a Bloodrayne game; there's that familiar over the top level of violence and humor and the story is gloriously ludicrous.  Overall it is a well crafted 2d platformer and an adequate, albeit unorthodox entry into the BloodRayne series.  However, I still cannot draw myself to recommend this, as Rayne's original outfit, which was featured on the very cover art of the game, is not in the game itself. I found that to the extremely misleading, and a clear attempt to pander to the franchise's fanbase.

Ah, Wolfenstein 3d. Many, incudling myself, would say that this game helped establish the First Person Shooter genre. Why would we, in 2015 (as of writing), want to play a 23 year old relic of gaming past? Well, it's because despite its age, and its primitive appearance, everything that makes the genre what we know it to be today is here. After all, this is the blueprint to the FPS genre. The gameplay is here, and there's a certain freedom that this and many games like it have that more modern games seem to lack. Sure, as I said before, the graphics are dated and it looks like a mess someone drew in MS Paint compared to pretty much anything since then. Sure, the voice samples are goofy when guards see you and yell in broken German and let out death cries ranging from cringeworthy to gut bustingly hilarious, but that's part of its charm. Wolfenstein 3d is a game that doesn't take itself to seriously and doesn't expect you to either. So, as inexpensive as it is to have one of the most vital pieces of gaming history to enjoy for yourself, you would be kind of silly not to get it and try it at least once.

review_text: There seem to be a lot of pretentious reviews for this game on the internet, which really put me off. Despite my criticisms, the dev gave me a copy through twitter. Stripped of the pretty words others seem to lavish on it, it’s basically a platformy puzzler you play at your own pace which you’ll either hate, grow bored of quickly, or love. There is nothing innately pretentious about the game itself which makes me wonder why indie game reviewers try so hard.  In Starseed Pilgrim, you collect “seeds” to build block formations to explore your central hub and unlock other pilgrims. To collect seeds, you must venture into a place where the darkness of space is actively devouring everything. The darkness of space, if you jump into it, inverts the playing field, making those solid blocks into empty space and vice versa. You want to collect seeds and keys in order to get back home and use them. You can’t grow anything in the darkness of space so thought must be invested beforehand.  The game largely leaves you to your own devices, so you’re free to explore whatever you can reach. Different coloured seeds grow into different shapes and sometimes do special things, like provide seeds in the darkness of space or allow you to jump higher. Certain levels have rules. It’s up to you to figure out what does what but nothing is so obtuse that it’s impossible.  If you find peace in repetition and enjoy setting your own goals, I’d say buy this game, otherwise you won’t get much out of it. And on the front of accessibility, the game has colour blind mode on by default and supports scaling. I was appreciative of both.

This game was great! My favorite part is when you're using the sonic screwdriver, and you're all like 'ZAP ZAP' and the Cybermen are all electrocuted, and- Wait... This is the Back to the Future game? Why didn't you tell me?  Now don't get me wrong. I LOVE the Back to the Future trilogy. It's literally one of my favorite movie series. Yet, this game just didn't really stick out to me.  *Pros*: Somewhat interesting story, okay voice acting, kinda funny characters, and simple game mechanics. *Cons*: Controls are buggy (Making Marty seem like a bit of a derp...), the camera is spastic, the dialouge can be tedious, and this isn't the game's fault, but more the company who made it. (TellTale games.) No matter what you do, it's just one path. No decisions, nothing. Just walking around... It just gets a bit dull.   Overall, I wouldn't really recommend this game if you're looking for a fun adventure game. If you're a die hard BTTF fan or it's really cheap on Steam, then you should probably get this game. (Provided you don't mind the controls.) 6.95/10 Stars

review_text: Are you Old Enough?    If you're old enough to remember the old Rollercoaster Tycoon games from the last decade or maybe even Theme Park (which was so long ago now I don't want to think about it) you'll be on familiar territory with Adventure Park.  Familiar but slightly disappointing territory.   Wait... This is a review for Adventure Park, Right?   It is, but a little historical context is important here. Let me take you on journey back to 2005.   We were listening to The Crazy Frogs Axel F on our I Pod, watching Star Wars: Revenge of the Sith at the cinema and secretly hoping that Santa would bring us a new Xbox 360 for Christmas.  One of the other things that I found myself doing back the halcyon summer of 2005 was playing an amazing game called Rollercoaster Tycoon 3.   Rollercoaster... of Love   A shiny new 3d graphics engine, terraforming, customisable scenery, Rollercoaster Tycoon 3 had it all and that's before you start building the facilities and the rides. You'd build a few shops, a few rides, maybe place a fireworks display or two. Employ some staff to run it all for you and tweak your prices to pay for them.   Then you could drop back down into first person view and explore your own park, even take a ride on that huge rollercoaster you'd just finished building! Now lets go back... back to the future!    So Adventure Park is Like Rollercoaster Tycoon?   Yes and no..... mostly no.  In the sense that you get to build your own theme park, rides, stores and all, then yes, Adventure Park is like Rollercoaster Tycoon. In the sense that it is executed as well or as much fun to play as Rollercoaster Tycoon then No, no it's not like the classic game at all.   But it looks 'nice'    Yes, compared to the classic park games, it looks 'nice'. The graphics are fairly well detailed (if not pretty) even when you zoom all the way in.  Then again, the hardware we're all running has come a long way since that Rollercoaster fuelled summer of 2005, so this is really a given.   Bigger IS Better    At first I found the tiny map size to be another hindrance to my enjoyment of this game.  Then I began to realise that this isn't a huge issue as there isn't that much content to place on them anyway.   Double Dipper    The star attraction of any game like this is usually the Rollercoaster design itself. Unfortunately here, your coaster construction options are limited.  Want loops, spirals, curved bends banking and twisting their way around the scenery of your park? then you're out of luck.  The track design here is strictly an up and down affair.   Hey Kids, What's that Sound?    Whilst playing I began to notice that the game felt hollow, empty somehow. I gave up with the clunky controls and left my second 'upty, downy' coaster half constructed to ponder why. Then it occurred to me, the reason this game was lacking any sort of atmosphere was down to sound, or lack there of. No people screaming as they hurtle around the (just up and down) track of your painfully built coaster. no chatter of people as they stroll between the burgerstand and toilets, no ambient sound at all,   Apart from the occasional squawk from a stray flock of birds lost in the souless emptyness that is your 'fun' park and some slightly irritating music, all is silent.   Death by a Thousand Cuts    There are many little annoyances in this game that by themselves wouldn't overly bother me, but taken all together they become a pretty big distraction.  I could point out a host of irritations here but I think I can some it up with the example you are about to read.   Why Can't I sit down?    You can't sit down because you need to achieve a 3 star park before you can build benches. Benches! Wow, We need to get the R&D boys on that! oh wait, their is no R&D, just the star rating system to unlock objects.  I know this is a game mechanic to incentivise continued play but benches.... really! Oh well, just keep walking...   Rose Tinted Monitor   For me, it's the nostalgia of building a theme park again that attracted me to Adventure Park, unfortunately nostalgia can only carry you so far.  After playing Adventure Park for a while I was left with an overwhelming desire to turn it off and play Rollercoaster Tycoon 3 instead.   Enjoyment can be found here, you just have to look really, really hard to find it.    ( To view the full review (and others) please take the time to visit  http://www.review-well.com/  )

COUNTER STRIKE: CONDITION ZERO   Cs 1.6 with graphical improvements and bot mode.  Graphics Same as CS but a few improvements. New player model 1 for each team. Slight improvements to weapons and such. But This is 2004 And Halflife 2 came out about the same time with the source engine. BUT this is on Goldsrc engine for a game that was realeased in 2004.   AI: 8/10  Has a 'Campaign' which is basically matches against bots where you try to complete goals like 'Kill 4 enemys' 'Rescue 2 Hostages' 'use a Flashbang' Makes a nice Practice mode for singleplayer because the original CS didnt come with bots. The enemy AI is quite good if you arent a CS Pro and I had to reapet level quite a few times.   Sound: 6/10 Slight Improvements but overall the same  Gameplay: 8/10 Same As the original counter strike but i feel the hitboxes are a bit bigger. Recoil is still there so Spray-and-Pray isnt the way to go.  Overall 7/10 Decent Game Dont buy it on its own get it with CS:Complete Pack or the original Counter Strike  Game Design 6/10 Pretty Good. Improvements across the board.   Overall 7/10 Only buy this with CS:Complete Set. Or with CS 1.6 Multiplayer is Mostly Dead.   COUNTER STRIKE CONDITON ZERO : DELETED SCENES Expansion Pack For CS:CZ With a new campaign thats exactly like your average COD campaign WITH ZERO PLOT. review_text: Early Access Review

State of Decay is a SINGLEPLAYER zombie survival game set in the Midwest United States. It involves rounding up smaller bands of human survivors and incorporating them into your 'influencial area', AKA your zombie survival society. While this mechanic is thought out in amazing detail, down to emotions and job distribution, a lot is to be desired for the entire game.    The first problem I encountered starting gameplay was the somewhat steep learning curve. As the de facto 'leader' of your party, a massive amount of responsibilites are shoved into your arms. And I appreciate that for its realism, of course as a leader of a surviving band of humans in a zombie apocalypse a lot of responsibilites are going to be picked up by you, but in State of Decay the concept is blown out of proportion. Your fellow survivors are utterly inept at taking care of themselves, falling off roofs and demanding to be rescued. Rather than an NPC taking care of the problem while you're out trying to aquire medical supplies, YOU have to go out of the way to save him or else he'll die and everybody will be angry at YOU for not saving him.   Another concept that was annoying was the fact you were STUCK with a group of man-children for your survival party. In essence, you had no option to go it alone or become a nomadic survivor. How hard is it to ask for a quiet life in the middle of the woods eating peas and corn out of a can while making the occasional excursion into zombie territory? The main reason why much of my map goes unexplored is because I have to deal with party issues that crop up every twenty seconds or so. I don't want to stop my search for fellow survivors because some idiot back at base slipped and dropped his ice cream.   This is another nitpick I had to deal with- the survivor-survivor interaction in the game was DISMAL. While other (NPC) bands of survivors eke a living out in the zombie territory, they never come off as hostile to the point where they start targeting you. While this may seem like a good thing, it's actually an extremely narrow-minded concept. Example: 'oh, all survivors are good, let's all be friends.' More than likely in a disaster scenario everyone would be thinking for themselves, take Hurricane Katrina for example. Gangs of looters were prevalent in the suburbs, and murder was commonplace before the National Guard locked the place down. While some NPCs may express hatred towards you or your group, they will never take an action against you short of refusing to come on an expedition with you.   Some other parts of the game greatly affected my enjoyment of gameplay, such as the fact that a solid steel crowbar can break if you hit soft flesh enough times. Close-up mechanics are about as robust as Space Station 13. Gunplay is almost depressing, repeated hits to the forehead from a .44 Magnum revolver to an eight-foot tall inbred zombie monstrosity wasn't enough to put it down, let alone shots from a Remington 750. This was during an early mission, and somehow the thing bugs through the window and tears me in half. Driving cars is fun until you run over enough zombies and your car inexplicably EXPLODES, or you hit a pebble in the road and you go flying. Zombies are also able to latch onto your car even as you're driving past them at 50 mph.    However the game still had some redeeming qualities. Guns are correctly named for the most part, only the ones with copyright licenses in place had to be changed and some others were just renamed for easier naming. (But really, 'Monag' is the new Mosin Nagant!? Since when is a 100 year old pre-soviet era rifle licensed?) Visuals are great, especially when walking down the highway. Just ignore that constantly updating survivor-♥♥♥♥up-o-meter in the top right and relax. Dialogue between survivors is frequent and expressive.   In the end, this game had a lot going for it, and possibly more in the future, although unlikely. The absence of multiplayer makes playing the game almost a sort of chore. Two heads are better than one, they say. And as of now, there's only one head. This game seemed good, but now it just reeks of wasted potential. The large screen at the start that says 'BUY DLC HURR HURR' is really a big sign that shows that the developers really don't care about their players. It's quite sad, really.  I rate State of Decay a 4 out of 10, because there were so many things that could have gone so right that in the end went so wrong. I want my five dollars back.

review_text: This was one of the first modern racing games I played. I hadn't played a racing game in quite a while beforehand. .. and I was disappointed. I remember playing Hot Pursuit 2 in the past on the Gamecube? or some console around that time and it was pretty cool.. it had good visuals for its time, an awesome soundtrack, fun levels, and a great car selection complete with the ability to freely set up tracks and play as you want alone or with friends. That was a cool game.  This one isn't.  Visually it looks good. The music is.. meh. The music is boring. The title or level select screens don't have that awesome upbeat style the older Hot Pursuit games did. Its so dark and boring.  The actual levels have a limited selection of vehicles of choice. The sense of speed is pretty good, but the levels are very very basic. Despite being a more arcadey game, it kind of felt like you should just play a racing simulator instead. Its like, a weird in-between of arcadey awesome and simulation and fails to deliver on either. There are some more arcadey game modes where you drop spike traps and things like that and they're just boring. Being chased by cops is dull.  The whole game is incredibly bland. Oh, and there isn't even a free-play mode where you choose a level and any car you  want + other settings like cops on or off, difficulty, and a bunch of other things the old NFS games had. Yeah, that doesn't exist at all. There's multiplayer but I never tried it. All you get is a singleplayer campaign with no story and limitations.  Maybe I didn't give it enough of a chance but I found myself tired of it too early on to keep playing. This game just isn't very good and there are much better racing games out there.

review_text: Early Access Review

As many people have mentioned, this game only gives you the very false sense of choosing your own path, while forcing you down an extremely linear story line.  I tend to be one of those gamers that normally, when faced with a choice, will save the game and see where each choice leads. Unfortunately, that is not an option in this type of game. If you make a choice and do not like the outcome, you are forced to sit through the ENTIRE session of dialogue leading up to that choice, which can range from 5-20 minnutes at times because of the horribly placed autosaves and the fact that you are not allowed to skip dialogue that you've already seen before.  Now the first thing of note IS the fact that you cannot skip dialogue. If this is supposed to be a choose your own adventure game 'where the choices you make matter', most people would play it multiple times through to see what impact the different choices you make have on the story. But solely by not allowing people to skip the standard boring dialogue scenes greatly dimishes the replay value, because when you are forced to watch the same scene 2-4 times in a row just to choose a different answer makes the game EXTREMELY boring and repetitive. Even their older games (i.e. Back to the Future) allow you to skip dialogue, so if you have played the game before (or decide to reload because you didn't like your outcome) you could fastfoward through all of the chat screens to speed it up. This is not the case with Game of Thrones.  Now, on to the fact that the game IS NOT A 'CHOOSE YOUR OWN ADVENTURE GAME'. Dealing with reading the same text over and over again COULD be reconciled by the fact that each choice you makes changes the game dramatically. But honestly, most of the time the choice you pick doesn't have any significance, leading up to your character saying something and the opposing NPC giving you a completely off the wall response out of nowhere. Or, at their very best, you get 1-2 lines of unique dialogue corresponding to your choice, wherein they proceed to launch down their original path of dialogue with no regards to anything you have said to them before.  Point of fact, as a very particular gamer, anytime I had a dialogue choice and picked a response I did not like, I would reload my game. And sit through 10 minutes of cutscenes and inane chatter. Only to lead up to me picking a different response and getting THE EXACT SAME LINE OF DIALOGUE I had gotten with my other choice. Rinse, repeat. Same deal.  *Spoilers may follow* In this episode, there is in fact only one choice that matter. Who you choose to be your 'Sentinel' (basically, the Hand of the 'Lord of Ironrath' aka ripoff Hand of the King). And this really affects nothing other than the very last scene of the episode, determining where you meet Ramsey Bolton (Snow) or who you meet him with. Granted, there are other choices you can make during the game that MAY affect further episodes down the road, but for a company/game that touts 'The choices you make affect your gameplay experience' they are getting extremely lazy in developement.  There are a lot of scenes in this game that while the dialogue was happening, I knew what choice I would pick. And then you dont get the option. (Spoiler, if you have a very serious diplomat versus a very serious warrior, why would you alienate one over the other instead of choosing them both to be your advisor?)  This game is an extremely forced and contrived story so far, where you opinion doesn't really mean anything. Telltale Games is basically just cashing in on franchises now, and pushing out a product that, while overall has very good quality, doesn't even come close to a 'Choose your own Adventure' type game, which is their biggest selling point. What they sell now basically equates to watching a 90 minute cutscene, where they stop to let you choose a response to a question and they either humor you with 1 or 2 lines of dialogue in response, or completely ignore what you've chosen and continue on a pre-determing path of dialogue.  Overall, as a fan of Game of Thrones, I liked the game, honestly. But I don't like Telltale Games as a company. They are becoming complete sell-outs with no respect for their original property, where its becoming more important to release and episode quickly than work on quality. And what that results in is a very Linear story where they can't afford to create different story paths because they are so focused on pushing it out quick.  (Spoilers)   Just in this episode alone:   **I don't even have a number to point out how pointless the QTEs are in this game. I've played through the prologue multiple times to try and save the Lord and nothing changes. You can fail half the QTEs and the scenes are basically still the same as if getting them right. Not to mention the fact that they even throw in some QTEs that you CANNOT win just to try and show off some new button presses and gore. (AKA the soldier choking you out and then you throwing him under the horses, or picking up a sword and getting knocked down like a punk every time)  1. Even in the very beginning prologue, no choice that you make matters. Talk as much crap to the Lord as you want. Be a horrible person to the other squire. None of it matters. The only real choice you can make here is to save the other squire when he refuses to listen to you and spouts the same blatant response of 'carry the wine' to you each time. If you save him, he shows up in further scenes during the prologue (although it CAN be very hard to make him out as of after this point he really doesnt matter). If you choose to leave him, you still sit around the wine trough and watch him die, the camp goes to hell, and you run through the exact same sequence except the Squire will be replaced by a random NPC and you will receive 1** extra dialogue sequence where they ask what has happened to him. Because in the midst of the battle, the Lord is more worried about some random squire than all of his other people combined PLUS his eldest son who he was just begging you to look after.... RIGHT... And considering the dialogue sequence leading up to the wine gathering where he basically promotes you from a squire and asks you to look after his eldest, he REALLY doesn't seem to care that much because the plot REQUIRES him to die.  2, Why is Gared (the first main playable character who is very quickly forgotten about) forced to ride to the wall? This IS supposed to be a 'Choose your own adventure' game, right? I would have had him stay and confront Lord Whitehill, or at the very least have a conversation with Lord Ethan. But instead you are basically forced to leave the city (in order to further their plot line of the 'North Grove cannot be lost'). Even his Uncle is like 'You need to leave now, this story can't stand up if you don't leave no matter what.'  3. Your dad dies. Awesome. No matter what you decide to do, this entire scene has no purpose other than to progress their story line. You arrive, 3 people on the farm have murdered your father, and no matter what you do you are forced to kill the first guy (who coincidentally is a Whitehill soldier). You can choose to spare the next one, but it really doesnt matter at all, you're gone.  4. Lord Whitehill... No matter what you say to him, he is an a$$hole and doesn't care about anything you say. You can't appease him (like your uncle wishes) and you can't stand up to him (like your Man-at-Arms wishes). You may get a different dialogue line depending on if you lick his boots or not, but it changes nothing for the story.  I won't even name anymore. You can look at any walkthrough for the game and it will show you the choices that you can make, and then immediately proceed to tell you that said choices do not matter.  Even though I love Telltale Games's graphics, they are very quickly becoming a company that is selling out.

review_text: Soundtrack while playing:   Survivor - Eye Of The Tiger   This is a pleasant little game. And by that I mean it’s been designed by Lucifer himself.   If you like Mazes, or old school first person dungeon crawlers minus the combat being turned base, dying a lot due to rats, poison, falling and cthulhu’s ball sack killing you then this is a game for you.   Do you like puzzles? searching for keys? Back tracking or losing your car in the mouth of hell? How about losing your way, or seeing the same wall 300 times? Or was it the same wall? Aw man, the walls move too.   Oh look a hole, oh, gravity works and suddenly you’re a floor down with what I imagine are broken legs.   If you don’t like rats, the plague or anything else listed above then this is probably not for you.   Fortunately, I’m a masochist in video games. I love it when they’re hard and designed that way. I swear a lot when I play them, but I’m in them for the long haul.   What I don’t like is video games that are poorly designed. Where I get stuck on walls, or even an enemy and then because someone smacks with me a trout until I eat all the cheese wheels in the kingdom, and still don't have enough health and die.   This game however, is not one of those.   It’s an awesome little throwback to old school games where you had to think, and remember things. The type of game that you would keep a notepad by your desk so you can remember a code (haven’t seen any code needing doors yet.) But yes, your memory will help you here. As will rock throwing.   Chuck that s**t, so hard.    Really, you should get it, it’s fun. Time consuming, and yet again, another game that I can play for a few minutes at a time.   It’s immersive, beautiful in it’s own way, terrifying in others. It even tracks how poorly you do. I assume that’s for how well you did, but I’m a pessimist by nature.   I also really like the way the flashlight works in the game, Just the light moves around, not your whole head. Which I guess is an odd thing to like, but it’s my damn review, so hush.    I’ve yet to complete it, because I keep finding new ways to kill myself. But I intend to complete this and find my way out. and for 11.99 you can get as much time as you’re willing to get out of it.   The overall point is to make it down to the final level, and then survive a trip back up.   So it’s a lot like Black Friday sales. Only you don’t have to put on pants, or leave the house.   Unless you’re in Kentucky, then it’s exactly like Black Friday Sales.

review_text: Witcher 3 is an open world action-RPG with multiple locations, a variety of enemies, a great number of characters and a story that covers multiple sides of a land plagued by war and monsters. Your actions affect people and the world, and sometimes the right choice is not so obvious.  Technically the game seems to function fine, with maybe one crash in about 100 hours gameplay. No major FPS drops or stuttering, and only one minor quest has gotten stuck, and even that quest only involved opening a single chest and nothing more.  This is one of those few games where there isn't (much at least) potato-mash filler content (Assassin's Creed flags, gah). There's a ton of quests in this game, but almost all of them have something unique to them. Witcher contracts are your main money earning venture, and even those are not your typical MMO style 'kill 10 monsters' types of quests. There might be an unknown monster terrorizing and eating villagers, someone's gone missing, you need to find a vandal, you need to get an object back etc. and the contracts may take the most bizarre turns, or you'll have to pick sides you never even knew were there to begin with.  The combat has multiple aspects to it. For physical close-quarters combat you have two swords, steel and silver, for different types of enemies. Usually the correct sword will be unsheathed automatically, but you may have to switch manually when ending up fighting multiple enemy types. For minor distances you have magical effects called 'signs', and they have a variety of effects from burning, stunning, knocking down to shielding. For ranged combat you have the crossbow and bombs. Crossbows are quite effective against flying and underwater enemies, or dispatching certain enemies in a stealthy fashion. Bombs come with different effects, and they have to be crafted first.  The special thing about combat in Witcher 3 is preparedness. You have to repair your equipment, or you'll find yourself severely handicapped in a fight. You'll have to create different battle potions and sword oils through alchemy to amplify your damage towards different enemy types, or protect yourself from some of the damage the specific monsters deal. It's also beneficial to know the weaknesses of enemy types, and for the bigger enemies and bosses it's pretty much a must.  The game also include levels and skill points, where points can be used to enhance your character in different ways (combat, signs, alchemy, general), and investing in a single category opens up your available skills. You have to think your character through carefully; instead of having every purchased skill being available forever, you have a maximum of 8 skill slots (in vanilla) where you specifically have to put your acquired skills.  The game does cram a hefty amount of text about it’s mechanics forcefully into your face from the get-go, but it's hard to imagine other ways of presenting this information. I did read most of the descriptions, but I mostly learned to understand the mechanics by playing.  Two expansions are also available: Hearts of Stone adds a massive questline in already established lands and some new game mechanics, while Blood and Wine opens a completely new location with as much content as a whole new game.  If you love open world games, I can't recommend this enough.

review_text: While a failure of overambition, and as a result clearly rushed, it is still one of the better isometric RPG's of the past decade. Its biggest failing, and it is one that is common in narrative RPG's which centers on an entire player created party instead of a single character, is that the story simply fails to draw you in. You are just not given a reason to care about your characters as anything other than stat sheets, and as a result the entire world becomes less engaging. The combat is, unfortunately, rather shallow and there is therefore entirely too much of it. Far too many areas are riddled with things to kill, and even the overland map has an abundance of random enemy encounters.  There is a good variety of enemies, but when your tactical options are limited to shoot, move or overwatch, that does not matter as much as it should. Various classes do get a few special attacks at later levels, but it is not enough to add any actual depth to the combat. You never find yourself wondering what the best tactical option is beyond what enemy to focus on, or if you should use explosives (aoe).  On the positive side, there is a great deal of reactivity in the various quests you uncover and the world is populated by plenty of well written characters.  Overall the writing is pretty good, the world is interesting and the art work is excellent. While perhaps not the 'real fallout 3' its kickstarter backers where hoping for, it is still a great game and definetly worth a playthrough.

review_text: No... Just no
Early Access Review

This game is not bad. I can understand why people enjoy it but I had more fun playing Dead Rising 2 than this game. There are many reasons why but it is easier to go over the most glaring issues I have with this game.  1. No experimenting. In Dead Rising 2, it had introduced combo weapons. To me, I loved the combo weapons, no matter how wacky or unimaginable the weapon was but at least you could experiment with it. Dead Rising 3 has the same but it doesn't have as much depth as in Dead Rising 2 because in order to craft combo weapons, you need a blueprint to unlock it rather than experimenting in Dead Rising 2 which unlocks the combo card but you get half of the PP earned while using it.  2. The level up system. In the previous Dead Rising series, it was completely random on what you leveled up. Sometimes it is good and sometimes it is bad but it adds variety and makes you want to level up and gain PP more. In Dead Rising 3, it is very simple and it doesn't motivate me enough to level up. The only skills I did end up leveling was health and item capacity which is a minor issue and can be a tad bit annoying.  3. Blueprint collectables. What is the point of collecting blueprints when they added the option to unlock all of a catagory of blueprints in your level up system. It ruins the point of exploring the open world. Sure you can run around and find other collectables like the Frank West tropies or the Sad Endings, but those only give PP which I never really cared for as much.  4. THE WEAPON LOCKER! Why give us a weapon locker that can spawn insane amounts of items? In fact, why is it in the game? It ruins the point of exploring and creating combo weapons. For those who have not gotten the game yet, essencially, the weapon locker is a locker that can spawn items you have picked-up on during your adventure. It may sound cool but when it allows you to spawn combo weapons, what is the point? All I had to do was craft one combo weapon and went back to the weapon locker to spawn 3 more of it. That kills the combo system that was innovative in Dead Rising 2.  5. Combo Vechicles. Now many of you may wonder why this is a bad thing. 'Its a cool thing to have man! A motorcycle combined with a steam roller is awesome!' But here is the thing, it makes the game far too easy. the first 2 combo vechicles you get in the game are easily the best way to kill hordes of zombies. Zombies are nothing more than like annoying piles of paperwork in the office. I know there are other combo vechicles but why use those when I can have a armored car with a shotgun attached to it and blades on the side? Also, you can spawn combo cars from garages as well, making the game even more easy.  6. Boss fights. Now boss fights were the cool things in the Dead Rising series. Seeing a person go mad because of the zombie outbreak and having to fight them was a challenge, but when I can spawn in combo weapons like rabbits spawning kids, then what challenge is there? I fought the first boss only having to drink a orange juice once because of a certain combo weapon that I had spawned 2 more of out. I know in Dead Rising 2, there were some bosses that were hard and unfair but at least it provided a challenge for you to get stronger and get more combo weapons but here, it is pathetic.  7. Co-op. If I am able to beat this game in single player without dying or even getting close to dying, then what is the point of co-op? The game is far too easy and your character becomes a god of death so easily because of the insane combo weapons you can use, which clears zombies and bosses like nothing. So what is the point of co-op to a easy game? There is almost no reason to unless you wanted to play this game with a friend. Sure Dead Rising 2 had co-op but at least the bosses were hard enough for co-op. In fact, I died 5-7 times in Dead Rising 2 because of bosses. Now its just like riding a car with spikes all over it only to try run over a peanut couldn't open.  8. Food combo. Why must I level up and put an attribute to obtain food combos? Why not put blenders around the map like in Dead Rising 2? because food combos are the lifeline to this game. I know that you can make combo weapons now on the go but why food combos too? It makes the game even more easy. Food combos can give you more health squares than a jug of orange juice and it gives you a buff. So why not follow all the other Dead Rising examples and put blenders around the map to craft the food combos? I don't mean like a blender on every corner but like a blender or 2 in each area of the map. That would provide challenge and make the player want to run to it for a huge heal instead, I currently have 7 food combos around me with 6 combo weapons, all of which, I can't carry.  9. Optimization. The optimization and port of this game is a mess. It offers too little and expects players to have powerful rigs. I don't have a powerful computer so I have to try run it at the lowest settings and it still has issues with constant fps drops and random crashing. It is not a very enjoyable experience when ones computer cannot run it so well.  10. THE TIMERS! What happened to the timers from Dead Rising? Why did they disappear for the main story? The timers in Dead Rising gave you pressure if you wanted to complete the story, offering a challenge to do all of the side quest and boss fights in a timely manner. Now, the main story has no time limit and there is no pressure or motivation on the player to even complete the story. Side quest are the only ones with timers but usually it is quite easy to ignore since they only give PP, which is ok but it is also un-motivating to do more when the level up system is quite stupid in my opinion.  11. (last one) The story and characters. The story is very stereotypical and easy to catch on. The plot twist are easy to spot and know and it is quite dull. Characters are not bad but most I didn't care for. Nick Ramos, the main protaginist and player, is a nice guy who is trying to get out alive but is quite bland and is always worried about the number of zombies around him when he can create the most absurd and god-like weaponry I have seen, making me wonder why he is even afraid of zombies when he himself is like a noris god of death. Your co-op partner, ♥♥♥♥, is not very likable and is kind of a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who is self-centered. Rhonda can be easily ignored. The only half-decent character is Annie, who you will meet in the start. She has her issues but is more interesting than the others, which is a lot to say for.  Now even with all of these obvious problems, there are some good things for Dead Rising 3.  1. Combo weapons. Even though the combo weapons can be spawned over and over from weapon lockers and are over-powered doesn't make them any less interesting. the combo weapons in Dead Rising 3 are cool to see and hilarous on how they can be crafted. Even though there are less combo weapons in Dead Rising 3 than Dead Rising 2, they are sometimes fun to use.  2. Outfits. Like in all Dead Rising games, you can dress up your character in whatever clothes you want him in and it is no different here. You can have Nick Ramos dress up in ridiculous costumes and outfits from other capcom games like Megaman or in a funny mascot outfit. It also carries over in cutscenes, creating hilarious and funny moments when people talk to Nick calmly while he is in a Banana Hammmock.  3. The map. The map is huge and breath-taking. Even though its not as charming as the mall in Dead Rising 2, it still shows what life is like now in the future and how expansive the map is.  Well, that about sums this review up about Dead Rising 3 for me. Overall, its not bad but it is definately not worth recommending at its price now and it shows how it has taken a bad turn since Dead Rising 2. I luckly got this game from a code my friend gave me so I feel a bit more satisfied with it being free but for $50, it is not worth it.

review_text: Early Access Review

Rocket Power  Here it is, everyone - eSports. In trying to legitimize playing video games as anything other than a complete waste of time, Psyonix have crafted quite possibly the most boring game I've ever played. Playing Rocket League is like playing a real sport, except it comes with none of the satisfaction that playing a real sport has. Sure, you just drove your cyber-car into the digi-ball, scoring the game winning hyper-goal, but at the end of the day you're still a gross smelly nerd playing video games and probably jacking off to anime girls. If I score a goal in soccer, I feel happy not only because I'm helping my team win, but also because I'm bettering my body and mind at the same time. I want to be able to win a game and then not immediately feel immense disappointment because nothing I've accomplished is real.  In attempting to emulate a real sport, Psyonix also failed to properly utilize their medium. Rocket League is so simple that any novelty or entertainment factor that it has quickly wears off as long as you're a normally-functioning adult. Think to any other game that one would consider an 'eSport', and you'll quickly see how little depth there is to Rocket League. Hell, Counter Strike, another game which requires very little strategy, is much more complex simply because of the nature of the game.  In short, what we have here is an attempt to fuse two incompatible worlds: that of real sports, and that of fake sports. In doing so, Psyonix has stripped away what makes each of these unique, and the result is some sort of unholy abomination that is better off turbo-blasting itself into the dumpster.  Taster's score: C-

But that's no fun, right? That's where the problem lies. It's a fighting game, so what you wanna do is find a character of your liking, start training, and become good! But that doesn't mean ♥♥♥♥ when all you opponent has to do is block and uppercut!   It took me around a day to become what I like to call 'moderately good'. It's that point where you beat everyone you challenge locally, and people start saying that you're good at it. But that doesn't help when, if I join a game and the opponent chooses Takeda, all he has to do is back-2-1-kunai!   It literally takes away 75% of the game! And that is why I just can't make myself rate it any better than this.   *reluctantly pushes post*

review_text: RWBY: Grimm Eclipse is great. It’s a simple yet addicting game where you play as one of the four main characters and hack and slash through hordes of enemies. Each character is fun and unique to play as, leaving lots of replay value to find your most suited and favorite playstyle. (For example as Ruby, I like to stun enemies with ranged attacks and then go in with a power attack) The locations are varied, beautifully designed, and shaded. Character models do not exactly resemble the show but still look fine. There is an enticing skill system you’ll find yourself coming back to in order to unlock new and powerful abilities. The game also features a funny and interesting dialogue, and you’ll enjoy hearing each and every one of Yang’s puns. In addition to all of this, there is also a ranking system, which allows you to increase your hunter’s rank after you’ve maxed every character’s level and completed select challenges. All in all this leaves for an immeasurable amount of playtime. So why a negative review? Because despite everything, this game is missing key features. Combat itself is okay but nothing notable. Generally speaking enemies are damage sponges. You continue to whack away at them until they die. For example the Beowolves. Although hits from these enemies will take a chunk of your health, it’s fairly easy to just spam the dodge button and escape from them, leaving no sense of actual challenge or skill. For the most part the enemies aren’t even difficult. They often stand around, wait a few seconds, and then execute a fairly easy to dodge attack. (I would add the android/robot type enemies as the sole exception) Therefore there’s no real sense of challenge. Most of the difficulty comes from trying to survive hordes as opposed to an enemy itself. Which is alright but definitely a bit lacking in substance. This game was supposed to release with a horde mode. As mentioned earlier, this would maybe rectify the games lack of challenging AI because most of the difficulty and substance of the game relies on hordes instead of individual enemies. However as of the day this review was posted, there is no horde mode. Furthermore, teamwork is virtually nonexistent. For a game that prides itself on being similar to L4D2, It really falls short. The most teamwork you’ll probably ever get out of this game is if one of your members dies and you’ll need to revive them. Oh, but that’s the thing. If your teammates chooses not to revive you, your character will just revive after a set amount of time. So there’s no actual consequence for not helping each other. Not to mention each character is set up in a way that they can all play independently of each other. So despite having a full team, you’ll find you often don’t need each other and can just go off and fight on your own. It really is disappointing. And finally, there is no Team JNPR. Yes Team RWBY is great, but eventually you’ll tire of the four characters. For a game with only about four hours of campaign, there’s only so much you can get out of a limited cast. Adding another team would definitely increase the value and playability. Supposedly JNPR is planned but is also not part of the game currently. With everything I’ve listed, I’d say this game is definitely not worth $25. It’s very much incomplete. $15 sure; it's simple, it’s fun, and it’s RWBY. But $25? No. The campaign is short, it’s missing a promised feature (horde mode), combat is flawed, and you only get four characters to play as. So my overall verdict is this. If you’re a huge fan of RWBY and don’t care about price, buy this game. You’ll love it. You get the main cast, some fun hack and slash combat, and an interesting side story. But otherwise avoid it until they at least add Horde Mode. Or buy it during a sale.
