review_text: i don't recommend this game though

Switch puzzle/physics games have always had a special place in my heart. You can usually find them piled next to things like my mental versions of political figures I dislike, or my memories of Star Wars Episode II--you know, things that I like to take out every so often when I’m bored in my head and burn just for fun. It took very little time for me to determine that Vessel would be added to this pile.  Yes, I’m a little (maybe a lot) biased. I did just admit that I don’t like these kinds of games--but maybe let me explain a bit about Vessel and myself, and you may understand why.  Vessel is inherently a game where you play as some nameless white guy who runs around and hits switches and pulls levers. I suppose he has some backstory of being some great inventor, but it doesn’t really matter to the game at all. I could have been playing as Calvin, the bag-boy from the local grocery store, and the game wouldn’t have changed much. In fact, I think I’ll just start calling the inventor Calvin. At least it makes *me* laugh.  So Calvin is sitting in his lab, possibly reminiscing about his past achievements--the invention of a strange aquatic creature called “Fluorous”--when one of the Fluorous lock him out of his lab, leading him on an… “adventure” (by the loosest definition of the word)... where he must get back into his lab. Along the way, he’ll hit switches. He’ll open doors. He’ll pull levers and walk through more doors. If you’re lucky, you’ll get to spray water at something in random orders until, eventually, it might open another door. This inherently is the problem with most puzzle games in general but specifically Vessel--what is my motivation? What is my goal? Why do I care? I know nothing of the character, he isn’t that likeable, the Fluorous aren’t really interesting, and after 5 minutes I already know exactly the kind of game I’ll be playing.  “Oh, but what of the water physics? They’re neat, aren’t they?” I suppose to some degree they are, but what does it matter? I’ve never understood why physics justify the existence of a game. Physics are simply that--physics. Companies sell physics engines, but gamers don’t *buy* physics engines, and most puzzle games are essentially just that--an excuse to display a physics engine. If I wanted physics, I’d walk onto my porch a drop a rock off my balcony. “Does gravity still work? Yep, it does.” Fascinating.  One might argue that Vessel displays impressive, or at least “stylistic” artwork. To some degree, this is true--while the design did not appeal to me at all, I can recognize that work was put into the game’s visuals, lighting and layout. While the rooms are basically boxes with switches and buttons, they do try their best to appear interesting, with pipes running everywhere, cave-work in the background, and dynamic shadows/lighting. Yes, one might say that the game is “artistic” or “beautiful.” I could also tell you that the Sistine Chapel is “artistic” and “beautiful,” but I wouldn’t tell you that it makes the Sistine Chapel a good video game.   I guess the biggest crux I have with Vessel and most puzzle games, is that I have to ask the question, “Why?” Why would I want to do this? If you stripped away the graphics--if you stripped away the lighting and soothing music--the actual game “experience” is equivalent to someone locking you in an empty white room filled with buttons and switches, and a PA box overhead says “you can leave when you figure out how to open the door,” and at that point it’s up to me find the correct order to hit all the switches and pull all the levers. Yes, there are generally clues, and yes it exercises problem solving--but so does a textbook--and ultimately at the end of the day, I’m just implementing procedural inputs into a linearly designed path that I have to follow to get to the next set of required procedural inputs and on and on until I can finally see the credits roll. Being a stubborn gamer, I usually do just that--however this time, I decided to break the mold and find my own escape from the crushing loneliness of the underground steam maze.  Once I obtained the ability to create Fluorous seeds, I soon discovered that the game allowed you to create an infinite amount of them--better yet, it did not despawn the old seeds. Thusly, I set out to see how many seeds I could create before the game crashed. In truth, I had more fun swimming through my seed sea than any experience I had solving Vessel’s puzzles. After 15 minutes or so, the game crashed, and I finally had my freedom.

review_text: Early Access Review

Very flawed. This game had the potential to be truly great, but carried no inertia into it. Coming from Unleashed (Daytime stages), Colours and then Generations, this title seems to be a de-evolution in reverting Sonic back into a janky platformer with many frustrating gimmicks and gameplay choices. At least it's not Sonic Boom...  The game runs great on most PCs, but does need a bit more GPU power to play compared to Generations. That may throw some people off, as Generations was a visually superior and seemingly larger game overall. However, if your rig can handle it, 60 FPS is consistent and vitally necessary to avoid gameplay issues and possible motion illness. Also, this is the first Sonic game to not need a launcher, which may or not be welcomed by some.  The soundtrack is fantastic, with quite a bit of variation between each track. Expect to hear some tracks multiple times, because the main theme repeats throughout and certain Acts and environments are recycled in later levels. The script is not as polished as Colors but is a bit better than Generations' story. I guess the Happy Tree Friends' writers grew bored after counting all their Sega money. Do you like cutscenes in your Sonic game? Expect to sit (or skip) through a LOT of them.  Here's where we start running into issues; the gameplay. Nothing is explained to you clearly. You shouldn't expect a game to hold your hand throughout, but at the same time, not referring back to the wiki every 15 minutes like you were playing Dark Souls because you can't figure out what the game requires. Targeting is nuance and do not expect a solid double jump if enemies or targets around. Want that out-of-reach red coin on the wall adjacent to you and two enemies? Good luck, you'll not be getting it unless you jump beforehand at a particular angle and speed, and you'll just target Badniks.  Bosses repeat themselves, are either overly simple or complex and take up a lot of level time. Six main worlds with four normal acts in each. Boss fights at the end of two acts in each world (with the exception of the last world, which has 8 fights). I just wanna go fast, Sanic. These fights felt more like a chore than fun to play.  Acheivements are possibly the worse offender. Most games progress naturally with acheievements. You earn 50 coins, an achievement pops, then later when you would get 500 rings, another one. Not in Lost World; you are limited to pursuing three achievements at any given time, due to arbitary restrictions by Omo-Chao. They are also heavily randomized so you may repeat a few or go from defeating 500 enemies for one, to only defeating 50 in the next. It is game padding at its worse. It does not innovate the player much and becomes a grind.  My recommendation is pick this up on sale, preferably less than $10. Or get it on the Wii-U for more 'natural' controls and the ability to play the Nintendo DLC stages without mods. It'll perhap show Sega that you want more Sonic/SEGA games ported and developed for PC, but this in particular was a slight misstep in the direction for the series.

review_text: Just a Demo.  No story

++[Contains Spoilers]++ What I saw in the trailer was what I experienced in the game, what it didn't tell me was how boring retracing my steps would be (being a completionist doesn't help) among other inconveniences. This game is very challenging, play it if you like that and don't if you hate a challenge in reflexes and  patience . Especially don't try to do a 'Let's Play' like I did the week a paper is due or something like that; doing an extra college class during the summer while recording my impressions of this game was excruciating. This game is best played when everything you have to do in real life settles down and should be especially enjoyable if you have plenty of spare time and friends to watch you fail (I'm assuming). Don't play this game if you're impatient, since some of the stages will entrap you in infinite loops if you go the wrong way, require an adequate level of dexterity and a good sense of timing. The game has no music, so hearing the only three sound effects this game has to offer played over and over can get annoying at times -if not drive you insane. I know people like this game since as of this posting, the game's rating is ranked at 'very positive' but I would rather express my partial disdain for playing this, since this game has loads of potential but fell short of my expectations. If you want a hard platformer that's more stimulating, and actually has challenges you can remotely solve on your own, try Eryi's Action (also available on Steam) instead. Let me warn you that you can only win the game if you can descipher the markings in some of the stages and figure out what the magic word is using the code, so good luck trying to figure that out before googling it like everyone else (if you must know the magic word is 'super,' because you have to be supremely patient and determined to win this). It was worth every penny, which is to say it's worth nothing at all and that's not just because the game is free. I'm sorry if I sound harsh, I just expected better from the developers. It's still playable if you're still wondering at this point.   You can watch me endure and overcome this game here:  https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLq7fziG4I2WvaBxdsv7Lvc3bO-f288UXO

review_text: I'm glad that 70 years after the fact we can make video games with superficial faux national socialist themes without the Politically Correct Coalition blowing a gasket.  As for the game itself, it's a 2D multi-direction shooter with hectic, reflex based gameplay in a very old-school vein: fly, shoot, die and retry. The inertia and limited turning speed system let's you pull of cool maneuvers and makes it a bit more skilled than the average shooter. There are a lot of ship parts and combinations that allow you to experiment and find different styles of combat, and the adaptive soundtrack is pumping, but ultimately the game's format is limited and you'll burn out quickly.  And we still can't make games from the perspective of actual historical national socialists. No, that'll take another half a century.

review_text: I really enjoyed the music of this game, the art style, and much of the gameplay. It's a platformer in which the graphics and gameplay advances a bit as you go along, much like in Evoland, albeit at a far slower pace. There are four worlds, the first two of which each contain 12 levels followed by a boss fight, and the latter two which each contain 18 levels followed by a boss fight. The game presents the first world as an Atari game, the second as a Gameboy game, the third as an NES game, and the fourth as an SNES game. You'll see a few enemies that are obvious references to enemies from other games, and plenty of jokes and references to games of yore as well. You would expect those, and they're pretty fun.  I already said I liked the music, but seriously, the music is quite awesome in this game, so I'm mentioning it again. One thing that's a very nice feature in this game that sets it apart from other platformers is a realm you enter when you die, which is like a sort of mirror version of the world with various differences, enemies and collectibles of its own, and this gives you a second chance to find a portal back to the normal realm, or even use the other realm to move ahead a bit in the level before finding a later portal to cross back over. It's nice! You don't run out of lives or anything.  In fact, the trailer for the game itself specifcally says 'you will die. But you always get a second chance,' and it shows you dying, then running around in the other realm and going back through a portal. Cool, huh? Except when it flat out lies about you  always  getting a second chance that way. The third boss of the game is positively infuriating. You are running away from the boss, and if she catches you or you hit anything in the environment you shouldn't, you pass over into the other realm. Guess what? The other realm is packed to the brim with spikes on the ceilings and walls with no portals, so it's impossible to get back to the normal realm, and you die after a few seconds of invulnerability when the floor/wall/ceiling spikes touch you because you can't proceed without touching them.  I've probably died 40+ times trying to get past that part so far, and it took what was a charming and mostly relaxing game for me, and turned it into a profanity-spewing evening for me. It's an aggravating 'tonal shift' in that the rest of the game follows the 'second chance' principle promised by the trailer, but this part does not. I wouldn't mind the second chance lie if they at least had a well-placed checkpoint during the dash away from the boss, but there's not, so I had to keep redoing really easy stuff which takes a minute every time before hitting the parts that are generally fatal. The farthest I got was to a wing powerup located up a couple of vertical jumps, just before a long stretch of spikes in the distance. Every single time I make it that far, the boss touches me and I die, and have to start the whole thing over again.  If you'd like to know how difficult this was, if you've played Ori and the Blind Forest, I beat that game, and it had boss-dash stuff in it that was pretty aggravating. Anywho, this boss spoiled the good mood this game had been putting me in and left me angry and cut off from playing through the SNES portion of the game, which is a part of the payoff to getting through each world. I'm not claiming to be very good at games, so you might enjoy this game if you're skilled enough to pull off the escape from this boss which is handled differently than the advertising claims and differently than the way you play the rest of the game (never forced to rush until now).   I, however, do not recommend this game to anyone who isn't highly skilled at games, or you may find yourself stuck by a segment of the game which it does nothing to prepare you for in regard to the sudden pacing. Getting stuck is annoying, but getting stuck by part of the game that comes out of nowhere and is nothing like the rest of the game is just cheap.

review_text: I nominate this game Kingpin;  Let me tell you something about this game first…it was released in a time when PC gaming was faced with the likes of Unreal, Quake, Duke Nukem...I would beg borrow and steal to afford games, it usually resulted in me buying them, downloading a new .EXE file and taking the game back to game for a refund..…   but I will admit when I first encountered this game on a website that allowed one to steal without buying it first… (a bit like the torrents of today) it was one of the first games I actually paid money for and kept because It deserved it!  Because back in the day it was a benchmark in PC gaming. It was one of the first games to feature amazingly skinned graphics (especially using 2 voodoo 2 cards in SLI mode to get an impressive 1024x768 graphics!!), the story line was very interesting...it allowed the control of NPC body guards… the sort you could direct what to do and actually set them upon a junkie that needs a crowbar over the head…  The sounds and music are awesome, so awesome in fact the game got banned in many countries for its obscene profanity that it featured and I have to mention, the game has guns & blood / gore. everything a man needed all those years ago.  It was also one of the first; first person shooters i actually enjoying playing online, it was released about the time of unreal and I found this game so addictive I splurged out on a ISDN connection for a better ping… to get an edge over my American friends that all had t1 connections e.t.c. which was about a weeks wages in rental costs from British Telecom…   See games like unreal / quake and the likes never took my fancy because I have a problem being inside a sci fi story… but this game was different… it took me into the hood with homies and gangsters and all that stuff you see on SKY 1 cops..… it was an epic adventure… so epic I bought It again in 2016….  While its practically impossible to get a server full nowadays back in its day the servers where bouncing with hordes of players. With that i met a great community of players all those years ago… the game also got me to found a clan, I even learned html to code a website / forum for my henchies… (a time before you could go online an rent / buy a pre coded version)  it came with a gamespy client. We used to jump into unreal chat and give all those players a real hard slagging off for loving another crap sci fi game with no soul…  Unfortunately i have lost contact with all my old {THUGS NOW}. but who knows or remembers {ThUg Pioneer?} ... well if you do.... hit me up im sure you have all aged well!!  While I will admit playing the game now in 2016 doesn’t have the same feel of 'wow this game is impressive' with its awesome graphics… i do find it touches my heart and soul with all the memories the game gave me…   but I will say the story line and ingame control still rivals a lot of games today, and for the cost of a few beers its worthy of any game collection.  Long live Kingpin!

review_text: This game is not like most other current games out there. Honestly, while I was playing I was wondering if this was someone's very elaborate portfolio piece for use in applying for other game dev jobs. But then I realized that didn't much matter. I played through to the end and I enjoyed the story.   The game reminds me of old school text-only 'adventure' games on the PC. If you remember those, and if you liked them, then you might like this game. For those that don't remember those games: the game is an exploration game. You make your way through a mansion and slowly but surely work your way through the story. It is not difficult, it just takes time. I saw 'walking simulator' somewhere in the tags and I think that is about right. It is a story-driven walking simumlator. Could it have been better? Yes. They could have done a lot more to add to the puzzles or take advantage of the generally spooky environment. Is the game bad considering what it is? No.

review_text: Hidden 'On the Trail of the Ancients' has a lot going for it but it is hugely overpriced given its extremely short play time. Expect no more than 4 hours, even if you end up stuck on the puzzles for some time.  Without a doubt, I'd say that this is a great start to a point and click adventure. The voice acting and graphics are good quality, there are some interesting puzzles and the 'creepy' factor is well pitched. You see the beginnings of a well throught out supernatural horror story with enough detail to make you want to know more and well pitched tensio leading to some properly 'jump out of your seat moments'.  The problem is the game is over before you feel it has begun. The story is set up but hasn't developed at all. If this had been marketed as an episode 1 of a longer story and priced as such then I would not complain. But it is shorter than a single episode of many other games and at least twice the price.

review_text: Disclaimer: Steam botched up my hours played and says (as of this writing) I only have two hours. I timed myself. I played for 5. Not saying it makes a huge difference, but I'm stating it to credit my experience with the game so far.  I was a bit put off by this game at first. I was afraid it would end up being another one of those graphic novels in the guise of an actual game, however after reading a few existing reviews I bought it myself and was pleasantly surprised. This is a management game with actual gameplay, even if it is all revolved around being in the right menu at the right time and clicking words.  I also wasn't very impressed with the character designs. Having a girlfriend that draws constantly, I've learned to pick details out of designs, and there wasn't anything overall impressive with these. However, this is due to a combination of setting, and the fact that the characters you meet in the game are more than just their appearance.  Sunrider Academy takes place, as is obvious, in an academy! Your job as the player is to take the role of a club manager, and to help several misfit, down-on-their-luck clubs get their members count, morale, and skills up in order to prevent them from being discontinued. You also have to simultaneously keep your own skills up and complete your school work, as you are still a student. There's also this, arguably unnecessary, sub objective in which you need to get a girlfriend out of all of this, and since each club you help is being led by an adorable anime waifu, your not exactly short of girls to woo.  If you already are into these kind of games, you'll more than likely find yourself completely invested in both the characters and the gameplay in a matter of 30 minutes. They aren't the most original or well written characters either, but they are charming enough to keep you interested. There are some genuine moments in the story and dialouge, though most of my time was spent figuring out what the bloody ♥♥♥♥ I should do to keep my characters stress levels down!  My first two hours went by alright, as I helped get each club in order and even helped two of them win bronze and silver in competitions, but this was quickly lost in shambles as I neglected to find a consistent way of keeping my characters stress low. I tried playing games, sleeping early, buying eroge, but some of these actions cost money, which required a job, which adds more stress, which eventually spirals into you getting sick and then getting the 'Bad Ending' of you being hospitalized. I know it's mostly my fault, but the RNG didn't exactly make it easy for me. Just be warned, it's definetely a game of patience, which isn't necessarily a bad thing. However this did end in my dumbass forgetting to save for over an hour, and having to restart quite a bit of progress.  Overall, the art is nice to look at. There is no voiced dialouge, but the music is pleasant to listen to, and the gameplay can be addicting. Just don't try to take it too fast or you'll end up in a disaster like I did.

review_text: I have a mixed opinion about the game. While it can be a fun enough game, there's a fair amount of cons that ruin the fun. I do and don't recommend this game, and here's why :  Pros : -Guns don't completely overpower one another -The game keeps you going by having to unlock things -Most Co-Op missions are swapped out daily -Fair amount of upgrades for your character -Co-Op requires a good amount of teamwork  *This also means that the team has to consist of at least 3 of the 4 classes with a team of 3^, if you want to be successful -Easy-to-use controls -Actions are easy to do and are beneficial  Cons : -Co-Op isn't that great, gets boring after the first few co-op missions  *The game requires players to play Co-Op to unlock some new gear -Most weapons require real money to keep permanently, instead of 1, 7, or 30 days -PvP isn't very fun after a while due to various reasons:  *People dropshot too often  *Spawn camping is insane  *Medics serve little purpose in PvP since you can respawn right after death  *Maps are incredibly small, disabling a need for any tactical play  *Snipers are rendered useless on almost every map, due to the small map size -Co-Op gets to be a bit repetitive -Great weapons are cheap, allowing new players to get an amazing weapon  *This is both a pro and a con, but listed under con because it takes away some of the purpose of Co-Op -Medic is used more for close quarters rather than reviving teammates  *The medics are equipped with a shotgun, while other classes aren't  Overall, I would recommend this game to anybody who's played Fps's (First-Person-Shooters) such as CoD when it was good (Before MW3 in my opinion), Battlefield, and Rainbow Six. Don't expect the game to be amazing, for me it's more a game to play when I'm bored with nothing else to do.

review_text: If you are looking for a recent 'old-shcool' RPG like a Wizardry or a Might and Magic, this is proably it, in that I don't think there is anything else out there. Party Based, Turn-Based Combat from a first person perspective, this might be it. And I really want to like this game, as it is faithful to old-school while adding something new.   But I cannot recommend it, as it just takes itself too seriously.  It is hard, very hard, unecessarily hard. For some people this might really make it more appealing. But the game has really come down to only two things, have I saved recently and resource management. Everything is based on gold, every hitpoint you have really does boil down to how efficiently you can use it, and the cost to replace it. You can replace hps with food, with mana or at an inn, but all come with a cost. And every battle is hard. At least at level 6 they all are. There is no resting after every few battles, you need to rest after each fight. unconsious party members don't get exp for parts of battles after they go down. And the monsters progress in difficulty very fast. And there is no place to go grind easier monsters a bit and catch up, let alone get ahead.   With all of that said, I made it to level 6. I can make it further, every battle comes down to a very tough fight that a little RNG love going your way means victory, a quick save and move on. But that is not fun. Fighting a long hard battle out, and having it come down to a point where you are just barely going to win, only to have your soldier critically fail and kill himself, instead of the enemy is not fun. Having to start a battle over and over again that the game says is balanced fight is just not fun.

review_text: Fallout 3 is a post-nuclear 'role playing game'.  It is about fighting against the conditions of a harsh wasteland to survive two whole centuries after a nuclear war, while everyone in the far West is already done with that, and has moved on to deciding how Society is going to be rebuilt.  You can play the role of a good 19-year old who grew up in an underground Vault and goes to find his/her father, then ends up helping some generic White Knight faction seize a water filter from some generic evil Nazi faction. You can play the role of a morally neutral 19-year old who grew up in an underground Vault and goes to find his/her father, then ends up helping some generic White Knight faction seize a water filter from some generic evil Nazi faction. You can play the role of an evil 19-year old who grew up in an underground Vault and goes to find his/her father, then ends up helping some generic White Knight faction seize a water filter from some generic evil Nazi faction.  You can use your AMAZING powers of deduction to figure out that a man hosting a radio station that helps people to survive is doing so to 'fight the good fight'. You can use your AMAZING powers of wisdom to convince a robot president to kill himself after he argues with you and uses logical fallacies a  High Schooler  would be able to easily avoid using.  You have to make file tweaks and download bug fixes JUST to run the game on Windows 7 and later, otherwise It crashes when you start a new game. Hell, even then, after a while, it'll just start crashing randomly.  Get New Vegas instead. Yeah, it's buggy, but at least IT RUNS without .ini tweaks. At least it lets you make important choices that really influence the ending. You don't have to be a 19-year-old either.  It just doesn't work.

Summary: This game is almost perfect for TabletPCs!    This review is focused on usability on a Tablet PC. I have a Cintiq Companion, 2.4Ghz, 8GB RAM, Win10, with some hardware buttons set to modifier keys and a pen with buttons. The game can be controlled with pen only-very well, I didn't have to change the settings much.   Performance  Yes, the game runs and even on the lowest settings it looks nice. Loading times and finishing a round take some time though. The UI seems to lag slightly.    Controls  99% of the interactions are left and right mouseclicks. A pen is recommended - I didn't try to play it with touch only, mainly because the buttons on the GUI are rather small even in 'big UI mode'. There are very few operations where you need to hold the control key and click: multiple selections e.g. - these are no game breakers though.   I tried to pinch-zoom the map, without success. And I did not notice any zoom buttons in the GUI. This can be a minor annoyance since some 'jump to location' operations zoom all the way in. However once I set my favourite zoom level (With either onscreen keyboard or the Wacom onscreen controls), I barely changed it during play.  A bigger issue is that the user has to type in a name for customized units: I alt-tabbed to the onscreen keyboard (osk.exe), which is cumbersome but okay. One can probably use windowed mode + onscreen keyboard, too. An easy solution would be a random-name-generator preset or as button.   GUI  The Interface can be displayed in a big mode, there are still a few small buttons and especially the scrollbars are tiny.  Sometimes it is hard to mouse over an element precisely to read the tooltip popup - which usually pops up bottom right aka RIGHT UNDER MY PEN! While reading the popups/tooltips involves finger acrobatics, it's not a big issue. However people who use touch only will not be able to mouse over!  The whole graphics design of the UI is sleek and has a tablet-y feel. Bonus points for offering multiple controls for common actions e.g. Exit-button and Exit-by-right-click. I missed a GUI button to jump to the capital city though.   Gameplay  This game just screams to be played while relaxing on a couch or lying in the bed, also it's fun and addictive! (Refer to some other review, I'm not an expert on strategy games ;) ... )

If you are a fan of 'The Neverhood' or of any other of Douglas TenNapel's work, chances are that you already bought Armikrog and probably supported it on Kickstarter. I'm personally not a huge fan of Kickstarter projects, but I am a fan of TenNapel and of the intricate visual style that can only be provided by stop-motion. This was something I've been waiting on for a while.  Armikrog plays exactly like The Neverhood: A point and click adventure game with strange puzzles to solve in order to progress. Though similar in visual style and surrealness, Armikrog differentiates itself from The Neverhood by being more science fiction inspired. You are exploring a relative catastrophe, rather than a world created by a deity. Sections with the blind dog Beak-Beak, with the appropriate visuals for a blind dog, help Armikrog stand out from its forerunner.   The game is really good, but it is an acquired taste. The puzzles in this game do require a lot of memorization and outside of the box thinking. The hints provided are also very vague, often you can find yourself getting stuck with no clue what to do. You need to have patience for these kinds of puzzles. If you don’t, then this game probably isn't for you.  The game could still use a bit of a tune-up, but is otherwise ready to go. A good return for the Neverhood crew!

Remember that cartoon Street Sharks? Yeah? Well this game has nothing in common with that show other than they are both JAWSOME.  Bought it on sale and been playing nothing but it for the past few days. I can see it getting stale but currently I'm having a blast playing it. As both a diver and a shark which is surprising. I expected the shark to be the main attraction. Not so much the case. Both diver and shark are equally satisfying to play. This is more of a game where smart play, teamwork and patience are rewarded. While it sucks being thrust into an angry fishes' habitat, you can take solace in knowing you're not alone :D And as a shark you can make those trespassers pay for peeing in your home. It’s a win win.  In one of the games two modes, Blood and Gold, there’s a team of 4 divers and 2 sharks. Each team starts with 30 tickets (lives) in a shared pool. The divers are charged with finding gold and feeding it to S.T.E.V.E., the divers guide and robot pal. He slowly snakes his way from area to area opening chests. This serves simply as a timer for the level. Once S.T.E.V.E. exists the level, if the diver tickets have not run out yet, the divers win. On the other hand, the sharks must eat the divers before S.T.E.V.E. finishes his rounds.  There’s a ton of tools on either side you choose. For example, assault rifle for divers and regenerating health for sharks. The more powerful items/evolutions (what the upgrades for the sharks are called) are given to those that have leveled high enough. There’s plenty to choose from at level 1 of course. And some nice upper tier stuff to reward those who stick with it. Like being able to dart with prey in your mouth. That’s a great one.  In Depth's other game mode is Megalodon hunt. On spawn, one person randomly starts as the Megalodon and there are 4 other divers who need to hunt the Megalodon. The person who gets the killing blow on the Megalodon gets to become the Megalodon.... Megalodon. First to 15K points wins. Simple, straight forward. While it’s fun being one of the greatest apex predators on Earth, the main attraction for me is Blood and Gold. Shark vs Diver.  TL:DR - As the diver it’s a blast to outsmart the sharks on their home turf. As a shark it’s fun to eat people. -The game style rewards smart, patient, teamwork. Whether your diver or shark.  -It’s a more laid back (for me anyway) FPS than a lot of the other PvP FPSs I've played. This makes it great for hopping in and just doing a match or two. -You would think playing as a shark would be the 'funner' part of the game. Not so, I enjoy both sides equally. -I've only experienced one really badly lagged match out of 10 hours of play. I am State side and only play on those and European servers. I hear its best to stay away if you’re an Ausi or Kiwi. Barren and laggy servers. Shame our mates can’t enjoy it like we can. -Not sure if it’s quite worth $25 but definitely pick it up on sale for $20 or below.

review_text: Hey kids! Are you tired of playing challenging games like Barbie Horse Adventures? Are the tasks in video games simply too hard to comprehend? Wouldn't it be nice if a game would complete itself for once?  Well now, morons, there's hope! Join Tomb Raider's Lara Croft on her new adventure: Tomb Raider, the game where raiding tombs is only an optional task. Those tricky puzzles no longer require a know-it-all when you can downright skip them. But fear not, completonists! Should you take on these daring one minute puzzles, you will be fully equipped with a special vision that highlights exactly what to do. Still too stupid or blind for that? Lara Croft herself will speak out the answers for you.  Now you may be saying, 'Fatbeen, this sounds all so great, but I want action in my game!' I'm glad you said that, and please don't ever interrupt me again. Let's talk about the real guts of the game: the action sequences.  Current game developers just love putting in those enemies that take a bit more effort to defeat. Perhaps the enemy requires a special way to be taken down. This is not a problem in Tomb Raider. Not only is every enemy just a human with a weapon, but they are all taken out the same way as Old Yeller. Take that you mangy mutt! But what if you are a gun hating liberal? Tomb Raider is two steps ahead of you. Simply follow our never-ending quick-time events where the controls appear on the screen for you. But the action doesn't stop there! Even when you're no longer in the heat of combat you'll find the landscape frequently breaking apart around you. Don't worry about getting lost, the extremely linear stage designs will literally crumble into a perfect pathway for you each time and every time.  Remember that last time you watched a movie and were just itching to occasionally press a button? We got you, fam. Sit back and enjoy unskippable cutscenes with a quick-time event thrown in here and there. If you're caught up in a tense moment of the game, time will slow to a near halt so you can pick your controller back up, slide out your keyboard, or install beautiful, hand crafted wooden cabinets and still have time to press the required button. Yes, these extremely frequent cutscenes will basically play the game for you.  This game sucked.

review_text: Early Access Review

'Memory is not particularly linear - it is associative, repetitive, subjective and porous.' - Dana Spiotta   This whole little game that Richard Perrin was working on for 10 years is actually about memory, and how definitely subjective it is. Any event, any occurrence that we either live ourselves or solely witness, will be retold through a certain perception. There is no such a thing as the truth once an event is recalled from the memory. There is only our own experience, thus, any narrator is eventually an unreliable narrator.  Journal  is a perfect example of this notion.  The narrative centers a 5 day period in a nameless teenage girl's life when she's been trying to deal with both expected troubles of growing up, establishing a character, making choices, learning hard earned truths and living consequences; and a specific, singular disaster fundamentally too heavy for her young being to deal with. Through detached and off dialogue options, abhorrently selfish exclamations and numbing expressions, we take a walk.  Journal  is an interesting, yet eventually surreal and nettlesome experience.  The artwork is hand drawn, picking a thin balance between beautiful and creepy, and that many would basically define as being eerie. A melancholy, water color environment combined with slender, faceless characters do create our setting. The whole display screams detachment and an inability of adaptation with the world. A failure to emphasize is written all over the artwork. The music changes as our heroine change places within a side scrolling environment from 'feeling blue' to 'something's off' any minute. We are given short stories told with shadow plays between chapters to declare some bitter life lessons now and then, and complete a whole playthrough within 2 hours.  I do adore story driven or narrative games mostly. But defining  Journal  to be either story driven, or narrative would be an understatement and probably even a completely wrong definition. In this collection of personal expressions, the narrative is completely subjective with little to no room for diverse character progression. Contrary to player expectation with the choice options presented in game, our protagonist already displays a precognized set of actions. Her mentality, her actions and reactions are not actually alterable. Any and all events that we encounter within the game, are already lived, thus categorized and concluded within the mind of our heroine. We are solely there to witness her personal recognition of events rather than having a gameplay. She is a bitter, troubled, detached, numb child; stuck in an existential crisis.  Is this a bad thing for us to witness in a game? Quite possibly not. This game was one of the best examples of 'unreliable narrator' in any game I've seen before. Yet, it also comes with the direct obstacle of constantly misinforming the player, making him/her walk into dialogues with semi-truths, wrongly presented information and endless defense mechanisms, evading for any kind of truth to be discovered. Being trapped within the narration, you are doomed to make wrong choices inconsequentially. Thus, there is no immersion, no character sympathy or even empathy, no control over any outcome. You are forced to witness endless apathy directed through stereotyped declarations.  The game is most definitely a great art project, and managed to accomplish what it has come to aspire: an ode to memory, perception and illusion of understanding. Yet, I find myself failing to recommend the game in the end. It is not fun to play. It is impossible for one to actually emphasize with the setting. It all is fleeting, surreal and numbing... as it was probably intended to be so. I most certainly appreciate it, yet fail to recognize the beauty of this kind of existentialist numbness as gameplay. There is no hate, no love, no fear, no bravery... 'Nothing' wrapped in pretty colors. Pick it on sale if you'd like to take an unusual experience with the grand illusion of choice.  Please also check out Lady Storyteller's Curator page  here  - follow for regular updates on reviews for other games!

review_text: Okay I'm gonna try to keep this short.  This game had the potential to be good but as it is I only see it as wank material for weeaboos. But you can get wank material for free on the internet so this game didn't need it.  STORY: Very thin of a story to keep you going. No one really asks what's happening (especially the MC) aside Tsu's explanation of how some of them got there. That kinda blows 'cause this game gave me more questions than it did answers. Making the game longer to flesh out the world and a more engaging story could have helped.  CHARACTERS: This is probably my biggest gripe with this game. They came off as very flat/trope-y. With the game only taking me just over an hour to beat it explains why I don't care for these characters. They spend too much time doing fanservice for the faceless MC and not anough time to actually let us get to know them. I can't even really give a personality description for the 4 main girls because they came off as the same aside one or two teeny tiny things. Like I said in the STORY section, making the game longer and making more scenes where the girls aren't flashing butt and clevage could have made me like them.  MUSIC: Don't have much to say here, sometimes it came off as typical chinese/japanese music but some tracks stood out to me. Overall, not too bad.  ART: My 2nd biggest gripe. For the most part it looks really good (especially those backgrounds, I want to touch them), but the character sprites rub me the wrong way sometimes. With all the shiny bits on their bodies I wonder if they spend their mornings bathing in oil. I like the details on the sprites at least. I like it when Visual Novels have more detailed-looking characters. I have no comment on the CG's. I don't much care for wank material.    FINAL WORDS: Had the game been longer and the characters and world fleshed out along with it, I could have easily liked this Visual Novel. Maybe Nitro+Chiral and Cheritz has spoiled me, but they also show me that VN's can be amazing things. I suppose the words I could use are 'Incredibly Disappointing'. The climax was hardly even a thing, ending within seconds and leaving an empty feeling. Maybe Pyrite Heart will be better, but I hope there will be something better in the future.

review_text: Edit: (I played the neutral ending. Killed a dummy at the beginning thinking I was supposed to.)  Maybe I don't have a soul, maybe I just expected to much from all of the hype, but Undertale just didn't do for me what it did for others. Discussing with a friend, they had it all suddenly *click* about half way through the game and they fell in love with the story and its characters. On the other hand, I never had this experience.   Without trying to spoil it, the game plays loosely like a JRPG. You meet many characters along the way, and you'll notice a lot of other reviews praise the story and its character development. The characters are quirky and might resonate better with fans of Earthbound, but I found the weak jokes and humor they gave to detract from the more serious message the game is trying to share. In fact, I'd have labeled the character development and story as pretty shallow, so I'm surprised to see how amazing the feedback is regarding the game. The message is pretty in-your-face from the get-go, and you have a good idea where the story is going within a minute or two of starting.   With the choices it offers the player as well, the game plays fairly black-and-white in its judgement of the player. It iasks you to make choices throughout the game, but they never seem to matter as much as you'd hope. This ends up robbing the experience of any depth you'd expect. This was the other issue I ran into; maybe it's because I just played SOMA, another game steeped in story and character development focused on a deep existential concept, but the main message of this game seems tame and weakly presented, and if they characters dont resonate with you, it kind of fails to bring any deeper meaning to the game.  I wrote this review not to call this a bad game, but to let potential buyers know that not everyone derived an emotional and thoughtful experience from this game. It isn't the perfect game that the reviews seem to reflect. It will likely connect with some players, but I believe there are better games sharing the same messages.

It's always risky to try some radically new base mechanic in a platformer game, so I tend to hail anyone who attempts to do so. Enter Mushroom 11, where you can't control your character directly - all you can do is erase a part of it. What you delete will regrow in a few seconds, randomly across the bulk of the fungus. Result: if you persistently delete the right side, the entire bulk will move to the left. And basically that's all. For such an odd mechanic to work, the puzzle design needs to be ultra careful, and this is one area where the game shines - most of the tasks ahead of our hero are well thought-out, rarely very difficult, and the checkpoints are thankfully frequent. The other aspect worth mentioning is the incredible background art and atmosphere.  Mushroom 11 is not without faults, either. One is a direct consequence of the base mechanic - whenever you need to do something with accuracy, you need a lot of luck or a lot of patience. The latter can run out easily when you're shaping your fungus into something elaborate for the umpteenth time, and still end up eg. falling down. The other is more strange - at some points, the game seems to interfere with the physics, forcing a certain solution path. I met this only 2-3 times - one example is the cart ride in level 5 where I found it impossible to keep the fungus in the cart at a certain jump.  Based on the above, there's a little secret to enjoying Musroom 11 - take breaks, even frequent breaks, depending on your skill. There are not many very difficult puzzles in the game (levels 5-7 are more difficult than 1-4, but not dramatically so), but the randomness tends to wear you down after a while, and you'll feel the game is unfair while it's only doing its thing relentlessly. Quit and come back tomorrow, it will feel fresh again.  Untame has done a great job with Mushroom 11 - made a very brave move with the base mechanics, and designed an interesting and visually superb world around it. Definitely recommended.

With the absolute abhorrent lack of new horror titles this Fall/Halloween season, I was taken by surprise when I saw Cold Dreams appear on the Steam store. Equipped with a decent price and a promising trailer, I decided to give this one a spin on an overly windy, chilly evening.  First off, this game uses the Cryengine, so that is already a plus in my books. Far too many recent indie horror titles on Steam rely on the awful and notoriously unstable Unity engine. The graphics here are wonderful and the atmosphere is immersive; at one point I was actually getting cold whilst playing the game. However, disappointment soon arrived at my fingertips.  I realized very early on into Cold Dreams that nothing was actually going to 'hurt' me, and that there was no pressure to make storyline progression. You can stand around for hours looking up at the snowfall if you really want to. I came to the conclusion that this is yet another walking and key finding simulator, like so many other indie games that boast the tag of 'horror' have ended up becoming. The 'scares' are extremely predictable and provide nothing more than a little jump during the first couple of attempts. As well, the story is excruciatingly basic, with little to no inspiration.  I have to wonder, when and why did horror games become so standardized? The genre is becoming a lost art. Developers need to realize that true horror does not come from jump scares, walking around 'creepy' forests only to solve puzzles, or natural sounds. Anyone can create a jump scare, but it takes a true artist to instill long-lasting fright.  The game lasts maybe an hour. There is no save feature. At this time there are still bugs that are being worked out. Sadly, I cannot recommend this title whatsoever. The graphics are nice to look at and the game is very stable, despite random framerate dips into the 40's on a high end gaming rig; check my profile for specs. The 'horror' aspect of this horror title is just not there.   Rating:  2.5/5.0  - It's not awful, but it's not great.

review_text: This is really hard for me. This game has charm, fun references to famous classics, beautiful art, interesting gameplay ideas and it belongs to one of my favorite genres (Metroidvania). But for the life of me, I just can't recommend it. See the gameplay time I have on record? I can assure you, without any shadow of a doubt, that more than half of it has been spent losing and retrying. This game starts great, but at some point it becomes brutally difficult.  Now, being fair here, the basic portion of the game is easy enough. The game only becomes harder when you try to find extras and complete challenges. You might say I shouldn't complain then, but see... this is a Metroidvania. Its entire premise as a genre is to make exploration fun. And this game turns exploration into an exercise in maddening frustration. This should be the best part of the game, and instead it's so bad in comparison to the rest that you'd swear this game started as two different ones that at some point were mashed together for whatever unexplained reason.  See, the more you advance the more abilities you unlock. That's par for the course for the genre, and it's one of the most fun parts of these games. The problem is that, unlike for all the other games in the genre, all your abilities are available at the same time. That might seem like a good thing at first, except that now you're forced to remember an alarmingly large amount of special moves, because the game will force you to juggle many abilities at the same time to advance through many areas, which becomes even worse when you have to deal with Super Meat Boy -like scenery.  I mean, a game like Super Meat Boy is fun because while it offers a challenge it relies on the simplicity of the controls. Here you have to deal with the challenge while mastering an increasingly complex set of moves. This becomes frustrating really fast and it's exacerbated by the fact that the controls are not particularly responsive. Push Up and the game will do Left instead. That's not a problem in the middle of walking, but it's definitely a problem when you're trying to do a special move and the game does a different one instead, which will happen half of the time.  I can't help but think that most of the positive reviews come from people who don't try to explore in the game, or that never actually bothered to try and finish it. Or people that have lots of free time so they don't care how much they lose. Looking at the game's stats, I think it's quite telling that barely over 15% of the people have defeated the last boss. Clearly most people are giving up way earlier.  I have to be as fair as I can here. Yes, I had a lot of fun with the game, but the frustration has severely trumped it. That's why I can't recommend it, unless you're either unemployed with lots and lots of time to waste and nothing else to play.

So, what is so special about yet another zombie horde-mode killathon? Well, to be honest, I forgot I had even bought this game, and it sat in my library for the longest time. Then I played it, and it is a game that I cannot put down.  Individually, the game seems absurdly simply. Graphics are okay, nothing special, controls are simple, weapon choices, while fun at times, are rather linear, but this game takes individual factors that work okay in other games, and mashes them together, making them even better.  Imagine if you will a co-op style horde mode, similar to Left 4 Dead, complete with your basic and advanced hordes, add in class mechanics that improve the usage of certain weapons, but do not limit you in any way (A Sharpshooter is not limited to pistols and rifles, a Commando is not limited to assault rifles), plus some added strategy (Where you make your stand in each wave and where you fortify your defenses can often mean the difference between victory and defeat) and a pretty decent difficulty curve.  The only downside I can think of is that the game is explicitly made to be played multiplayer co-op. I'm no ace, but I'm rather good at FPSs, and I have trouble playing on Normal difficulty alone. I personally don't do matchmaking, because I dislike the idea of having to rely upon a bunch of strangers who I don't know to have some modicum of intelligence and as I don't have a headset, communication would be a problem, but I suspect if I'm able to play this game with some friends, it could be some of the most fun I've had in a while.  Summary Rating: 8/10  There's a lot here, and it's a really fun game, with a lot of different ways to play. You can even switch between roles mid game if you feel a flamethrower might do the job better than an assault rifle, or something like that. My only real problem doesn't have to do with the graphics or anything like that, but a few other gripes, primarily the linearity of gin upgrades in each category, and the multiplayer focused design. Having good multiplayer is outstanding, but making it near impossible to play alone on higher difficulties is kind of a low blow. Anyway, good game, definitely recommend, hope to play with other people in the future.

This game sets up a unique kind of ambiance. An unknown apocalypse. Monsters run rampant, Food is a commodity in high demand, and survivors band together to overcome the odds.  Yeah right. More like they bash your skull in three feet out of the safe zone for the f***ing fun of it. Hands down, the most unfriendly community I've ever seen. As a new spawn, walking out of the safezone for the first time, and coming face to what passes for a face with a Golem, and killing it. I was filled with anticipation. What else could this glorious magnum opus of survival bring?  Three minutes and two deaths later, I'm fighting a golem with the starting knife as two OBVIOUSLY veteran players look on, waiting for me to either triumph, so they could swoop in for the kill, or die (Which is what obviously occured) only to realize that I was completely un-equipped.  But that's not the fault of the game. That's the community.  No, the GAME is at fault with all the glaring other issues. Walking half way through the ground, through walls, getting stuck in corners, (I was stuck in one corner for ten minutes before I decided to die and respawn when I crawled clear, stood up, and snapped back to the corner, trapped like a rat.)  And if you encounter water? (A likely scenario, considering it takes up half the F**KING MAP.) You gett to have the joyus experience of either A: Struggling to reach land by looking up, mashing jump and attack, and vowing to avoind water all together, or B: Waiting to drown. If you're going to have that much water, maybe fixing the manner in which you traverse it should be a priority.  Many people compare this game to DayZ, which appals me. DayZ is superior in all aspects where it counts.  Points to DayZ --- Looting that makes sense. (Kinda my own opinion, but walking through a crap ton of buildings, only to find one glowing box that;s empty was a major frustration.)  Loot worth taking.  SPAWNING WITH A FLASHLIGHT. Seriously. What harm is it to give us a damned flashlight? Oh, it's supposed to be realistic? You won't always have a flashlight? I'm pretty sure you won't always have a useless knife either.  The community. It's diverse, It's interesting, and some people will give you a warning before they decide to repaint the local area with your bodily fluids.  The experience.   DayZ experience: 'I was walking along, and I was held up by a group of bandits, but when they saw I didn't have anything, they let me go, and even gave me food.' (Actually happened once.)  Nether Experience: 'Yeah. I was fighting a Nether. Then some guy shot me. I lost that canned food I had.' (Happened three separate times in my two hours of play time.)  Points to Nether --- Economy. I like buying things.  Decent character customization. Too bad you have to pay for anything good.  In game map. Bohemia, it does help.  Area names not in Russian. Kinda self-explanitory, really.  All in all, most people consider me rather practical when it comes to games, and I rarely get this outspoken when reviewing one. But when I pick up a game for three dollars, and I feel like I've been ripped off?  You've got your work cut out for you, Phosphor. Maybe I'll give this game another go in a few months.  But I doubt it.

review_text: Early Access Review

TL;DR: Read the Conclusion at the bottom of this review!  2265 A.D. - 3 Collective (meaning Slavers) alien races declare war onme. They have a combined strength of 15K-20K. Me, playing as the human United Nations, had overstretched. Too many planets, and a navy with only 2K strength to fend off 3 enemies with 10 times my military size. There was no way I could win, so I cede. I negotiate a peace treaty with 2 of the alien races and give them 2 of my planets  They then proceeded to enslave every human on the 2 planets I gave them. I had abandoned my own people to slavery on the promise of colonial riches. I actually felt bad for abandoning them :'(  But I also went into a fit of rage. I was still fighting 1 alien enslaving empire. Despite still being outnumbered, I fought a war of attrition, getting them to attack my space stations to decrease their strength, despite losing some of my space stations. My economy went into overdrive, every planet was churning out ships and I was running a government budget deficit.  2281 A.D. - After 16 years of war, I defeated the alien bug race with whom I had was still fighting against. I had smashed their military and economic strength and my allies, who initially didn't send fleets in, eventually sent them over and helped me defeat my enemy.  But the other 2 empires still had control over my 2 planets that I ceded to them. Worse yet, the humans they enslaved there were running out - THEY WERE FORBIDDEN FROM BREEDING :'(  There was still much work to be done.  2291 A.D. - After the 10 year ceasefire was over, I went to war again with the alien race I had smashed earlier. I won in 3 years, although I failed to successfully capture their capital planet, their homeworld, I still managed to take out the rest of their planets and establish a Vassal State which eventually integrated into my empire.  Initially, I purged some of the aliens living in the vassal state that I made once they integrated, but my people didn't like that since they were very individualistic and as such, I went back on my purge policy. Purging is a combination of ethnic/species cleansing and genocide.  But the 2 other empires still had my 2 planets where there were still a few humans left.  2309 A.D. - A few years before, the 2 empires that still had the 2 planets with humans enslaved in them had gone to war against another, powerful empire. This was good news, all I had to do was wait. Eventually their military would wind down, they would become weak, and then I could strike, reclaim the 2 lost planets with whatever humans were still left there after over 5 decades of slavery :(  in 2309, the time came. I negotiated help from a different empire that I had good relations with. My plan was, I can't go after both the empires on my own, but if I can keep one empire distracted by fighting a war with my ally, I can focus on the other one.  2325 A.D. - The plan worked. After another 15 year war, humans were once again united everywhere. It had been almost 3/4ths of a century since those planets were lost in a peace settlement which saw them abandoned, so I decided to give them their own independent state, but I also sent them a guaranteed monthly economic aid of minerals and energy credits to help them themselves back up on their feet. It comprised almost 25% of monthly government profit. Within 2 weeks they asked to become a vassal so they could be protected properly. Soon they will be re-integrated back with the rest of humanity.  So what happens next? I don't know, but I now enjoy having the most powerful military in my region of the galaxy, and a healthy economy capable of growing my military into twice or even 3 times the size it currently is.   CONCLUSION: This game gives you amazing stories. Let your imagination go wild and run free, it's the best way to enjoy this magnificent work of artistry that Paradox has produced =)  I give it a solid 8.6/10.

I played this game when it first came out on a friends account and just picked it up for myself on sale. Early Access Review

They took the 'TOMB' out of Tomb Raider & made an Uncharted clone.  All the 'tombs' take about 30 seconds to complete & the reward for doing them is stupid. There are collectables throughout the game that don't reward you for anything. There's a multiplayer mode that NO ONE CARES ABOUT! Because 'Multiplayer is what people want these days!' just NOT in their Single Player games!  The dumbest part in the entire game is when you have to save your friends by exploding them to safety. I kid you not. There is a segment where your friends are in a rickety shambled cage over a pit of lava & you save them by EXPLODING THEM TO SAFETY!  How the... I just... I can't even...  The shooting mechanics are good at least. Aiming is very precise with a controller or m/kb. The downside is that's all the game is giving you. A 3rd person cover shooter that holds your hand the entire way. There are sneaking segments but you don't even sneak yourself. The game puts you in auto sneak mode whenever you enter a sneak situation. It also auto puts you behind cover when available. It just feels really hand holdy like they think people are too stupid to use cover in a cover shooter game.   As you get toward the end of the game it just becomes a generic horde mode cover shooter w/ a lot of tough enemies in armor that require headshots to defeat. Luckily, headshots in this game are incredibly easy to pull off. The only time in the game that really gave me a hard time was the very last part where you're basically in a giant wind storm slowly moving from cover to cover while snipers shoot at you & armored enemies charge you, but since the game is really hand holdy dying doesn't really even matter. I played the game on hard & it was still fairly easy.   It was kind of disappointing after I beat it because you get a bunch of upgrades throughout the game & I was hoping to use them in a NG+ mode, but they didn't bother putting one in. You can keep playing the game after you beat it & gather collectables you missed but there's really no point unless you're addicted to achievement hunting.  There are some great cinematic sequences that are exactly what you will find in an Uncharted game. Suspenseful & over the top cliff hanger moments that will get your heart pounding & they are fantastic. They just aren't Tomb Raider, it's Uncharted. Everything about this game screams, 'WE WISH OUR GAME WAS UNCHARTED!'   The multiplayer mode I can't comment on because I didn't play it. I doubt anyone else did either. No one asked for it to begin with.   Overall, the game isn't bad but it just isn't Tomb Raider. It's Uncharted featuring Lara Croft. I get that this is supposed to be a reboot for the franchise & that Lara is supposed to be inexperienced & getting her gutsy sea legs but come on. If all you can do with a game series that is based on having puzzles, platforming, tombs & monsters is to take out all those things & turn it into a generic 3rd person cover shooter then how bout you just MAKE A NEW FRANCHISE instead of butchering the Tomb Raider one.   The only other positive thing I can say about this game is that now we have an Uncharted game for PC. That's the only reason why I'm recommending it. If you want an actual Tomb Raider game with puzzles, platforming, & TOMBS! Then you should grab the Tomb Raider Anniversary or Legends games.

review_text: All very well put together board-game conversion. Control wise, the game stands head and shoulders above other current turn-based tactical games. It has a very clear event (dice) log and explains itself very well (except for exact line-of-sight rule). In addition to that, it has crisp controls including an undo button and decent pathing. The game (despite the slow and cumbersome nature of your troops) does not in any way waste your time. Enemy turns are lightning-fast, cutscenes can be completely turned off, animations sped up to ludicrous levels and you can command other units, while one is moving, something that I have been dearly missing from all turn-based games I have been playing recently.  Gameplay wise, it is Space Hulk. Your team may very well die because you rolled too many doubles (both attack die showing the same number) in a turn. Overwatch is king. Assault cannons go boom. And genestealers always win in melee.

review_text: This review is for Witcher I only of course  ( I cannot compare to Witcher II or III )  PROS * RPG quest with a lot of investigation ; * CHOICES : though I was not impressed by the claimed consequences or the depth of the choices, most RPG games oddly enough to not offer choices at all, so this is a good thing as such.  * The interactions and stories with some characters during your investigation are interesting.  * CHARACTER : the skills or powers tree and how to level is fun and intriguing (you cannot really customize your visual though, not even with armors). * USER INTERFACE : quite fine, easy to use, you can pause the game anytime. * The immersion and world graphics are nice. * Does not require a graphics card : I can play it even on the Intel integrated graphics.  * I got it for about 1 $.  NEUTRAL * TIME : there is a decent amount of quests that can be time consuming, of the type 'Fetch 5 samples of Ghoul blood', 'Eradicate x creatures in that swamp', etc. It is the Witcher concept to hunt monsters, but some quests are the classical 'farm creatures for money (Orens)'. If you like that, good, but I felt like I was wasting real-life time. BUT you can ignore many, though a few are part of the main quest. * TACTICS to defeat some monsters : some creatures require tactics or being careful. The downside is you will die quickly to learn that the hard way (do many save games).  CONS * COMBAT : it is horrible, counter intuitive and repetitious. How it works : you must NOT move at all during your attack. You can only do movements or side steps between attacks, otherwise your attack is broken! You must not click during an attack, otherwise that breaks your attack. You click in timed sequences. The sequence varies based on three stances: Strong, Fast or Group. Luckily, you gain magic attacks during the game, which makes it a lot more fun.   * NOT open world at all : paths are set-up. You cannot jump nor walk over anything one foot high and must follow the pathing. May I say INVISIBLE paths in a swamp were annoying and confusing (like in a maze you can't see) ?  * CUT SCENES WITHOUT ART : while the in-game animations and immersion are fine, most cut scenes simply use limited emotes/in-game animations, so they are not convincing, do not look dramatic or cinematic. I am really not asking for a lot: a bit of blurry effects with some better animations would have done the trick. A few cut scenes use paintings/art and these are actually better for ambiance.  * NPCs with the same visuals : really !? In an Enhanced Edition, is it too much to expect that merchants do not all look the same, that big thugs not all have exactly the same visuals, etc.?  * The sexual, romantic or erotic moments felt irrelevant to the main storyline and out of context. They are like candy without much meaning.  * Useless weapons : you can carry a whole array of useless weapons that specifically cannot be used with your Witcher powers. Using those for fun will probably get you killed. Also, if you add a Rune or try to modify a sword, it is replaced completely: hence destroying your former sword and all its qualities (I read the Wiki before making any mistakes).   Witcher 1 does still stand out (is original on some aspects), but not enough to make me feel like spending over 50 extra hours is worth it.  Note : I usually leave positive reviews for games, but this improved version has disappointed me too much. I am doing Chapter 3.

review_text: Okay, The 'Dead Synchronicity' was a kind of an interesting game. It was a new genre for me, but I ended up liking it at some points. I can see today that this is not my kind of game, but I understand too now, why some people have a love for these kind of point-and-click indie games.   The game itself actually had a very deep story, even though the game wasn't that long. The puzzles were great but some times a little bit too hard. review_text: First off, don't get me wrong, this game is good, and has some good moments, its fun, the combat is great, the story is OK and funny in places, so what is the problem? why a negitive review?  if you go here,   http://steamcommunity.com/app/405900/discussions/4/412448792361194752/#p34   Kaldaien who is a GL expert, has been making a mod that hopefully will fix some of the issues that the game has, however, the more he looks, the bleaker things get. there are some many things wrong with this port, and this is why I don't recomend the game in its current state. While I have pumped over 100 hours into the game, but I'm coming to the point where I will need to be going into item world, and doing some serious grinding, and this is where the port falls to pieces.   even though there have been several patches, they have fixed / broke / fixed / broke and not fixed some issues, the not fixed is the core crashing issue that still plagues this game. And to be clear, IF you just play the game for the story, not interested in doing long runs in the item world to get the best gear, or power level to highest level, then this game is worth it.   But if you want to collect stuff, power level, and play for long perios, then I would hold off getting this game, its not stable, (once you reach CoO and play the item world for long periods) then this game can't be recomended in its cuurrent state. As much I as enjoy playing it, the game is unstalbe, what is wrose, its down to the game code, how the game is bottlenecking stuff at a driver level, between the CPU and GPU, plus is using bad graphics handling that should never have been used to start with. and really bad resource mangagement. which pretty much sums up that this port has been poorly coded from the ground up.   We the community are at the mercy of the devs, and IF they decide to fix this mess, but from what Kaldaien has found out, I suspect the only way to fix this mess, is probably to do a rewrite of the code. and I'm not sure that will happen.  but if this is how NIS will release games onto steam, then sadly, I can see other games falling into the same mess as Disgea.   Again, I love this game, only found out about it when it came onto steam, its a great game, but saldy, this port isn't, and for that reason, I would stay clear of this mess until the devs fix it. If the devs do fix this, I will change this review, but I do feel people have a right to know about the quality of this so called port.   one last thing, because of the issues, I have two theories on them, either this game wasn't remotely tested in any degree at all, consdering it really doesn't take much effect to trigger this crash bug, plus I not mentioned the second crash bug, that is when you try and save the game, probably linked to the above, but it triggers if you not saved for a long period, so after a long spell in item world, the game might crash. so in all honesty, this bug should have surfaced for anyone who has played the game before release, and it should have been fixed before release. but this leads to my more concerning theory, NIS knew about the crashing and didn't bother to fix it, because of the time it would take too fix it.   so which of those applies? I don't know, but I would say this, if this is how disgea gets treated on steam, shoddy coding and issues that should have been dealt with before release, will other games wind up the same fate?   I guess we will have to wait and see, and hope that the devs will fix this mess.   so if you want to endure crashing after so long, sure get the game, otherwise at this point in time, stay well clear of this shoddy port.

review_text: getting real sick of rift

Bottom line, l would only recommend this game if you have good friends to play with and can deal with the stuff l'll mention. At first this was negative because of a big combination of things Starbreeze/Overkill think are good ideas but really...aren't. But now, l realize that it's that plus the challenges of playing public (which l never did).  This 'review' details issues with the game l run into, but ultimately ignore because it works fine enough for me. l still play this game a lot with friends, but the following should be known before someone makes a purchase:   - Gameplay is solid on its own, but is greatly enhanced by mods such as HoxHUD and PocoHUD. Without a mod, you have no form of communication outside of text chat and mic, and there's a lot of F-key holding without mods. And there's a lot the ingame characters have to say, too.  - The gameplay itself is either stealth or full combat. It tends to lean on combat more, though. The combat itself focusses on holding out somewhere and carrying bags to the escape van. Basically.  - Game was made for cooperation. I would never touch public lobbies in a game so centered in cooperation, though. You'll want to bring your own friends, do private lobbies, etc. It's much better with a solid crew.  - Has some pretty bad online desync in regards to stealth and tossable loot bags. It's the source of much frustration in stealth jobs, which is a shame because the stealth is generally, mostly, somewhat forgiving otherwise.  - Singleplayer bots suck. They're just meatshields that might revive you if they remember to kill the Special enemy that caused you to go down. There's a mod for Better Bots,  but l never tried it in the first place . I tried Better Bots and it works great. You'll want it if you're planning on playing solo with AI.  - Weapons were rebalanced to offer a wider variety of choices. You'll have to try things out to find what works, since there's not really a Jack-of-All-Trades rifle anymore (there kinda is, but not really).  - You can't shoot people in the legs or arms. It's literally all body shots and head shots. Y'know, because a PC game can't handle that. What? N64 games could do it?  - Microtransactions are butts in a paid game that also needs paid DLC to use grenades.  Said microtransactions are being arbitrarily limited to make them more expensive in the future community market. That rubs me the wrong way...  Hold on, they added two kinds of free grenades. They also made the microtransactions free, unless you want to buy a specific skin on the Steam Market.   I end up using about 2Gb of RAM on top of integrated graphics. It runs 18-25 FPS for me ingame, but thanks to that GPU Flush option and 30 FPS cap, it doesn't hurt to play. One or two maps are optimized poorly and render more than they need to, but that's never actually hurt my combat, either. Actually, update... Two new maps added are polygon-happy and they're combat areas. But, l'm still confused how the main menu screens only reach 28 FPS...   Again, l'd only recommend this game if you have good friends to play with.

review_text: This game is great for anyone who loves RPGs and FPSes at the same time. I haven't really played this that much because I liked the first game much, much more due to the classes and the overall gameplay feel of it but this game is pretty good. The biggest dissapointment for me was the classes. I didn't have any class that really appealed to me in this game, they all felt really lame so I went with the Siren in the end cause her ability is pretty good. In Borderlands it was pretty straight-forward. You had Brick, the tank with the heavy weapons, the Soldier who could either be a healer or a supporter, Mordecai, the sniper who could take down enemies in one single shot but had a slow fire rate, and Lily, the quick crowd controller. Here, there's nothing like that. There's a fat, midget mexican who I don't even know what he does. There's a soldier who I think is pretty much the same as in the first game. Then there's Zero who every single one who plays this game wants to be because he's so 'cool' and then there's the Siren who I think is also pretty much the same as in the last game. The best thing about the game is pretty much the replayability and the customization. Unlike in Borderlands where all you could do was change your 3 colors, here you can choose heads and different color schemes and stuff. I haven't played this game enough to actually say if you should get it but I might pick it up and play a little when I think about it. If you liked Borderlands one, really loved it, then you'll most likely love this one aswell even though in my opinion it's not as good and cool.

review_text: Early Access Review

Going into the game, I was extremely hesitant; especially pre-ordering. (If I do that, I generally wait until the last minute because then other people can tell me if it's worth it before I place my order.)  I took the risk, as some had mentioned it wasn't a broken mess on PC.  Well worth it. I have  EVERY. SINGLE. OPTION.  maxed out, and I'm still getting 60 fps...on 3 screens.  also, AMAZING SUPPORT FOR EYEFINITY 'out of the box'.  That said, I did download AMD Catalyst 14.11.2 beta drivers (they net a 50% performance increase in Far Cry 4).  Currently, i'm running the game with; AMD Phenom II X6 1090T OC'd to 4.0ghz. 16 Gb RAM Two R9 280x at 6 Gb VRAM (combined) 240Gb SSD (RAID 0) + 1 TB HDD  Display: 5760x1080p (3k) resolution  Maybe it's because of the patch that i'm seeing such amazing gains, and after the beta driver update, but still, 60 fps at @ 3k res. I even booted up Far Cry 3 a month ago, and I was still getting stutter and 30 fps in most places with everything maxed.
