This is Dungeon Defenders 1: Complete Edition. Without hacking, with some extra content. As such it gets my full endorsement. As someone who came to the party late, there was no point in playing public multiplayer due to the hacking. This game finally allows me to play the original as it was meant to be played.  Despite early reviews, this is not:  1) A mobile port (this is a complete misunderstanding of the cross platform service launching later) 2) Full of microtransactions. The only thing you can (currently) buy are useless Emotes for $1 - all skins and dyes are earned as usual, as are all weapons, armor, etc. 3) Dumbed down or DLC deficient - the gameplay is identical, and the UI is largey the same or slightly streamlined 4) Lacking single player or private matches (both were added with a day 2 patch)  They did add a uPlay-like launcher to the game that might annoy some, but I found it to be seamless. They need the overlay to offer cross playform play later on, which steam cannot do.  Drawbacks: - You cannot import your characters. This is to prevent item hacks from exploiting this game also. - They added too much bloom. Hopefully a patch will allow you to turn it off.

review_text: Early Access Review
Early Access Review
Early Access Review

[I backed this, just so you know.]  It is very tempting to lump this into the 'walking simulator' category. That's pretty much all you do most of the time - you're going through (largely linear) corridors and taking in the scenery. There are no puzzles. There's no inventory. It's short. The player character is slow as a snail. There's little interaction with the environment.  But, there are threats and obstacles to traverse. There are branching paths and endings that range from obvious to very subtle. In fact, much of the changes in the environment (and thus the story it tells) is incredibly subtle and easy to miss. The mechanics present in each chapter are minimalistic (it's always about simply moving, really) - it is clear the entire game has been deliberately designed to have the bare minimum of interaction, to focus on the atmosphere and unraveling of the plot and setting.  This is a double-edged sword. On one hand, you don't have interesting (expanded) mechanics to keep the player engaged. On the other, the game builds tension and dread almost perfectly because there is no clutter in-between the player and the spooky. Every time the player thinks the game is starting to get predictable, it throws a curveball. There are some really well done moments (no, not jumpscares, even though there are a few of those as well) that could easily fit in a Clock Tower or Silent Hill game. It would be a shame to miss those.  The (objectively!) best part of the game is the sound design - it is absolutely necessary to play with headphones to get the full experience. There's plenty of dynamic changes to the BGM along with superb sound effects, even completely fine voice acting too. The credits song is practically a Yamaoka homage. Props to McGlothlin.  The artstyle is also worth a mention. Everything is in black and white, except blood and interactive objects that are shaded in different colours. It feels like a cross between a child's scribbles and a newspaper caricature. There is a huge amount of detail put into scenery, and there is one nightmare that makes very good use of this style to keep you on edge. There's a surprising amount of gore and uncomfortable imagery too, making it pop out even more.  All things said and done, if you like horror in general, you'll probably like this too. If you enjoy survival horror though, your mileage may vary. It's worth the couple of hours, especially if on sale.

review_text: I really enjoy this game. Each character has something special about them, so no character is the same. The playstyle of each character is different, and the multiple character creation helps if you get bored of a certain character. The story isn't the greatest, but the later dungeons are enjoyable and fun to do, especially with friends.  I would like to warn you that if you are new, the game does appear very pay to win. And to all of the people saying you HAVE to pay to actually do good in the game, you're wrong. You don't NEED more inventory space, a pet, a good costume, a good weapon, and etc. As long as you have armor you can do perfectly fine in this game. I'll repeat it again, you don't have to spend a single penny on this game. Just enjoy the game!  One of the few problems with this game is PVP. The community is very, very toxic in PVP. If you join a match you're either going to have someone who is lagging, someone who is hacking, a toxic opponent, a toxic team mate, an AFK, someone who refuses to fight you, someone who is deranking, and so many others.   My final problem with this game is that some characters are unbalanced. You can have a balanced character vs another balanced character in PVP, but then the class change will automatically make a certain character win. It gets really frustrating, honestly. No matter how good you are with a certain character with a certain class, there will always be another class out there to ♥♥♥♥ you over.  Do I recommend this game to new players? Honestly, give it a shot. You can either love it or hate it. If you do play it though, try and get a pal to play it with you. It's a lot more fun to play Elsword with another person.  Thanks for reading my review, and have a nice day!

While I haven't played a super high amount of hours on this game, I am a huge fan of the Total War series. After the travesty that was the launch of Rome II, I was a little burned but decided to buy this game as I'm an even bigger fan of the Warhammer series. From what I've played so far, I have really enjoyed the game, but their policy on DLC is utterly disgusting. £40 for the game, £2 just to add BLOOD to it, in a WAR GAME, £6 more to add a race that is ALREADY IN THE GAME... but that isn't too bad, right? Those aren't too much. It costs more than that just to buy a few of the miniatures, right..?  £14 for beastmen.   Nearly three times the price of the first race for their first significant dlc. As much as I like this game, as much as i love BOTH series, Total War is dying. I recently got into the Dark Souls games. I put about 80 hours into Dark Souls: Prepare to Die Edition and absolutely fell in love with the challenging but fair combat.  I decided that Dark Souls 2 would have to be everything I loved but more, right?  Wrong.  Dark Souls 2 suffers from this idea that people liked Dark Souls because it was frustrating. So it seems as though every idea that the developers came up with to make the game more difficult was added without ever thinking about whether the player would enjoy it.  For example, when you die, you go Hollow, just like in DS1. The main difference though is that you are penalized with lower maximum health for every death until you reset that counter by using a Humanity (which are much more rare in DS2 than in DS1). This discourages experimentation and makes those frustrating deaths from geometry or traps absolutely infuriating.   Another example: when you are given your Estus Flask, you start off with only a single charge. To get additional charges, you have to find Estus Flask Shards, which each give you one more charge out of the Flask. You can find other consumable health items called Lifegems, but they do not refill at Bonfires like Estus. This discourages you from using them unless you absolutely have to.  I have other complaints about the game which include the very design of the levels and enemies as well as the pacing, but I believe what I've written so far should be more than enough to discourage anyone considering purchasing this from doing so.  I'm excited for Dark Souls 3 to come out and fix all the crap that From Software did in DS2.

Do you love :  *board games?  *fantasy settings?  *roleplaying Games?  If you answered 'yes' to more than one of the above, go ahead and buy this game while it's on sale. You'll enjoy yourself thoroughly. To everyone else, I must say...   Talisman is a hard game to recommend. My partner and I enjoy playing and we laugh hard at the antics which play out over the board, but there are still way too many things not working as intended, too many bugs, too many instances where the user interface doesn't do what you may want it to do.  They're charging a lot of money for content that isn't even created yet, and the stuff that is already there often does not work as advertised. I've had a lot of fun with this game, but as yet it still acts way too much like a beta version for Nomad to be charging full price.  I can recommend this game only to hardcore fans of the genre like myself. For everyone else, I'd have to say... wait for them to finish the game and maybe get it on sale at a deep discount.

I ended up quickload-dragging myself through this on easy and with cheats, because I found it impossible to block enemy shots with the disc block maneuver (right-clicking through a weird lag). The sound, visual animation, and actual effective timing of the block motion seemed to be completely independent of each other, making this essential combat maneuver impossible to pull off. Was the original Windows installable release like this?  I'm recommending this game primarily as a wonderful color-burst of nostalgia. And godspeed to anyone who can pull off the disc block. Maybe you can actually play through the game as the developers intended.  And also the awesome computer puns. Hearing the other light cycle pilots insult Jet's program-worthiness was priceless. Much better than the forced-chuckle computer puns in Jazzpunk.

review_text: Early Access Review

DCS has a steep learning curve, however the large international community behind it fills the void with tutorials, campaigns guides. Eagle Dynamics have continued to lead the way in Flight Simulators in the void left behind by Microsoft and Jane's, with DCS World providing different Planes, Helicopters and Strategy modules through affordable DLC with Campaign and Mission Editors to prolong gameplay.  While the Visuals are impressive and the controls are reasonable quick to grasp, the game is very unforgiving. Many hours need to be put in to master combat flight, as quite often new players to Eagle Dynamics' games will be destroyed by the AI before they can even see them. This can lead players to become frustrated, so I do recommend those who wish to play this game watch some tutorials and practice against Harmless targets before trying against Hostiles.  I do recommend this game, but I have to admit I got very frustrated with Air to Air combat, so Air to Ground will prove less challenging for newcomers.  6.5/10

Background: Picked up during the summer sale for pennies. Apparently it's an upgrade of a 2008 DS game with improved visuals and audio, part 2 was never released but you can complete the story on youtube via officially released cinematics.  The Good: * Great story and plotting that mix things up * Characters and voice acting are fantastic * Self-aware quips make the characters stand-out * Fun mix of gameplay styles show depth right from the start  The Bad: * Looks dated, the pixelated cut-scenes especially * Awkward controls for interacting with characters but you get used to it * Mismatched lipsync is distracting * Combat is simplistic and weapons lack impact * Half a game that stops just as things are moving  Impressions: Looks dated with low-rez cutscenes, awkward controls and mismatches mouth to voice work.. but in the first 10 minutes I was impressed by the story-telling, characters and gameplay. Reminds me of the first time i played Sam & Max.  Recommended for: Noire detective fans who don't mind some run n gun n puns.  Conclusion. 7/10. Shines through its limitations to be memorable and a lot of fun.

I'm a big sci-fi fan and I've been stung on a few 4x space rts titles in the past, so I was really excited when I saw Paradox launch one. Big Paradox fan since Mount & Blade.  On the upside, the game looks good and runs well. When I combat opponents, I can feel the push and pull between me and the game as it tries to find weaknesses in my strategies. The race customization options, both at the start and during the course of play, are fun. Overall, the title is promising.  On the downside, I've played through seven different games over 200 hours of gameplay and all but the last came to a statemate. I develop my empire's military strength and manage diplomatic relations, and each time my npc allies waffle right before my npc enemies zerg. In this last campaign, I managed to fly under the radar of all the other pc races, built up my empire strength and technology until I'm just about to peek out into the universe... then the game suddenly decides that my empire is due for a revolt that instantly spawns invading troups on every one of almost a dozen worlds and a fleet three times everything I've got. It was like the game saw I was doing well and spontaneously created an unwinable game event.  Between the fun I had when I was doing well and the disappointment I felt for seemingly impossible odds, my Stellaris experience feels like a wash. I wish I spent those 200 hours playing a game that was more fun.

review_text: Early Access Review

It's only been a couple of months and 2010 is already shaping up to be one of the best ever for gamers. If your wallet hasn't already been emptied, Electronic Arts and development studio DICE have tossed yet another videogame on the pile that can't be missed. Battlefield: Bad Company 2 ups the intensity and visual prowess of its predecessor, while still delivering one of the most compelling multiplayer games around.  If you were to buy Bad Company 2 solely for the single-player campaign, you might come away a bit disappointed. That's not to say it's particularly bad in any way, but it doesn't feel impressive enough to stand on its own as a great experience. Once again, the multiplayer game is the star of the Battlefield show.   Watch the video review. The single-player campaign follows the story of a rag-tag bunch of soldiers as they traipse around the world on the hunt for a mythical weapon of mass destruction which absolutely must not fall into the hands of the Russians. It's a typical story of unlikely heroes as they attempt to save the world, and it will take you across a great variety of locations that range from frozen mountains to densely packed jungles.  The detail of these gorgeous locales is the first thing that will spring out at you as you begin the fight. The vistas and skyboxes look nearly photorealistic in many situations, and DICE did a wonderful job blending the particle effects and game objects in the foreground with the more static backdrops. The result is a sense of depth that few videogame worlds can offer.  Things become more impressive yet when the action kicks in. Returning from the last Bad Company game are nearly fully destructible environments. If an enemy soldier is holed up in a second-story bedroom taking potshots at you, all you have to do is send a rocket at that wall and he'll either wind up dead or fully exposed. The same line of thinking applies to just about anything you see – send enough firepower at it and you can watch it crumble.  Once you're done ogling the smoke trails or mountain ranges in the distance, you'll start to notice that Bad Company 2 has taken a few cues from the Call of Duty franchise. The last Battlefield game was the first to introduce a fully fleshed out storyline and it stumbled a bit in the process. The humor was goofy and over the top, the open mission design was a bit too open, and everything seemed coated in a dense fog. A lot has changed and improved for the sequel.   The backdrops are breathtaking. The big change comes with a more streamlined and cinematic approach to the action. The dialogue is less overtly inane, though it does offer its fair share of humor, and the level design feels more straightforward. While the last Bad Company game couldn't hold my attention, this one kept me interested and having fun from start to finish.  It shouldn't come as any surprise that this Battlefield game has some great mechanics. The guns react well, and sound fantastic. The vehicles handle smoothly and really do a great job of making you feel like the king of the battlefield. The instant-respawns and med kit injections of the last Bad Company have been replaced by the more standard checkpoints and regenerative health bar and that makes the challenge of war feel more realistic.  To top things off, the AI squad mates at your side act like real soldiers in battle. They'll press the attack while you flank and hold off the enemy while you duck behind cover to recuperate. Many games slap you in a squad of largely ineffective soldiers and let you do all of the heavy lifting. Bad Company 2 is a refreshing change of pace in this regard.  Of course, if you want to start nitpicking, there are plenty of instances to call out. Some of the details and little pieces of the environment stream into view a bit late. There still are no arms drawn on screen when driving a vehicle, causing a poltergeist-like steering wheel to move on its own. On the Xbox 360 version, slow loading from the disc causes the player to be locked out for as much as five seconds from throwing grenades or using the knife when picking up a new weapon or changing kits (this issue went away when installing the game onto a hard drive).

review_text: Very good game. Although the videos made it look like assassin's creed, never once during the game did I feel like it was a copy of AC. The wraith powers completely change physics in the game, making jumping off high places and teleporting a reality in shadows of mordor; something you simply can't do in AC. The only thing that I wasn't fond of was the shortness of the story line. Without DLC there are only 2 areas in Mordor you are allowed to play in. Also I really wish the would've expanded on the story line. I felt like I beat the game fairly fast. Now all I have left to do are the side missions involving re-forging the three weapons. All in All i was pleased with the game. I hope they come out with some major DLC in order to keep the story going, otherwise this game will get old fairly quickly.

review_text: If this game was a tune. It would be a BIG TUNE!!!

Not as fun as I thought it would be. So many bugs/problems despite the long time difference between launch and the day I bought it. They really didn't bother to patch up their game. I likened EA and Bioware's Mass Effect 2 marketing as a Renegade move that has earned them a bad reputation with me. In future, I will consider Bioware/EA as trying to pass off junk onto me.  PROS 1. Marketing is good Bioware really knows how to let everyone know their products are out there. This is their greatest strength.  2. Characters I liked Jack and Legion. They were new and fresh with interesting backstory. The others like Garrus, Grunt and Taylor were predictable.  3. Heavy Weapons Different. From missile/grenade launchers, to ice launchers, to death rays, to particle accelerators. E.g. M-920 Cain is very interesting, you just erase the enemy in a single railgun shot.  4. Lore Somewhat interesting. Mass Effect 1 had a lot of background on the Volus, the Hannar and all the other non-anthropod aliens. For some strange reason, every alien that serves on the Normandy just seems to be a human with skin disease.  CONS 1. Bugs, bugs, bugs a. Using Incinerate while using a sniper rifle auto-zooms when you cast b. There are locations you can walk onto that will glitch the map causing you to hover in mid air c. If you press Space and skip the cutscenes too fast, the game glitches e.g. Thane's loyalty quest, if you trick the maintenance worker and walk back out, the door closes and locks itself behind you, an instant fail. When the Collectors attack, if you skip too fast, Yeoman Chambers will freeze in between 2 Collectors.  2. Waste of Space Unable to move around to explore the map, despite the large zones  3. Combat System So many abilities yet limited to a lousy combat system. Could have made a fast-paced shooter will abilities for this game.  a. You can't climb over small obstacles properly. You have to slide to them, crouch behind cover, then climb. b. You can't shoot unless you're at the edge of a crate c. You can't shoot over top if the cover you are hiding behind is at bent an angle of 135 degrees (Not exactly at that angle but close) d. AI will shoot itself with a rocket launcher if you force it to shoot at you from behind a wall.  4. Final Boss A giant T-100? Is this Terminator? This was quite lame.  5. Repetitive Maps They use the same maps to simulate different combat. It's so obvious. E.g. The floating platform stage in the Suicide Mission is the same as the one where you first meet the Collectors.  6. Uninteresting Cities Still the same. I think Mass Effect 1 was even bigger and better looking. E.g. Citadel. I can't believe a continuation actually shrank. Keeping to lore isn't evident here and I suspect it was just rubbish work.  7. Wasteful Ship Upgrades/Technologies With so many ship upgrades, I was hoping for space battles. Sadly, it doesn't exist.   a. Thanix Canon : You have such a wonderful weapon where you could have created missions where you use the Normandy SR2 to have space battles. E.g. attack space pirate. Instead you waste it on just the last mission.  8. No Driving/Piloting Mini-game Bioware removed the Mako from Mass Effect 1. Supposedly, you have the UT-47 Kodiak Drop Shuttle, which you can't use at all. The shuttle has no function beyond existing.  9. Morality Choices So fake. Some of the things are just railroaded according to the moral scripting of the Bioware developer. There are no almost no gray areas. Everything is clearly either Paragon (blue) or Renegade (Red). Some choices even clearly make you commit crimes. E.g. Hiding the information from the Admiralty Board in Tali's trial is considered Paragon, C-Sec helping Thane's son, Kolyat avoid criminal indiction is considered Paragon. These are all gray areas.  10. Weapons a. No real discernible difference when I upgrade them. The playstyle is almost the same. The main difference that I saw came from the different heavy weapons that were provided.   11. Story The Mass Effect 2 story sucks. Despite the reputation that Bioware focuses on narrative instead of gameplay, the stories are really very boring. There is no surpise nor something interesting to learn about the Mass Effect universe. It's just so straightforward, go though the Omega Relay, kill the Collectors. First 15 minutes of the game.  Compate this to Mass Effect 1, where they slowly tell you the story of the Citadel, the civilizations, the Mass Relays, the Protheans and then surprise you with the Reapers. The Mass Effect 2 story is horrible.  12. The Ship Up and down the elevator, back and forth from the bow and stern, left and right from the port and starboard. So ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ to keep walking. They could have put teleport shorcuts to let you access the diferent sections through your Omnipad or EDI. All your crew members are located all over the ship, it's such a chore to find all of them. E.g. Jack. Go to the elevator, go to Engineering, exit the elevator, enter the engineering bay, go down the stairs, find Jack.  Captain's Cabin : Admire your achievements? Listen to more intro-screen music? Nothing interesting to do beyond feeding your fish or cleaning out the dead ones.  13. Graphics Really low end.  SUGGESTIONS 1. Morality Choices Place 2 NPCs who will each give diametrically opposite responses depending on what choice you make. E.g. If Shephard says he likes helping the poor, NPC A will claim that he's an idiot for helping those lazy louts while NPC B will admire him for his charity and altruism. If he doesn't give charity, NPC A will tell him that it was good choice to let them get back on their feet on their own while NPC B will admonish him for his cold heartedness.

review_text: I really loved Hitman when I was younger, so i was really excited when I heared about Hitman: Absolution, but did the game live up to my expectations? NO! Don't get me wrong, Hitman Absolution is pretty good as a stealth game but it's a terrible Hitman game.  First of all the levels are much more liniar compared to the other Hitman games, combining that with the fact that your cover can be blown if you come too close to a person that is wearing the same uniform as you, witch mostly results in killing everyone to pass a certain part of the level because for example only police man may acces a certain area and every person in that area will discover you if you try to pass them. Second of all you can not select your loadout at the beginning of a level witch was a cool feature in all the other hitman games because you could select a loadout that fits your strategy. last but definitly not least, there are a lot of levels that don't even have a target to kill, you just have to go from point A to point B trying to sneak past all the guards.  So now the bad aspects are out of the way here are some good aspects. There are mostly multiple ways to kill your targets ranging from setting up an 'accident' to gun everyone down, and the stealth has been improved making it easier to sneak past guards in previous Hitman titles without them seeing you at all, and there's contracts mode which makes you select a level, kill up to 3 targets selected by you and the game creates a challenge based on your actions to share with the rest of the world. And the game has a better story compared to the other Hitman games, not that the story was very importand to me in a Hitman game. I mean you're a hitman, someone wants someone dead and you're hired to do the dirty work, no need for a fancy story there.  It's pretty good as a stealth game but because it's called Hitman: Absolution i'm rating it as a Hitman game, i'll give it a 5/10

Overall score: 5.5/10 Points reduced because of tediousness and many other reasons listed bellow.  This game has such a great first half. Seriously exemplary and I am recommending it specifically for that purpose.   The story is so compelling and interesting. The gameplay, level design, the story itself, and basically everything about the game is wonderful.   However, I almost don't recommend the game. It suffers from a lot of just trashy design right at the end. Everything goes from being logically challenging and entertaining to being blatantly annoying and just extremely rushed.   I don't want to give too many spoilers, but at about the half way mark you take a vehicle that is rather large for what it is, but then it just feels like you hit a brick wall as far as the entertainment value is concerned. Every level after feels like it's just more of that level for no reason at all. The boss fights stopped being fun and started just being really boring. The only thing challenging about the final boss is the pure ♥♥♥♥ that is the level design, hands down one of the most dissapointing turns of the game. Had I known the game was only going to be annoying for the final half, I would have simply stopped half way because I absolutely hated the way this game ended.   There was nothing fun about half of the game. The story was completely rushed to find a conclusion and the conclusion itself wasn't satisfying in any way. The main bad guy is someone you see twice in the game.   I'm so emotionally invested.  /sarcasm  Another dumb part of this game is the fact that absolutely none of it was over the top scifi like how I wanted. I wanted glowing nazi's, I wanted crazy weapons that do ridiculous things, I wanted silly magic, I wanted a story that doesn't wrap up about as clean and efficiently as a 30 car pileup caused by a downed cargo plane full of deer ♥♥♥♥. All of a sudden everything just goes to crap in the story, no real build up to it, no real reason explained as to why it happened, but whatever.  The core game is really fun, you'll probably have a lot of fun playing it, but you will get really annoyed by a lot of the things the game does wrong. And I mean really ♥♥♥♥ing annoyed.   I also hate how short this game is. It suffers from what a lot of games suffer from. It doesn't feel like a whole game. It feels like a tech demo almost. How do they compensate for the lack of a longer story? They throw in audio logs, which is fine because that part ended up being really cool. What else did they do?  They made it to where half of the game is just replaying the game with a slightly different aspect. That is hands down the worst thing this game could have done. I really don't think I'll ever play this game again, I have no reason to.   The decision at the start determines one thing: Armor or Health upgrades. That's it. Realistically, someone who spends a little less time fooling around would finish the story in under 8 hours. Double that. That's the whole game.   All in all, it's visually stunning, plays really well with very few bugs (knife throwing is done horribly, as well as the grenades, and switching weapons, and the weapons themselves are all a major let down by the end).   I have a whole bunch of other reasons why I didn't really have fun with this game, but overall, I think most people will have a lot of fun with it and it's really not even that bad of a game. Bethesda just did what it does with literally everything, a full on nuke to the foot at a certain point and it just never recovers for me after that.   Spoilers: Do not read further.     I seriously hated the fact that the nazi's were able to take over the entire ♥♥♥♥ing planet, colonize the moon, and set up programs to expand to Mars and Venus with just one tiny vault of crazy advanced, mystical technology, meanwhile, we go to one that is supposed to be way bigger and better in every way compared to the vault the Nazi's found and all you ♥♥♥♥ing get out of it are some dumb little magnetic ball and a single power suit for a captain no legs to use. Yeah, if you solve the easy puzzle, you get a generator for your little dinky laser gun. It's still too slow to be considered useful unless you're standing next to a charging station.  THE NAZI'S TAKE OVER THE WORLD and all we get are some novelty items that ultimately mean jack ♥♥♥♥ in the face of the enemy. Such a waste of a good time to throw in crazy scifi weapons. Nah, better stick to the old shootemup pow pow pow weapons instead of some kind of black hole cannon or anti gravity bombs or ANYTHING cool at all.

review_text: So basically, it's an RTS with a twist - the enemy is (mainly) some toxic slime and you need to get rid of whatever is spewing it out. There's a fluid mechanism that makes it possible for the slime to build up and spread out, which is kind of neat (e.g. if you're on top of a hill, the slime will take longer to reach you and it flows into cracks and valleys first).  So, the one player campaign begins nicely enough, you get introduced to units and game mechanics. The story is also interesting (and is mainly what kept me playing). However, it seems that on every mission there is always something new; a new unit or building you need to take into consideration that does one special thing which you need to take out some specific enemies. They're not exciting or add much to your strategy, except 'to kill unit X you need unit Y'. Which doesn't give you much choice of an attack strategy at all, except to build whatever is required to make it through the level.  And this is where the game fails the most - every level is a  grind . You quickly set up your defense perimeter, but once you manage that, it's slowly building up an attack force and inch by inch moving away enough slime to get to an 'emitter', destroy that, and do the same for any other stuff you need to destroy. You know you'll make it, it just takes time and there aren't many surprises.  So, on top of not being very challenging or exciting except the first five minutes of every level, it takes between 45 minutes to over an hour to finish one. When you're finally done trudging through, you breathe out in relief - not satisfaction. Then you get annoyed because it has taken so damn long, and 90% of it was just waiting for things to happen.  So, to sum it up:  Yays: * Interesting campaign story * Fluid dynamics are nice  Nays: * Levels are a grind * Too specialized units make things more annoying than exciting * Tactics are more or less the same every level * Takes too long to finish a level * Only exciting first couple of minutes on every level, after that it's just routine

review_text: Early Access Review

Wow, my last review was huge.  Before you buy, (who is looking to buy this game anymore??) be sure to check out various reviews and decide for yourself what you expect. Yeah, there are bugs, the game is unfinished. At launch, it was one of very few city building sims on Steam that had that element of RTS/Hero management.   Banished for the same money is a near-AAA experience, so don't go buying this game unless you own that or Cities: Skylines. If you are into resource and development sims then Factorio is infinitely a better choice. There are others, but I'm only naming ones I have a lot of experience with.  Towns can be considered an early Java template game, it doesn't stray far from what you usually see in typical browser games, but I love it and that's that. This game has a much better experience than Towncraft, an abandonware game that is way worse of a consumer-trust relationship.  Make sure you educate yourself if you're actually consider spending full price on this game. There are hundreds of games on Steam that would probably be better suited for that amount. If you want a unique glimpse into what was one of the 'founders' of how Steam became the conglomerate of early access/scam gaming, you won't be disappointed. review_text: I would give Crysis a 5/10 because overall, to me, it wound up being a drag to finish.   Crysis is a first person shooter set in the near future. You are part of a task force sent in to help remedy a broken situation, only to find that the circumstances are much different than originally thought. The game provides an open world through which to navigate and complete objectives, and this is easily the strongest aspect of the game.   Levels are gigantic and there are no typical corridors like in most shooters. This gives you as the player a lot of control for how you handle situations, and you will be further encouraged to do so thanks to your special suit that you wear, which provides you with different buffs depending on what you need. You can become invisible, coat yourself in armor, boost your speed, or become very strong all in an instant, allowing you to navigate the levels with a varity of tactics in mind. You can go in guns blazing, or skirt around and never fire a shot. The enemy AI are not the most believeable, but they do a decent enough job of responding to what you do and behaving realistically. They can reach different stages of alertness, which can affect how much they patrol, etc.  Unfortunately, this is where most of the fun stopped for me, even thought the level design and ability to choose your tactics is a huge plus. While most of the levels are well designed, especially in the first half, there are some levels that were exceptionally frustrating to play through, especially in the second half. I found myself very easily lost in the later levels, or facing circumstances that were much more narrow minded in design in comparison. The second half of the game winds up being very confusing, both in the layout and in what you are supposed to do for objectives. The story isn't bad, but the manner in which it is delivered later on felt really stupid and cheesy, and the game lost its sense of adventure for me.   You are given weapons you have to use, but only at certain times, and the game does a poor job guiding you on how to do this. I often felt like I was guessing or throwing darts in the dark for much of the movement and objectives near the tail end. I felt like I was fighting enemies as well as the designers of the game, as if things were either not finished properly, not thought out fully, or intentionally made more difficult just to lengthen the game.  I will be trying future Crysis games, and if you are interested in other installments I would say wait for a sale for this one. Otherwise, you can find other first person shooters that are of higher quality.

I finished this game completely, including achievements, and overall it's not so bad.   First, dont spend more than 5$ on this game. It's not a finished game. It was not polished. There are 3-4 areas where the scripting is so bad it makes a huge contrast with the best aspects of the game.   Pros : - Scenario not so bad. - Multiple ends. - Level design is interesting. - Some models and textures are really great. - Sand and destructible environment is interesting. - Cover mechanics are not bad. - Grenade mechanics are acceptable (you cant aim to throw a grenade inside a room when standing next to a door).  Cons : - The game is not finished (where are the updates ?) - Allied AI is dumb - Ennemy AI is great but lacks of specifi capacities (and sometimes is dumb). - Some scripted events of the game are just ... ( [Minor spoiler] For example, the second commercial center). - Some parts of the game are not realistic at all ( [Minor spoiler] The big ♥♥♥ fall, the C4 blow, ... ]. - Some 3d models and textures are just ugly as f* and do not blend well in the environnment. - The base sensitivity is way too high - Lack of settings in the menu - You have to decrypt and then encrypt the .ini files in order to modify them, which is just useless and time consuming. I did that in order to disable mouse acceleration and set up my mouse sensitivity how I wanted it. I also tweaked some graphical settings manually this way. - No need to talk about the multiplayer  Overall this is not a bad game, but you have to be patient sometimes, due to the poor scripting of some events/AI.  Anyway, I recommend this game if you can get it for 5$/€ or less.

review_text: An astonishing exploration game, with some breathtaking moments, that ultimately fails to be satisfying.  Your heart will soar as you glide gently through changing landscapes, weather patterns, and incredibly subtle day-night cycles. There will be moments where you reach a new place and you'll just stare at the screen at awe. You will find well-written documents that will wave together a remarkably rich, sad, and inspiring story.  Unfortunately, you will mostly be bored. The game is far, far, far too slow. How slow? Think that reaching the next location can take 45 minutes. Sure, it's a beautiful ride to get there, but actually the scenery is not so diverse, and it will get boring. With a game so focused on nature, there really should have been more than three kinds of trees and shrubs.  The slowness is exacerbated by a raw navigation system that will not let you easily plan your journey, so that you will surely have to go back and revisit places you've already been. Since it can take 45 minutes to get to them ... you may find yourself looking up a cheat code for faster travel (and it still will take a very long time!), or just quitting and heading to the wiki to read the rest of the documents and get the whole story.  As other reviewers have mentioned, there is a terrible 'survival' system that is an entirely unnecessary addition to the gameplay. It's charming for the first 10 minutes, but then it's just a bother. I wish, instead, that there was a good navigation system.  A wonderful idea, and I did thoroughly enjoy some moments in the game, but the execution would have benefited from more development.

review_text: Nuclear Throne! The wasteland twin stick shooter you've always wanted, but didn't know it.  STORY - There isn't much story to discover here outside of your own imagination and interpretation of the Text-less cutscenes you receive each time you reach the throne (ala Binding of Isaac) or loop a certain number of times, but the story is clearly not the focus in these type of games.  GAMEPLAY - The gameplay is all it's about and this game has it pretty solid, playing like a much quicker paced Binding of Isaac with a stockpile of unique and fun weapons with ever limited ammo, you race across a handful (4 for the most part then later on 7) of randomly generated levels with random enemy counts - the way the levels are designed to generically path themselves hardly creates something much different then the last time you ran through,   The only noticeable difference comes in where the enemy spawns. sometimes they could all be scattered about in every corner making the level longer or sometimes they will just spawn on top of or in front of the player - It feels in need of some work and doesn't quite live up to the remarkable feature other games have utilized. But I don't expect it to be perfect as it is random. You have good runs, you have bad.  BADDIES: The guns are insanely fun and the enemies are interesting and frightening upon first sight, not know how they attack or move towards you.  You hardly ever see the same types on other levels. Which brings us to the BOSSES. Starting off there are 4 for every 3 stages of each location. Many are total pushovers except, the 3rd boss, Lil' Hunter who arguably is more difficult to fight than the main boss of the game and almost requires you have equip a melee to combat his endless reinforcements!  As you beat these enemies and defeat their leaders. You're thrown back to the start of 1-1 to continue your game through 'Loops' with tougher enemies, higher counts and rarer weapons. Only problem with the game getting harder as you 'Loop' is the poor level generation, resulting in instant deaths and unfair, unsafe spawns in the middle of the dozens of quick moving enemies, making the randomly dropped melee weapons A MUST by those levels.  MUSIC - I like the music, it really distinguished each of the environments adding it's own flair to every map. Couldn't be better, unless there was more.  ART - The art is just above barebones and still leaves much to be desired for these interesting levels and Characters.  Controls - Are bindable, But CANNOT be overwritten, by this I mean, if you change 'Space' for swap weapons to 'E', 'Space' will still swap weapons, but now so will 'E', the main controls can't be swapped between the actions. only new ones added,  COOP - Terrible. It's ONLY playable through 2 Xbox controllers. Each player starts off with their chosen character, their own ammo and weapon equips, each player also has priority of the camera however the HEALTH is shared in a stupidly obtuse way. If one player dies, the health bar of the other is depleted each second until they're are dead and the game ends or they reach their partner and give half of their HP to save them and repeat. It's EXTREMELY unforgiving; even if one of the players is inexperienced in every little mechanic about the game.   This game is a very good and fun game, anybody will find that out within the first 5 minutes, But! It shouldn't have been pushed out of Early Access. It's not ready and isn't yet half as polished as these other Rougelikes are.  And this is not to mention the many game breaking crashes the  Game experiences during the level up screen this happens 25% of the time I've Lvl'd up and has ruined countless runs. Despite that I keep coming back, Hoping to avoid it's bugs and just have a good time.   The way this game is, it's STILL a beta. I believe they've wanted to push it out as it is to make larger profits on PSN for the holidays because I can't imagine them considering this a finished project.

review_text: Could have been great, exploring the possibilities of memory alteration and its ramifications, but ended up as one long massively glorified quicktime event.  I kept wanting to like this game. I kept going back to it in the hopes it would get better or grow on me. I really did want to like it. The graphics, music, story and characters truly are all great and I loved the artistic world that has been created. The game truly does *look* great. What lets it down is everything else.  The trailer gives you the impression that the game centers around being able to modify peoples memories. From that I had gotten the feeling that you could 'stealth around' enemies or convert them rather than rely on direct confrontation every time. Sadly though the memory remix moments are few and far between and they are (like the game in general) far too linear, there being only one 'right' way to remix the memory. This seems like a bit of a missed opportunuty in my opinion, there could have been so much more focus on the memory scrambling and altering abilities in the game.  What little story there is between chases, telegraphed bosses and too-long quicktime chain cutscenes made me want to carry on to see how it went. The story is actually compelling and interesting and I wanted to see if/how the character could grow. But time and again the QTE based fighting and QTE based running away along with the stiffling train-track limited 'open' world the game was just ruined.  I liked the main character, her self questioning, her reassessment of what she had become forcing us to question our own motives and morals.  But with a combat system that is more an exercise in futile button mashing and enemy avoidance than actual combat I really cant recommend this to any but the most die-hard of QTE fans.

review_text: I wish there was a 'neutral' option...  TL;DR: I got my moneys worth out of it, but if you're looking for a challenge, you better give it a pass.  I like all sorts of Rogue-likes and while I am usually not exceptionally good at them, I beat this one with my very first character in about 6hrs (6.5h according to steam, with some small breaks in between).  I like the mechanic of cards (everything is a card, spells, abilities, equipment) wearing out after using them for a while, but once I got my head around the basic strategy of    1) Search for the boss, kill him and everything in between with the cards I feel comfortable with, WITHOUT looting anything at all 2) Search for the Chalice which may or may not completely repair your cards 3a) If your cards are repaired, loot for upgrades only 3b) If not, replace your cards with new ones 4) Leave the level   it felt a bit grindy towards the end. The enemies don't get much stronger and only slightly 'bullet spongy'.  Improvements could be made regarding to controls and UI but for the most part it works. Only the scrolling is complete bullocks right now: scrolling with the mouse stops when your invisible cursor hits the edge of the screen (I guess), and scrolling with keyboard (WASD) means if you release them it will instantly snap back to your character - which posed some serious problems when trying to look at monster hitpoints.  Card variety feels also a bit limited due to overlapping and certain subsets I never felt inclined to use, like push/pull, trap removal, walls, shields, control, summon - even heals I never used again after the card I started with wore out. In my playthrough it came down to this finger of god thingy (a ranged massive single target damage spell), an AOE like heat wave and a fire/lava tile spell of some description. With repairs I could sustain my favourite combo pretty much over the 2nd half of the game, rarely having to trade any of them in for something else. At the end of a run you can unlock some trait cards to change the starting conditions of your game or give yourself a serious disadvantage, but even so, I don't feel the urge to play it again.  It's OK but not more (yet).

review_text: A short game for a steep price.  If you look at the achievement in this game, you can beat it in 25 minutes in a single session.   I beat it in about 4 hours after learning how the game works.   It's a rogue-like game that feels like a Super Metroid.  It was short and sweet but I will reiterate, the price is not right.  Get 'The Deer God' that looks more satisfying.  [Edit] Some people were asking me if I enjoyed the game because that's what counts.   I had a mixed experience of both positive and negative outcomes.  1. The game often likes to screen tear. Hurts my eyes which also deduce me having headaches.  2. Confusion. I had no idea what to do. I just explored and repetively died and learn from each death. I thought there was going to be a save function. I thought the game was going to be a very long one like Dark Soul with some sort of progression. Nope. Just 4 bosses including the last one. I was about to drop the game within the first hour and give a bad review anyway, but I couldn't justifiy that without beating the game first or at least spending 12 hours on it. Ending up doing most of the game in less than 6 hours.  3. They gave some sort of reward for remembering places. Like Super Metroid, you can go to certain places to enhance your characters. Thus rewarding you for as long as you remember where to go. I like that.  4. However, the psedo generated system sometimes replaces the enhancement that you were going to get with something else, and at one point, destroy my entire progression in one run proning me to become stuck. I had to start over but this time I didn't die. Bad experience there.  5. Areas are instanced. There are 3 or 4 areas. One of those areas are in the ceiling or in the sky. If you fall, you are thrown back into the beginning area at the same location regardless of where you fall. I fell a lot. The definition of madness here.  More frustration than satisfaction.  I thought I was going on an adventure but because of it's pseudo randomly generated idea, it ruins that sense of wanderlust. The game had potential. But if anything, it feels like I bought into a early-access product. Not much content mean not much to do.  If I had to compare the experience to anything, it feels like a real short session of Binding of Isaac.  You will get mad. Then you will enjoy it for a bit. And then that's it. I need more and I don't feel like I got my moneys worth.

Hello everyone, I got two minutes to write a quick thing about this game. Around the internet you could probably read something good and something bad. I think this game definitely deserves the good side of the review. First of all, the whole engine of Warframe is of indie manifacture: it's an independent project quite successful. Seriously, the whole thing is really light (not Source's lightness, but still) and scalable. The gameplay is seriously good: various combos, quick reflexes actions and complexity of combat (expecially with some specifical Warframe , which is the type of your armor, aka 'your class/role/set of skills') will keep you intrigued only to see how much combinations you can get with basic attacks. The game doesn't use a real 'cover' system, there is a flexible system with the objects around the map. Here comes the first (maybe of the few) bad thing: you can't change your 'aim side', stuck with the character on the left. The aim point remains on the center, anyway. It's just a matter of perspective. Now, the second real thing is the level up system: you'll level up weapons, 'Warframes' and, obviously, your account. Leveling up will unlock more 'mods slots', which are equipped with 'cards' having each a cost going from 2, to 10. The free-to-play system is really cool: you can't get anything in-game which you can't obtain without game money. At least, at the state of thing right now. Maps aren't really vary, but they're critically and intensive, opening new areas when you do new missions and also, having hazards in the midst of the map: fire, ice, biological things... all sorts of things.  Ending this (and for too long to read): Warframe is DEFINITELY a free to play third person shooter, without PVP for now, so the whole 'cooperative' side of the game is true. review_text: I think this is the best total war game ever made. The campaign is very entertaining and plays totally different for each faction. I have finished two runs (empire and greenskin) and loved both. When I have more time again I'll also finish the other races at least once.  Now people often critize CA for their business practice regarding DLC. In my opinion people have no idea how much work it is to make a game. Blood and gore is dirt cheap and they have to sell it separately or lose a large fraction of their userbase. (Quick math: Their market research indicates that they could lose up to 25% of their customers, to keep the same revenue they'd have to increase the price of the base game by 33% if they add blood and gore in there.)  Beastman are not cheap, but there are tons of individual models. People critize that the dlc is more expensive compared to the base races and this has to be the case. Only a fraction of players buy the dlc, e.g., if 50% of the buyers of the base game buy the dlc for a race that is as large as a base game race, then the dlc has to cost 100% more than the races cut in the price of the base game. So if each race in the base game is 10€ and only 50% of the players buy the dlc, then it has to cost ~20€.  Anyway, I'm really looking forward to the dlc. I love smallish story campaigns most and I really hope the mini-campaign delivers.

review_text: I'm only a few hours in, but I don't think this game deserves the 'mixed' rating it's got just now, so want to get a review out there.  So far I'm enjoying this very much. Despite the big changes I think it still looks and feels like an Anno game. Although I do agree with some reviewers that it may have swung a _bit_ too far towards simplification.  Things I really like:    Better interface for figuring out the flow of resources. You no longer need to consult the wiki or write things down manually to figure out how many buildings you need in each production stage.    Less rigid building constraints. Area of effect for service buildings is now much more organic and sensible -- so again, no need to consult the wiki to figure out how best to cram buildings into an arbitrary circle. (admittedly this process was quite satisfying, but I'm not sure I'd call it _fun_ after the first few times...). This does still exist to an extent in the arctic and moon.   Things I like less:    Stuff that makes the goods feel less... tactile (i.e. numbers on an interface rather than real things moving about):    Better interface for figuring out the flow of resources... its now so easy to be maximally efficient that you don't really need to worry about storage any-more.    Transportation also appears to be essentially instant, so you _literally_ cant go and watch things moving place to place. Also no managing transport routes, which was always fun. This along with the interface does alleviate some of the annoyances with e.g. figuring out where odd bottlenecks are appearing in yor chains -- but that is also reduced complexity (a bit of compexity I always found fun).     Resources that can only be acquired through missions -- I have a feeling these will become tedious soon.   Overall, very positive so far. Although I'm hoping the apparent lower complexity at the start of the game is made up for in the later game. That would really be a major improvement over past instalments, I feel.  UPDATE: Changing to not recommended -- 7 hours in and the novelty wore off. And the late(er) game did not show signs of materializing more depth like I hoped. It would seem the aforementioned streamlining cut out what kept me coming back to previous titles.

Great game - with caveats.  It's times like this I wish Steam had a more granular recommendation option, because there really is a lot to like about this game. It's gorgeous, the art design is amazing, the playable factions are unique and interesting and the music is also great. The beginning game is a lot of fun (if somewhat daunting for newbies), but the game starts to bog down in the late game. The problem is that the challenges don't scale as you level up, so combat becomes inconsequential, and the game goes from being a fun turn based strategy game into an excruciatingly boring 'resource management' chore.  Master of Magic struck the right balance, with high level enemies and challenges to keep the player engaged. Once you've reached a high enough level in Endless Legend, there is little reason to move your armies around the map, and the game devolves into just allocating your city populations between resource priorities.  Still a very good game, if you love spreadsheets.

review_text: Lets start with the good: - The atmosphere is great The world of deadlight will make you feel like you're living Randal's peril and experiencing the awful time that is running through a crumbled zombie infested world.  Sadly thats all I have for good....  Onto the bad: - Controls suck, BIG TIME The controls feel dated, if this were 1999 the clunky controls would be fine, but this is 13 years later that this game came out. What makes matters even WORSE is that this game wants to be a platformer... YOU CANT BE A PLATFORMER WITH CRAP CONTROLS! Nothing is more frustrating than failing that jump and plumetting to your death due to the controls being so freaking awful you can't accomplish simple tasks without the game thinking you are doing something else. For Example, want to jump straight up, better totally lift your hand off your keyboard/controller(yes its bad with both...) or else you'll be jumping forward RIGHT INTO THE ZOMBIES, BAM dead, reload from last checkpoint(yep, load screen time).  - Sound is buggy Some of the cutscenes have sound, some of them don't, and some of them have sound for a few seconds only to be cut out due to some sort of sound bug, AGAIN I must stress, this is not 1999, this sort of thing is not acceptable, where is the patch to sort out the bugs???? You had 2 FREAKING YEARS since the game came out to fix problems like this....  - Suffers from memmory leaks? The game is just plain bad on performance, it randomly decides to start making my PC start chugging away eating up all that processing power, and even just plain crashing if it really feels like it! The settings don't matter, you can go anywhere from a few hours of gameplay, to a few minutes and you'll start noticing performance fluctuations, and they are big ones...  All in all, if you can get past the technical challenges of the game, you MAY be able to find some enjoyment if you are looking for a trail and error zombie survival platformer that can't make up its mind about being one or the other.... If you want a short little narative driven zombie experience, it may even tickle your fancy there. Me, I have to recommend not getting the game, the cons just far outweigh what little benefit the game has, but for some people out there I'm sure they will get some enjoyment out of it, and hey, its at indie price point $14.99 and should give you a couple hours at least, so you get what you pay for right?

review_text: This is one purchase you won't regret.

F.E.A.R.: I’m not F.E.E.L.ing it…  When F.E.A.R. came out back in 2005 it was considered to be a game changer! Not on the level of Half-Life 2 or anything, but, people loved it! They always talked about how good it was. The horror elements were well done, the enemy AI was fantastic, the shooting was super great, and the graphics were amazing. You had to have a pretty beefy computer to play this game.  Maybe back in 2005. Because, man has this game aged poorly. Like, really poorly.   Not only does the game look... bad. It’s really not that fun either, sorry to say. Now this is probably due to the fact that I have ten years of games between the release of F.E.A.R. and now to compare it to. I can definitely see how, at the time, F.E.A.R. would have blown my thirteen year old mind. But as it stands, in 2015, F.E.A.R. is very underwhelming. It feels like a pretty standard shooter, with some cool bullet time and horror elements thrown in.   I’m sure if I could force myself to play more I would probably get something out of it. But as of four hours into the game all I’ve done is: run through long hallways murdering puppet soldiers in an industrial complex, and an office building, while occasionally being frightened by a little girl. And... she doesn’t show up all that much, sadly.  To be fair, there were moments where I was genuinely enjoying myself. Rolling up on fools with a shotgun and slo-mo sliding into them while tearing them to shreds with buckshot is really cool. Like the first 50 times. Then it just becomes an easy “I win” button. Now, the horror aspects of this game were the main draw for me. And trust me, they can be genuinely disturbing and definitely made me jump more than once. But, as you play through the game you can see the seams at the edges of the experience. After about two hours I could tell when the game was going to go from ‘action’ to ‘horror’ just by level design.  Big well lit area funneling into a small dark hallway? Scare incoming. Better turn on my flashlight!  Eventually I just got bored with the game. There wasn’t enough of the creepy to keep me interested, and the ten year old shooting and level design weren’t doing F.E.A.R. any favors.  I honestly spent a large chunk of my four hours trying to figure out where the hell I was supposed to go. Which is the weird thing, because this game is quite linear. Sadly it’s very easy to get turned around, and everything looks the same… Plus sometimes the level designers expected you to intuit where to go without any indication. I felt like I was playing Duke Nukem, or Doom, considering how many times a seemingly decorative A/C vent was actually the way forward.   I hear this game gets pretty crazy towards the end, but I can’t bring myself to continue any further. I wanted to try and complete every game I reviewed for this project… but, I don’t really want to spend anymore time on this one. Especially considering I have over one hundred more to do.  I think the coolest part of F.E.A.R. is probably the firefights. Although the shooting is rather lackluster compared to today, the effects of your combat are really cool. Glass will break, the walls, floor, and ceiling will have large chunks blown out of them. And large amounts of dust, that actually lingers, will be kicked up. It forces you to move around, and looked really cool. Plus the bloody aftermath was always satisfying...  The dust tech is the most impressive part of this game to me. Seriously.  Honestly, this game reminds me a lot of Psi-Ops, an old game for the PS2. It revolved around a paranormal military task force fighting against another paranormal military group. It had all of the same hyper violence, weird psychic powers, creepy horror segments, and strange meat puppet soldiers. Expect I played it at the time... so I liked it.  It’s not that FEAR is bad… It’s just like an old movie. It was amazing when it came out! It’s just, going back and looking at it compared to ‘modern’ releases, it just doesn’t hold up.  Luckily I picked F.E.A.R. up on sale for like $2.75. If you’ve never played FEAR and always wanted to. Watch a let’s play. Or, if you absolutely have to play it, grab it on super-sale. It’s… really dated.  Like this review? Check out  100hoursave.com  for more.

review_text: Ok, after having completed the game on the first playthrough I will write my review.  People seem to be giving this game a lot of crap for 'no new game engine' 'it's to short' 'it's to expensive' etc. etc.  Firstly, they don't need a new engine for this game, they have a good formula with what they have done in the past and I see no reason to change that, the game looks great for us PC players, not sure how consoles look. As the saying goes, 'If it ain't broke, don't fix it.'  Now, this game IS shorter than Borderlands 2 BUT I did every single side quest with the exception of like 6 on my first playthrough and that took me 18 hours, Now, playthrough times for everyone will vary, I don't speed run through but I don't take my sweet time either. That being said, minimum of 2 playthroughs for max level, grinding for gear, etc. you absolutely will get your $$$ worth out of the game.   Is it a 60$ game? That depends greatly on the person, I personally would get enough time out of the game to make it worth the 60$ price tag, other people might not, it's all on what you view as worthwhile.   My personal rule for games is 1$ per hour of game time makes it worth it for me, got the game through Green Man Gaming for 45$, I'll do that with just the first character easily.  TL:DR - In my opinion the game is worth the time and money, enjoy the pre-sequel. It ties the first 2 games together nicely.

Whispering Willows is an intriguing horror/adventure puzzle game that takes cues from Native American lore to bring the 'haunted mansion' levels so often seen in RPGs to the forefront of the story. The game is about Elena, a girl whose father went missing and who keeps getting nightmares about his disappearance. Determined to find him, she sets off to the mansion, but it turns out that the Ghostbusters hadn't exactly come here yet. (There's actually an achievement referencing this, so I'm allowed to make that joke! :P)  The best part of Whispering Willows has got to be its aesthetic. I absolutely love the art style of this game; everything is so beautifully rendered, from Elena to her spirit to the environment and all of the buildings and effects. Even torches and fireplaces look absolutely magnificent.  Unfortunately, that's pretty much it for the good. The story is extremely bland; there's no voice acting besides some sounds in the cutscenes, and little is actually said by any of the characters that talk to Elena. She herself doesn't seem to have any lines besides the aforementioned cutscenes. Outside of the intro cutscene, there isn't much to what's going on, and it's just 'solve this mystery, now solve this mystery, oh hey someone's chasing you'.  The gameplay mechanics are there, but even these are under-utilized. What Whispering Willows allows players to do is take control of Elena's spirit separate from her body, using it to get through tight spaces or possess items or switches to help Elena advance. Unfortunately, this isn't used enough times, and it feels like a gimmick more than anything when you do have to use it. Most of the time it's just used to talk to characters that don't really add much.  Probably the worst part of the game is its pacing. It feels like everything is happening so  slowly ... Elena walks too slow, or there's too many corridors, or there's too many levers, or you don't know what to do next because almost nothing is explained to you, or usually a combination of all of that. I was getting really bored really fast. Not to mention, every time I tried to press the SHIFT key to start running and speed things up, Elena turned to me and refused. It's like Elena wants to admire the scenery of human bones more than I do.  It's still regarded as a really good game by the few people who have reviewed it, so don't let my experiences completely shroud your potential of playing this game. Especially if you're looking for something based off of Native American lore; there's plenty of that in the journal entries.

review_text: Explore a desolate environment and build the road on the go in this exciting racing game  Although you might assume there’s not much left to innovate when it comes to racing games, the truth is that there’s always something one change, not to mention all the things that can be added. Not just that, but genre mixing is quite popular as well, which opens up an even larger palette of possibilities when it comes to gameplay mechanics. Build the road ahead one piece at a time  Skyreach is not your typical racing game, but it’s not exactly a revelation in the genre either. In short, the main idea is that you are free to build the track as you go along, making it more of an exploration game than anything else. Unfortunately, it has a few flaws that make the experience a tad too annoying to be enjoyable, especially if you go in expecting speed and actual driving.  To begin with, the action takes place in some kind of great desert, although you are actually racing between floating islands. The fact that there’s no ground means that you have to place each piece of the track as you go, otherwise you fall to your death. If this sounds anticlimactic and slow, it’s because it is. Hence, you’re usually moving along at a snail’s place, partly because it’s difficult to maintain speed and build the track, and partly because the game takes forever to place the said track pieces anyway. Over-sensitive controls and decent graphics  You also have to deal with the poorly optimized controls, given the fact that the vehicle oversteers at the slightest touch, making it incredibly frustrating to negotiate corners at slightly higher speeds. Thankfully, at least you can use a controller to have a better grip on the situation, but that only makes it better than with a keyboard, not better in general. It shouldn’t come as a surprise that controls are important in a racing game.  Things are much better in the graphics department, and it’s obvious that the designers put some soul into the environments and the game world in general. The desolate landscapes can be quite moody and engaging, even if you’ll barely get the chance to admire them while you build the track. The graphics quality is decent as well, although there are some performance issues that shouldn’t be there. A great concept that needs a lot of work  All things considered, Skyreach is certainly something new, and it’s only natural that it didn’t come out perfect the first time around. However, something has to change as quickly as possible, because right now the game is not able to keep players happy for more than a few minutes.  With out a few updates this game is unplayable. As of now i cannot recommend the game in its current state at all. If you want a racing game look elsewhere cause you wont find it in Skyreach.

review_text: There are no really choices, only 1 story

I'm afraid my overall experience with the game has been far too negative to warrant a positive review.  First off: the good. - Graphic: The game is visually stunning, smooth and very pleasant to look at. - Sound: The soundtrack is spot on and all audio effects are great.  The bad. - Story: For what i've played, the story of the game is ridiculously bland. [Minor Story Spoiler]   You start off as a white leaf in a stormy night, you fall off and you turn into a child. A forest creature takes care of you. You see the pair eating fruits until nearby trees run out, your newfound mother dies of hunger just as you bring home enough fruits for a few days, so you depart. You die, but by dying you are brought back to life because the ancient tree you initially fell from has put all its hope into you. Eventually you reach the tree but a giant black bird decided to steal a glowing orb inside the tree and threw it away, now your job is to find three stones and bring them to three different places to restore order to the forest.   Now, in my eyes this is a very, very boring storyline that exists mainly to justify your adventures. If you disagree, which is absolutely fine, try to tell any friend or person you know what the game's story is without having them laugh or be left extremely confused.  - Gameplay: Frustrating and tedious, put shortly. The game feels like a mix of Super Meat Boy and Metroid, as in you roam around from point to point across multiple obstacles with a certain degree of difficulty involved, while you will eventually find statues that give you additional abilities and with a bunch of side pickups if you're interested to hunt for them.  Now, unlike SMB and metroid, Ori and the Blind Forest doesn't do a great job at telling you what or where are threats; this is caused by graphical clutter (as you may find enemies completely covered by the foreground) or by using different colors: initially purple means bad, which makes sense and helps recognize threats immediatly. Eventually you may find differently colored threats that are hard to recognize quickly (mostly due to the graphical clutter, again).  So you died...it will happen, especially as there are a few areas where the developers want you to die at least that one time, and you'll find yourself back to your save point. Where is the save point? It's wherever you want! You're in charge of placing checkpoints! While this seems like a great design choice at first, it turns out to be fairly tedious after a while mainly because you do not know what you will face next. To create a checkpoint you'll need one unit of energy, which can be found in blue crystals scattered around the scenery, no energy = no checkpoint. If you save sparingly enough, then you will always have a few units of energy to save pretty much anywhere, however the game does a very bad job at telling you when you should create a checkpoint. Initially you'll find a crystal in locations where saving makes sense, which is how it should work, however after not much time you're left on your own in this regard, causing you to potentially save in terrible places or not save after you've been smashed by a falling rock, causing you to repeat a portion of the level.  Speaking of repetition, just like in metroid you'll notice there are a few areas you can't quite reach yet. Most of the times they aren't subtle at all and they serve as a reminder to come back once you unlock the ability required to reach it, which means you are bound to backtrack your steps. Most times those areas are accessible after jumping past various obstacles, which is fine, however it may happen that you go through all obstacles just to discover you need an extra ability just to reach that sweet sweet power up, essentially removing the prize after beating the challenge.  Now, while roaming around and defeating enemies you will eventually be awarded an ability point, which can be used to improve your capabilities. In the definitive edition this system is a tad flawed: the 4th upgrade of the 3rd skill line (yes, the skill tree is made of three lines) is a bonus to an ability you unlock at the end of the game and you must get it in order to access other upgrades in that line, which is a TERRIBLE design choice (this isn't an issue in the normal version of the game). Of course there will be more upgrades for this new ability which will, obviously, be in between the ones you may want, instead of being at the end of the line. Because reasons!  You should buy this game if you don't mind frustrating games.  TLDR: a mix of super meat boy and metroid, visually stunning but frustrating gameplay and bland storyline.

Early Access Review

No Man’s Sky: Casual Survival Score: The new Benchmark in Space Exploration and Survival.  Pro’s-  Unique art style, vibrant, colorful graphics. Simple spaceflight and navigation. The music steals the show and matches nicely with each situation. Progressively upgrade your Ship, Multi-tool and your spacesuit. Combat is simple and can be avoided (most times). An economy that rewards exploration as much as mining. Discover new species of plants and animals and sell the data for Units (cash). Play at your own pace. It’s just fun to explore a universe of wildly diverse worlds. It’s your universe, enjoy.  Con’s- Planet/atmosphere flight is ‘wonky’ and difficult to control due to invisible boundaries placed on how you position your POV.  Draw distance is too short. Large objects and some terrain ‘pop’ noticeable across the horizon.  Mining is necessary and repetitive but can be mitigated with trading Units for minerals instead. Deep Space navigation can seem aimless until you grasp how enormous the Universe is and begin traveling to your long term goal.  Review: No Man’s Sky is a Space Safari in an Exploration sim that offers you a chance to play at your own pace. The goal of the game is what you make it. You can follow the ‘Atlas’ and try to reach the center of the Universe of this huge game, or just wander. It’s up to you.  As you travel from world to world, you will mine necessary resources that are used for fuel, ammunition and item creation. But, you can also use your multi-tool to scan and document new species of plants and animals and upload that information to the game database for Units (cash).  The economy is balanced in your favor so that you will have more Units than you need after a few hours of exploration. You can use this currency to purchase fuel/energy for your ship. Mining becomes more optional over time.  Combat is necessary to gain entrance to certain locked structures, but you don’t have to break into these buildings to play the game. It’s an option. The first battle you enter with the Sentinels may seem very difficult if you haven’t upgraded your multi-tool, but with time and upgrades combat becomes very easy.  Overall, I have greatly enjoyed my time in this sim and look forward to finding new worlds, new civilizations….to boldly go where No Man has gone before.

review_text: Recommend but Buyer Beware!  So let me start with that I've spend some money on this title. A-10C, Combined Arms, Flaming Cliffs, and the Campaign pack for A-10c (so far).   I used to play Falcon 4.0 all the time back in the day, and I'm probably around a dozen hours into DCS.   First thing to know is that this is more like a system of games that all work together in one interface, this is why the DLC seems expensive. Next thing to know is that apparently the steam version of this title won't pass the multiplayer server checks and the recommended way to install is purchase and get your license keys then download and install from Eagle Dynamics website (wish I would have known would have saved me a lot of time and heartache).   The next thing I want to talk about is the mission / campaign packs, especially the enemy within pick for A-10C. While the missions are well thought out, the voice acting and dialogue is amateurish. To the point that I am wondering if the pack was fan made, yet I got charged $10 for it. I'm glad the missions are well thought out and briefings are detailed, but for the extra money on top of $50 for the A-10C ,I would have expected better recording quality and to be given a choice of starting locations.   That's one of my disappointments with DCS. In Falcon4 you could choose ramp (cold) start, runway, or flying. D.C.'s has some separate missions for this but campaign missions do not give a choice. You start wherever the designer wanted you too.   Finally, multiplayer, and I mentioned at the start there's some kind of oddness with the steam download. Reddit seems to think installing a 'pure' version from Eagle direct fixes it. I'll try that soon  Overall I'm going to have to give this one a 8/10 would def have still purchased these games, but I wish that some of these known issues were out there front and center for purchasers. So all that said, if you like flight simulations, you won't regret this.   I recommend you have or plan to purchase a HOTAS I play with 3 monitors trackir, and the x-56 HOTAS. If your a veteran of any of the Janes games from back in the day or Falcon4 like me then you will quickly feel at home
