review_text: Crysis is the best and worst of gaming.  The best is it's got all this emergent gameplay based around simulation, where there is a giant map you can seemingly go anywhere in, can accomplish objectives however you want. Blow up all the buildings, drive all of the vehicles.  Your suit has a great mechanic to it where you can select between 'modes' like strength, speed, or armor, and run around  The game is surprisingly low budget as well, but uses every dollar spent to maximum effectiveness, because it's not about the 'guided cinematic experience' but rather the actual gameplay.  The bad is the gameplay isn't all that great.   All of the randomness in the gameplay systems makes for a frustrating experience. Mostly this takes the place of weapon accuracy where you are forver at the mercy of RNGesus. Sometimes you can waltz into the middle of a crowd of enemies and one shot them all to the face. Sometimes you can shoot into a wall and the enemies still die. Other times RNGeus will dictate your shotgun blasts all miss and the AI gets headshots at the same time and you die instantly. Sometimes enemies take 3 seconds to react to you standing directy in front of them. Other times you get insantly killed by a mounted machine gun on a boat 1 km away while you are hiding in the bushes.   Other games with recoil, even in 2007, usually make it feel good by making the camera shake so the weapon always shoots where it points, in a semi-random pattern you can learn to control. In this game the bullets spray out in a random pattern around the reticle, in addition to randomly rotating your camera to simulate unsteady aim, in addition to randomly rotating your reticle because of screen space 'cinematic' effects.  The audio sounds good sometimes, and bad other times. There is an issue with prioritizing effects here. Stuff like distant explosions has no audio at all, because the game doesn't know that is more important than a nearby ambient sound.  Something with the rendering pipeline seems to be adding significant display lag to things. This is often done when people want to really push graphics, as you can parallelize things better if you can delay processing of graphics stages by several frames so the CPU isn't spent waiting. Unfortunately in this case it seems to mean where you shoot isn't where the graphics is rendering you will shoot, but rather where you are in the simulated world. This is just too much lag for a FPS game that asks for precision. RNGesus may be here to try and mask this issue.  The end result is the gameplay loop is reduced to this: save often.   This may have been the norm in 2007, but playing it in 2016... yeah it's not worth finishing IMO.

review_text: Early Access Review

In short, Sniper Elite is a third-person-view PS2-era shooter about American covert operative in Berlin in May of 1945, sniping Soviets and occasional Germans with that famous 'bullet cam' effect, 'borrowed' from Max Payne games. I can recommend it only to people who want to kill Ruskies in bulk. There you'll be mowing hundreds of those stupids. Which is good, if you're, say, some right-winged Nazie-lover, still wishing for re-count with USSR. I repeat - for the most of the game, you'll be running in Wehrmacht uniform, assisting Germans in killing Soviets and hiding some 'war secrets'. Great deeds for every karma, don't ya think?  As of shooter rules, obviously, this game is focused on using the sniper rifles. Using those boomsticks was somewhat elaborative for the year 2005, as those rifles are using basic ballistic rules, so the rifle bullets are flying on some parabolic arc and not just direct line. Which means that you need to aim higher on bigger distances. You can use some other weapons, like a silenced pistol, or guns picked from your enemies (machine guns like DP-27 or PPSh), but their ballistics were nerfed alot, to make player use his main rifle more than other weapons. Alas, the situations where you're supposed to perform really long shots is quite numbered. Damn, there are only 5 maps for the whole game! And only 2 of them are of some use for sniper work.  As of 'stealth' there... Just forget the word. You can't be stealthy. Your silenced pistol can't work, your enemies will always be knowing where you are, no matter how good you're with hiding in dark pixels.  Now, WW2 tag. It's fake. The history details were not simple altered. The whole game setting is a pure phantasy! Where diesel tanks can be blown up with normal bullets, where war veterans waltz in the war zone without cover, and don't hide in front of enemy. Where... everything is not simple not based on reality, but twisted with good amount of absurd and ignorance.  What I have against that game other than siding with Nazies in their fight against good guys? As I am sure that Russkies in the 1945 were certainly good guys killing the dragon with the sword, whereas USA was dropping A-bombs and performing all other usual stuff like turning into dust by carpet bombing cities like Tokio, Dresden, Würzburg, Swinemünde and so on... And yes, the story of covert support of Nazies retreat and revival is one which (some people in) USA is quietly proud of, and I strongly despise. Read some books about it before critizing me there, folks.  Okay, again - why Sniper is a bad game, in my opinion? First is the setting, where you have 5 maps on 28 'missions', with huge backtracking part, always. The structure of levels leave to player a light possibility to make some maneuvers, but in practice it is even more strained, than in the Crysis 2. If you count the possibility to turn left or right from rails as 'non linear game play', I'm sorry for you. In the end, Sniper Elite turns out just as beeline as any typical shooter, very far from the defintion of 'non linear'-something. Second, the AI. It is horrendous. The meanest soldiers of the Europe of year 1945 never use any real combat tactics. They just standing in the middle of a street, shooting at you, or rushing forward on your position. On the other hand, they will be always knowing where would you go, no matter how silent you are, how many hours you spend crouching and proning in the shadows. Dead stealth experience. Third, the weapons aren't any good, even with addition of ballstic rules. I know dozens of other games, where you can hone your sniper skills much better.  All in all, there is no point for I can like this game. But I'll give it a 5/10 mark, for its effort to improve the 'bullet cam' effect. Now, I know there are a big fan base of Sniper games, which turn down any negative review on their beloved 'shoot-the-Soviet' shooter. I can't care less.

review_text: Early Access Review

Analogue: A Hate Story is a visual novel in which you try to uncover what happened to a lost spaceship and more importantly, it's crew. The story is presented via numerous documents and two A.I.s who also serve as pursuable love interests, all the while trying to influence the player's decisions. The experience is quite short-lived, which feels weird, considering that the story is clearly trying to emotionally influence you and centers around some fairly dark themes. More often than not though, these are presented in a pretty hamfisted way and the addition of extremely stylized characters makes for quite a mixed-bag overall.  Apart from an input-based sequence I encountered right at the end, there really is not any gameplay to speak of. The soundtrack served it's purpose as background music for you to read notes too but that's it. And finally, there are several different endings and achivements to obtain.   Most of this should not be news to any Visual Novel aficionado out there but rather maybe serve as a warning to anyone looking for a different experience. So for anyone interested in a decent enough VN, I would recommend getting this game on sale because even considering possible replayability-incentives, it's a very short-lived tale. Overall though, I will not recommend this game to a general audience which is most likely looking for more game and less novel.

review_text: The game starts nicely enough, and I must commend the artists who have done an excellent job. The renaissance Italy, full of colors and rich in details, provides an excellent and atmospheric setting for the upcoming adventure. Nostradamus, horoscopes (strangely called 'charts' in the game), historical figures, sinister plot threatening the ruling family, all that is very promising and fresh. You can even pose as man or woman according to your wish, and the dialogues play slightly differently, which I found impressive.  However, as the game unveils, its quality starts to deteriorate. The translation is not very good, sometimes you wrestle with an odd expression of unclear meaning. There is absolutely minimal amount if animation in the game - a guard extends his arm, woman throws dice, Nostradamus nods and smiles. At one occasion, you talk to a person over a closed door, which no doubt saved the dev team some time and effort. The late game puzzles become infuriating, especially a particular substitution cipher where you must decipher.. a French text, probably because translating the cipher would take too much effort. The characters are barely legible and sometimes similar, for example the character '-' (hyphen) means three different letters.   The game also suffers from a split personality disorder. One moment you discover a dead body in a room, and are getting ready to open his personal chest to shed light on his murder, but your character refuses - Nah, probably nothing in there. Just another corpse. Without reporting your find, you are suddenly teleported to your home. Nostradamus listens to your report about the murder and then gives you a very important task - to brew a herbal tea, so that you can sleep better. I run back to the castle in the middle of the night, but the drawbridge was closed. Nobody cares. Another dead body? Old news.  The game ends, I imagine, in a moment when the dev team run out of finances, their creditors probably stormed their offices, and started confiscating computer equipment. A duo of a artists have decided to quickly conclude the game with a 20 seconds long cutscene that makes absolutely no sense whatsoever, and that's it, end of story.   'You have finished the game with %d points'.  '%d' is a symbol for numeric value in formatted print. The programmer was probably dragged away by then, so there was nobody to correct the error.   What a pity.

R.I.P WOTR...  Thanks for the memories...

Sid Mier's Civilization Beyond Earth is a great game but at the same time lacks in content and can be a disappointment to people who have already played Civ 5.   It seems like unfortunately Firaxis is going down the route of 'lets sell an unfinished game at full retail price and charge our customers for 25 dollars worth on every DLC that has new content which should have been in the base game to begin with.' Civilization Beyond Earth is still fun to play but I am sad that Firaxis is starting to money milk thier titles and not finishing a game before they sell it.  Like civ 5, its a turn based strategy game that will have you hooked for hours. In fact it looks and plays just like civ 5 but looks sci fi which sems to be a big problem among civ fans but it doesnt bother me any. The game play is fluent, minor glitches and errors happen time to time but nothing major and it really does make you think when you play 'just one more turn...'  You'll spend hours of excitement and having fun. Its always a blast trying to beat your opponents in several different ways and trying to get an advantage to be the dominant player.   The only complaints I have about it is that it is priced to high for what its worth. They took a lot of good game mechanics from civ 5 and the one big thing for me is the lack of sponsor choices they give. There are only 8 different civs to choose from which seems like a big draw back and as of right now the game only goes up to 500 turns before the game ends. I like my civ games to last a bit longer.  My opinion is if you like civ games it is a definite must play but I would suggest waiting until it drops in price and for DLCs to be released. Right now Its only worth 30 to 40 dollas and they sell it for 60. but when it does go down in sales price pick it up! it is still fun to play and will have you hooked for hours despite it's obvious set backs.   Gameplay wise i give it a 9 out of 10 but content wise i give it about a 5 out of 10.

review_text: A story-based adventure/exploration game with very little story and very little game. You are a teenage girl, coming home after a year abroad, to find the home empty. So what does a girl do in this case? She goes rummaging in the trash for notes about what could have possibly happened. It is this implausability that really detracted from the mood the game was aiming for. All you do is rummage through the house for written notes from various members of the family that conveniently allude to the plot. Interacting with some objects/notes allows the player to hear narration from the sister, a key figure to the storyline. The story is very forgettable, however, I do commend the game for effective use of multiple red herrings (some of the best I've seen in a videogame).

A Bird Story is a short narrative about a boy who saves a bird from being attacked. However, the bird does take an injury and so the child takes care of the bird and builds a lasting friendship with it. Your take on the game is to follow this narrative with minimal interaction. The stroy is rich in detail, and if you place enough attention to detail you can get caught up in story. A very emotional journey of growth as we see this boy go from no purpose in life to enjoying imaginative adventure with his friend.  This is a very simplistic artstyle, but one that stays with you if your paying attention. The most powerful part of the game is the music which really carries both a whimsical fantasy and a tone of everlasting change. And each scene is very powerful with both mediums working together and blending well.  A Bird Story is a great narrative, and one that I would recommend to anyone who follows Freebird Games as they make powerfully moving games, but the question that is asked when making this review is, 'Do you recommend this game?' And in all honesty, as much as I loved the story and was able to connect with that child...it wasn't a game. Very simple. Those who have played To The Moon will really enjoy this, and those who appreciate art and music will as well, but there is no interaction, no reward, and no real input from the player that affects the game. Like I said a great narrative, but a very boring game.

review_text: I've played through Dark Salad's first two installments, though I'm not a massive fan of the series. That being said, here's my spiel on the third installment: From is one of those publishers who apparently really like to reuse their formula for gameplay. This is by no means a bad thing, especially since it works here. Overall, the movement for the player and for the varying sizes of flesh in this installment feels way less stiff from previous games. Movements feel faster and more fluid, allowing for combat to be way more intimate over previous installments where the stratagem was usally just to wait for Bigbritches McBossalot to do a heavy attack so you have time to peck at his ankles. The one place this falters, and feel free to tell me I'm wrong about this, is with shields that have 100% physical block. In my opinion, these things make Dark Sutra games way too easy, as only a handful of enemies have alternate means of damage.  The enviornment of this game is something to behold. Many of the areas (save for some out of bounds nonsense, as per usual) are marvelously crafted and mind-bendingly interwoven. It's truly something to visit the other half of a destroyed bridge hours of meandering around later. Something I kind of hated about Dark Souls One was the amount of depressing caves and dark areas that were visually akin to hard pudding. There's a lot less of that here, and environments of that sort are better crafted this time around.   All I can really say about PvP from my lack of expecience in prior games and lack of a pretentious state of mind is that it's a lot easier to have fun with it if you're new to the series. Being bad is totally fine, because odds are you'll have like 3+ dudes with you engaged in jolly cooperation anyway.   Class rebalancing is pretty solid. Weapon stances are a welcome change to make melee combat seem less mundane. The same can be said for spells. Good God there are a lot of magic and faith options this time around--both in gear and in heathen spellcasting. There's so many that I'd even say that the devs clearly favored all classes having at least a bit of every category, because as Strength BigCubberhousen I feel that I'm missing out on quite a lot.  Lastly, and this can be a good thing or a bad thing, Dark Souls Tres really, REALLY wants you to remember the first two games. There are so damn many references through bosses, enemies, equipment, areas, and everything else that are about the first two games, mostly Dark Souls One. I honestly feel that they overdid it a bit, because the game feels less like its own thing and more like a reimagining of the first installment. To this end, you might actually enjoy the game more if you haven't played previous installments.  Overall, I'd say this game is worth your time if adventure games are your thing, casual or not. Given the game's resources for the player, these games really aren't as hard as they're infamously said to be. In fact, I'd say they do difficulty extraordinarily well, as encounters are engaging and require you to think quite a lot more than most other modern games, at least that I have played. Murdering those big bosses for the first time really feels like an achievement, and to me that makes the game well worth what I paid for it.  In summary:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4ZvbN4MXQDM

review_text: Never have I felt more emotionally connected to a game.
Early Access Review

First thing wrong with this game. The price. Its $39.99 for an almost 5 year old game. That is absolutely ridiculous. But if you are willing to buy this, there are many good things in ahead.  Campaign Mode Pros: -Veteran Campaign lasted roughly 9h 30m. If you take into account that there are collectables, that I didnt go out of my way to find, the time could rise another 1h 30m, with Spec Ops untouched. The story was nice and followed TF 141 after the MW2 story well. It was a fun experience in full.  This is something small, but the 'Graphic Content' didn't stand up to MW2's No Russian stage. Just in my opinion.  Cons:  -I dont see any reason to play it again unless you A: want to collect the intel. B: Didnt beat it on Veteran and want the achievements, or C: Just want to refresh yourself of the story. While fun, I wish there were more throwbacks to MW2. Like what happened to Private James Ramirez? It would have been cool to hear some dialogue, or see something about him being promoted. -The soundtrack. Ugh. The first thing I noticed when playing the campaign was this. It all felt like 'genericactionmusic.mp3' Nothing stood out to me like Hans Zimmer and Lorne Balfe did. Perhaps it was because Brian Tyler did about 1h 20m of music and Lorne Balfe did 3h. MW3's soundtrack seemed very bland and felt like it got old quickly. -The final level on Veteran. My. God. Just the last little part after the elevator. Hated every second of it. The AI is almost impossible to beat.  Spec Ops- Pros: -Survival. Just everything about it was fun. Unlocking the weapons so it didnt get old too quickly and kept you on your toes especially when you leveled up during a game of survival. Probably my favourite feature out of all of it. The goal is to survive as long as possible with the waves getting harder every like 5 waves. You start at level 1 out of 50 and you feel really helpless at the beginning. But as you level up more it gives this sense of really growing. Some people may dislike the leveling system, because everything isn't at your disposal in the beginning, but I liked it.  But that brings us to our con Cons: -Additional Spec Ops are unlocked by this leveling system. You could just play Survival alone and unlock the Spec Ops missions rather quickly. It makes the urge to play Spec Ops much less than in MW2. Seeming how the missions aren't unlocked by beating the previous missions. Speaking of which, MW3 with 16 missions, and MW2 with 23. Ok. I suppose there are more if you want to buy them, but for almost $10 as writing this, Im not getting it. Chaos is also a mode I havent been able to play because it is $10.  Multiplayer- Pros:  -People still play it. Thats fun.  Im going to be a little nitpicky, but why do some guns look better in MW2 than in MW3? They made the AA-12 look tons better, but the Desert Eagel looks tons better in MW2. I found this kinda annoying.  Cons: -Hackers I guess. I dont really play multiplayer.  Overall: MW3 was a fun and enjoyable game with many things to do from the campaign, to multiplayer, to survival. Perhaps most of my complaints were minor and dont seem like much to most people and I can understand that. I will always like this one the most, right behind MW2. If you are able to though, I suggest waiting for a sale, as this game is NOT WORTH $40.

review_text: The thing about Divinity Dragon Commander is it NOT a bad game, it just isn't a very good one. Everything it does, it does mediocre. It has trouble finding a true audience because if you take each of it's elements, RTS, Turn-based Strategy Maps, Unit Upgrades, Dynamic Story Progression, Politics, etc. you can easily find a better game that outmatches this one in every aspect. The real downer is that this is the only game I know of of it's kind. You'd be hard pressed to find a game that combines all of these amazing elements into one game, it's just such a pity that they didn't put more effort into them because then it would be amazing.  The Real-Time Strategy battles are very bland, the map, units, and buildings are not exciting to look at, and battles are just very uninteresting exchanges of little fireballs. The 'strategy' element is virtually non-existent, it encourages Zerg tactics of just building fast and attacking fast. There's no sense of 'going your own pace', the way the battles are set up, if you don't push hard early on you'll lose whatever tactical advantage you may have.  Using your dragon avatar in battle is very novel, but unfortunately it stops as being just a novelty, it rarely impacts the battle in a significant enough way and as the game progresses it becomes more of a tool to prevent enemy dragons from wrecking your own troops rather than an advantage you have over your enemies.  The political RPG aspect has some shine to it as each character is interesting, well animated, and has a real sense of personality and their own agenda. It however falls flat as you realize that most choices you make are between two arbitrary extremes that will please one side but not another, forcing you to make decisions more on which factions you want to appease than on your own personal agenda.  Finally the Turn-based tactical map is also very bland and even less interesting to look at than the first Total War game (Shogun). You can do very little, limited to building a single thing per turn, and deciding which territory to move your troops between. It's not bad per se, but basically comes down to very basic chess, moving your units between where you need a defensive line and where you expect there to be an enemy vulnerability.  All in all it's not a bad game but it's very middle of the road. I can't really reccomend it to anyone but I don't want to bash it because it did take a big risk in doing what it did, combining multiple elements into a single game idea, and I personally feel it could work well if each element was given more. As it stands, it's a great concept with bland uninspired execution, but is one I feel could be done well.  If you must get it, I would suggest waiting until it's on sale.

review_text: Early Access Review

I am completely revoking my positive review because of the way 2K/Firaxis are starting to treat this installment of the franchise. I had damn good fun with BE for a good while, enjoyed Apollo difficulty thoroughly, but now that I am seeing the Rising Tides expansion, I can't help myself but think that we are being served a 3-course Civ-5-reskin meal.  The Good: - Sci-fi Civ 5 - New mechanics pertaining to all of the 4 X's - Beautiful alien world - friendlier factions - Aliens!  The Bad: - It's still civ 5, just with a reskin - new mechanics that were so botched or easy to exploit that Firaxis had to overhaul most of them - completely overhauling the faction bonuses just a few months after launch showed us clearly how little 2K/Firaxis invested in playtesting all the aspects of the game - No variation - quests stay invariably the same, and while that might help min-maxers, it feels like a rigid system - Not enough leader personality - IMO, there are 3 personalities in this game: Purity, Supremacy and Harmony. The leader's name or mindset doesn't matter at all. - The aliens are a shortsighted addition - there is so little you can do with them. Granted, they can mess you up, or make your advancements slower, but I expected more from it than adopting an alien next and turning them friendly after half a game, or just plainly exterminating them. Using aliens to mess other players up or having a way to sic a while alien colony at someone else would have been nice (apart from builying around a nest to force the game to spawn a new alien near another player).  The conclusion: It is obvious that in the end, we will get a complete beyond earth experience, but we will have to wait till 2K/Firaxis launches all the DLC's and all the additions to the game. Otherwise in my eyes, it is a huge no-no-buy. Neither the full game nor the DLC's.

review_text: Harder than Takeshi's Challenge.

A fairly solid 4x game, with out-of-sequence turn based combat resolving rather than the 'bash units together' that Civ 5 has. It has absolutely beautiful graphics, and some of the most unique races to play as. Whereas in Civ 5 you would get a unique building, unit and perk, this game has races that will alter your entire playstyle depending on who you choose.  Be warned however, one of the main mechanics of this game is 'Winter' - which slows all production and movement to an almost halt. The 'story' of this game pretty much revolves around Winter getting longer and more frequent until it is permenant. This causes two things: 1) Unfun gameplay for those who didn't want to invest in all the anti-winter upgrades and perks. 2) A completely railroaded research and production path for those that do invest in all the anti-winter upgrades and perks.  There is no way to disable Winter without mods as yet, and whilst interesting and unique the first couple of games, it very quickly drags.  So on the whole, this is a very well made game and you're sure to lose a lot of hours to it - but some of the mechanics are ill-thought out and you may get much more rapidly tired of this game compared to the likes of other 4x games.

review_text: Short version: Sleeping Dogs in the Mad Max Universe. If you like either, go for it.  I got this as part of Humble Monthly, which is the primary reason I played it since I wouldn't have picked this up on my own, but I'm incredibly glad I did.  The game players entirely in third person except for a few short parts, and doesn't really suffer from any big camera issues in my opinion. Most of the controls are fairly responsive, although I had some issues with parrying sometimes not responding, and difficulty with finishers during combat, although that could have been a positioning issue on my part.  Melee combat is very strongly flavored as Sleeping Dogs - with more than a couple of enemies, you're far better off parrying if there isn't a ton of space, especially once melee weapon enemies are introduced, but against non-boss groups you can easily overpower them as you unlock more upgrades.  Upgrades are handled via an in-game currency, 'Scrap'. Conquering enemy camps will earn you steady scrap income to either upgrade Max ( fists for attacks, wrists for blocking / parrying, jacket for defense, bandolier for ammo, skills, appearance, shotgun, and the jimmy bar accessory ), or the vehicle you spend the game with ( engine, ram, wheels, suspension, armor, etc ).   There are several 'gates' where you are required to reach a minimum level of upgrades before you can proceed, to ensure that you aren't completely outclassed.  Gameplay itself is varied - there are races, base conquering, zone control, collectibles, and a series of side missions that reward you with extra vehicles, scrap, or unlocks like side burners or the final sniper rifle upgrade.  As far as the plot goes, it was pretty decent, although this essentially being a move tie-in leads to a heavy case of plot railroad towards the end. First and foremost: This game is free, so it's already going to get a positive review from me.  Second: It legitimately deserves a positive review.  I did enjoy this game, regardless of it being free. The plot is decent. Not spectacular, but not awful, either. It did give me a major case of the feels at points, which I consider a good thing. For the most part, the writing for the characters is good. It gets a little cliche at points, but considering it's an otome game, and there's only so much that could be deemed as 'original', I didn't consider this a bad thing. Some of the voice work is very nice, quality work. The game is short, but when dealing with visual novels with multiple endings, I generally view this as a good thing.  The cons are heavily in the artwork for me. It definitely felt rushed and unpolished, like the art staff ran out of time and submitted their rough work in. With more attention to detail put into the art, and perhaps more time, the game would feature something more pleasant to look at. That said, I liked the gradient effect that some of the characters had. I would have liked to have seen it carried over to each of the characters for a more uniform style. And, some of the voice work isn't as good as the rest. Some of it was bad acting, but some of it seemed to be poor equipment being used.  Overall, I liked this game, and played through two routes. I suggest playing it if you enjoy otome games.

review_text: I got this game for under $30 AUD off of ozgameshop, so thats about $50 cheaper for us aussies then getting it off of steam with their australia tax.   I dont know how i would feel about the game if i had payed full price, i probably would not have picked it up if it had been.   That said tho, the game is fun, and somewhat true to the mad max franchise.   The gameplay i would describe as a mix of borderlands and the arkham knight games.  Borderlands for the open world exploration feel, and level of freedom.  and arkham for its combat system (very batman but with less gadgets).  it also boasts some cool survival aspects, tho arguably some are poorly implimented.   -Water, you need to find water sources in order to properly heal, water is your lifeblood.   -Food, your on the fly instant heal stuff can be found rarely in camps and such good after a hard fight  and you need to heal fast but dont want to waste water.   -Fuel, this is for your car, this one is one of the poorly implimented features, in the sence that when you are out of gas  your car still magically drives, slowly but still drives, it would have made more sence for chumbucket (your side kick) to be pushing the car while you steer.   -Ammo, this is very important, its used to disable other vehicles or blow them up, kill snipers, and blow obsticals up as well as kill other enemy NPC's, this is by far the hardest to get early on and it can be very frustrating.   -Weather effects, there are storms for the most part they act as a minor inconvenience, the bigger ones come with risk/reward features in the way of loot box's you need to snag quickly while avoiding lightning strikes and howling winds.  The bigest draw back of the game would have to be the lack of multiplayer, this is a single player only title, hopefully they add a proper MP co-op element down the line.   The RPG elements in the way of upgrading max and his car is well done, and leveling seems smooth.   your biggest early hurdal will be scrap, its needed for everything, and until you have a good level of camps under your control and some stronghold upgrades this will take quite some time to get.   the open world feels alive and thats great, events overlap well, you could be doing a race and a warboy party comes to mess you up, or a sniper you havent dealt with yet takes out your wheels.   its a fun SP game and i reccomend it highly. just try as always find it for the best price.

Disclosure: I wrote an article for TechRaptor on this and got early access to produce a review for them.  Fans of the top-down shooter genre will like the progression system in this game and will enjoy the accuracy needed to go further in the game. Hitting zombies in the center results in more damage, and rewards accurate gameplay without being too reliant on it. The progression system makes your character really feel like your own: there are hundreds of combinations you can end up putting together from relying on sentries and the tower defense elements to protect you, to being a speedy shotgunner running around hitting everything.  The problem the game faces is a very long campaign that goes way too long, and the variety hits a very strong wall ast the environment and enemies don't have that much variety to it. The lack of guns variety hurts too, 4 base types are a little disappointing as the guns have a vast variety to them in terms of available stats. The art style isn't the greatest, and will get boring to look at.  Honestly though, it's one of those games that will be a challenge to see how far you can get, and if you like the top-down shooter genre, it's definitely worth a look. I will not sugar-coat this, X: Rebirth was absolutely terrible at launch, but the reason why I still recommend it is because I think now with all the updates, it has become a  decent game . If you don’t want to read the whole thing, then just go to the last paragraph on the bottom.  First of all, let’s consider the price. I think the current price of whooping 50€ is unjustified; it wasn’t justified at launch and it still isn’t justified now. What I would recommend to people thinking about buying this game – wait for the  CHRISTMAS SALE . I’m sure XR will be on sale this December, so even if you don’t like the game, you will have not spent too much money on it.  Many people already familiar with previous X games will say XR should have been X4. What you have to consider is that the developers never intended to make X4, they wanted to create a whole different game with different mechanics. The biggest problem here is that they (over) simplified  XR to appeal to wider audiences. This is the sole reason why XR does not feel like X4. They reduced the game complexities and I believe that cost them more than they thought it would. Many features were completely stripped from the game; the most debated being able to fly  any ship  reduced to  only one ship  in XR. Some features have been added now with patches though, which I find this ridiculous as they should have been in the game from the very start.  As I mentioned before, at launch the game was riddled with bugs, crashes, and whatnot; it was completely game-breaking. But I believe they fixed quite a large amount of those and the game is slowly shaping up into a decent game. For players thinking about getting this game, you should be aware that XR is  very sloooow . The developers wanted to make space exploration and economic empire building to be as realistic as possible, so even simple operations like ordering your ship to trade some wares, takes a while. They justify this by saying, “while you’re waiting for the trade to complete, go do / explore something else”. But after a while, this becomes terribly tedious and annoying, especially if you opt for building stations.  Building stations for your trade empire used to be much simpler and faster in previous X games. Now it just takes forever, and to top it off, it takes a vast amount of money and resources. Again probably to make it more realistic. Fleet control is hardly on par with X3, there’s less races, less star systems, trading can be grating at times while you’re waiting for something to happen, travelling times are longer, the plot is not really that great, the interactive menus are badly designed (improved with v 2.50), and the NPCs just suck.  BUT despite all this, I still invested 300+ hours into XR because I enjoy space simulators. The vast  space cities  they designed are stunning and gorgeous to explore, the universe really does feel alive, capital ships and freighters are also badass and I enjoy capturing and collecting them, watching your stations expand and earn you more money feels good, manoeuvring around stations at high speed looks awesome and your choices can really affect the universe.  Hopefully with the release of XR 3.0, new features/improvements will make the game more enjoyable.  In the end, it’s all a matter of taste. If you enjoy space simulations or you’re a hardcore space gamer, I think XR would be a good game to try. But if you are a casual player not looking for slow and complicated in-depth gaming, then you’re better off playing a space shooter or fast-paced strategy game.

review_text: Early Access Review

From playing a full game as America and beginning a new game as the Aztecs, these are basically my early impressions since Civilization is such a complex game to really have a final verdict on without hours of gameplay experience.  Pros:  -I like most of the changes that have been made to the mechanics. The district system is great, providing more strategy and choice compared to Civ 5. -Strategic resources work waaaaay better this time around. Instead of using one up each time you build a unit, You just need a certain amount of that strategic resource to actually build the unit. Then you can build that unit as much as you want without exhausting the strategic resourse. (Ex. You only require 2 iron to make as swordsman, once you have that amount you can make as many as you want) -I like the new graphics style. The attention to detail is fantastic. Zooming up on a wonder or a pair of knights really shows you the effort they put in. The more vibrant graphics also give the game more personality. This also includes the world leaders. They are animated beautifully and really give off more personality than compared to their Civ 5 counterparts. -Combat is improved from Civ 5 (though the A.I. still suck at it). The promotion system provides more choices for specializing your units combat abilities. Cities have garrisons now that basically provides another health bar, but takes more damage from siege units. This obviously makes the siege units more useful than before. -Religion is waaay more fleshed out this time around and its great. I'm trying to achieve a Religious victory in my Aztec game right now and it's going to be a blast when the religion war heats up between me and Brazil. -The policies and government types provide even more customization and specialization to your Civilization, really letting you play the way you want to play. -Sean Bean saying 'I'll be back.'  Cons:  -The A.I. is broken right now. They are overaggresive and will literally hate you for anything you do... For some reason there seems to be way more ways for the A.I. to hate you than to like you. There is also a bug where Civs will get ♥♥♥♥♥♥ at you for having units near their borders when you actually haven't even found any of their cities yet. It is a huge challenge to actually get a Civ to be friendly with you. I managed this in my first game with Arabia. I liberated two of their cities from Brazil and wiped out all of Brazil's army within their territory. I ended up killing a spy of Arabia's after all of that and made them promise not to spy on me again. Brazil agreed and I still caught a spy in my capitol later on. Thanks jerk. -Bugs, bugs, and more bugs. The game isn't broken, but it is a very buggy mess right now. I even had a bug that basically screwed me out of my game as China, but fortunately it was early game. I ended up getting invaded by two civs early on as China while I was focusing on production and wonders. I didn't find it a big deal since I had just finished an encampment (which allows me to build swordsmen with one iron instead of two, since I only had one iron at the time). Plus my production was crazy so I could pump out a swordsman every two turns. I would be fine right? Nope, the game glitched and wouldn't let me produce any swordsmen, saying that I needed two iron to make one or that I needed to make an encampment(even though I had one already) to make them with only one. There was nothing I could do to defend myself against the onslaught and I had to scrap that game. -You can't remove districts from tiles, why can't you remove districts from tiles?? -As I mentioned before the A.I. really suck at combat. They will completely disregard some of their units they have for some reason and not even use them when attacking you or defending themselves. -The U.I. is nice but for some reason lacks a lot of important and useful information. For example, when looking at the city list and the wonders and buildings I have, why doesn't it display the description for the buildings or wonders? The game is brand new and I'm not going to remember everything each building or wonder does specifically.  -Games take forever to load for some reason (GTX 970 and i5 4690k 3.5 Ghz). So my specs are more than capable enough to run the game well. It is also installed on my SSD.  It's difficult for me to come up with a score for the game right now since Civilization VI is such a large and complex game, but I can say if you are a huge Civ fan it is worth picking up right now for the awesome gameplay additions and changes to the formula. If your'e not a fan of the franchise or turn-based genre then I would wait a bit and let the bug fixes and DLC come out first, which is guaranteed to make the game a much better experience.  Edit(few months after release):  There have been multiple updates now and the new Poland civ is nice but unfortunately the shoddy A.I. problems still remain. It's so unfortunate because this has the potential to be such an amazing game. It's just too hard to ever befriend a civ because of the A.I. traits and their anti-war stance. Even civs that want me to be at war denounce me because I declare war on someone who is settling on my borders! What?? I am going to have to change my review to a thumbs down until these A.I. issues are resolved. It ruins what can really be an amazing 10/10 strategy game.

review_text: Early Access Review
Early Access Review

Aliens versus Predator Classic 2000 is an example of how to do First-Person-Shooters right. The fast gameplay requires more skill and focus than most modern First-Person-Shooters. Instead of trying to level up your character to get OP weapons, most weapons have a legitimate purpose, and most of them don't feel too overpowered (except Disc, Pred Pistol, Skeeter, and Sadar. Not to forget Mines). Instead of grinding to get perks to power yourself up, Aliens versus Predator requires skill from the start.  Aliens versus Predator Classic 2000 [Which I will be calling 'AvP' through out the rest of this review] allows the player to take control of three species, Aliens, Predators, or the human Marines. Each have their advantages and disadvantages.  The Alien is a melee only species, a hurtful disadvantage, but is patched up by the speed and wallcrawling ability that the Aliens have. Aliens also can smell the scents of other beings, allowing it to see its victims in the dark. The Alien regains health by headbiting Predators and Humans, you can do this on both the living and the dead. Obviously headbiting live beings is harder than dead ones, but gains back more health.  The Predator is a mix of Melee and ranged weapons, the majority of his weapons, Medicomp, and cloak are powered by 'Field Charge'. This will auto-recharge if you are not using anything that uses charge, or you can find field-charge items around the map which will give you full energy. The only weapons that don't use energy are the Wristblades, and Disc (Unless you use the recall ability, that will use a small amount of energy), as well as the Speargun. But this uses ammo, which only replenishes upon death. The Predator can heal using his Medicomp, this will use a large amount of energy, but puts you back to full health everytime (TIP: Secondary fire of Medicomp will put out fires). In some of the bonus levels the Predator gains a new gadget, the grappling hook, which can pull him to higher heights. The Predator also has three vision modes, which can help him spot enemies. The first vision mode can help spot humans, the second aliens, and the third other Predators and field-charges (Of course you have the default normal vision).  The Marine is a ranged only species, guns guns and more guns. The Marine replenishes health with medkits, found about through-out levels. Armor, which can protect against Alien acid and absorbs small amounts of damage. The Marine has a Motion Tracker, if anything moves, a small dot and a beep sound will appear on it. But it isn't always reliable, if ANYTHING moves it will beep. In some of the bonus levels the Marines get a jetpack, allowing them to fly to spaaaaace! In multiplayer Marines can select a specialist sub-class, this will allow them to pick from any of the weapons in the game (unless game host restricted it), and you will always spawn with the selected weapon, but you can only use that weapon (and the pistol).  Each species can unlock bonus missions after you complete the main game, (you will unlock more if you beat it on a higher difficulty) these levels are the levels from other species singleplayer, for example: You can unlock a backwards version of the Predator's first level as Marine if you beat the Marine SP on Director's cut [Hard]. Pretty cool!  Not to forget, you can unlock cheat options in the game by completing certain objectives through-out the levels. These can be things like slow-mo whenever you are near enemies. Or unlimited speargun ammo and more spears per shot as Predator.  The music will change depending on what species you are playing as, Predator will recall images of the jungle, Marines have a very militaristic beat, while Aliens have a slow and creepy tone. The music really sets the tone well, but I wouldn't find myself listening to it outside the game.  The Multiplayer is where this game shines most. Fast gameplay and the different play-styles of each species make for very fun and memorable experience. This is one of the fastest games ever, you will die, fast, and a lot. It is extremly well balanced, and I almost never feel like a player is winning because of the species they are playing as. But, unfortunatly, this game has a small player base, so at times you may not be able to find a game. Because of this the community doesn't play competitively (most of the time), anymore. Servers will also usually have a small amount of players, 8-player games are kinda rare, but when they happen, it is sooooo fun!  Aliens versus Predator Classic 2000 is easily one of the fastest games, and one of the best, ever made.  You would be doing yourself a disservice by not playing this game!  Why are you still reading this review, go buy it and play it?!

review_text: This review is coming from a stand-point of about 7 hours of solo play.  Do I like this game? It's okay, I mean, it's nothing special, it's a twin-stick zombie survival game with a somewhat interesting crafting system.  The game-play, it's basic and easy to understand as all twin-stick games are. Nothing new. Which is totally fine, I mean it's a tried and tested system.  The story, I haven't finished the story, simply because I hit too many BDN (Board, done now) moments. I just couldn't do another fetch quest. That's all this game is, fetch quest after fetch quest after fetch quest. When did I hit my limit? When the drunk pilot wanted his cargo, where was it? All over the islands... All twenty pieces of the damn stuff. I quit, I was done with the game from then on. The characters were, eh. The only person that interested my in any way was Kovac, mainly because SPOILER ALERT SPOILER ALERT OVER. I say apparently because I have no idea if he is or not, though if that was him on the radio at the start of the game it makes sense as he seems to have a radio station at the top of his island. I digress. The characters are cliche to say the least, oh no a mother and daughter that are split up on a terrible island, the friend who was bitten but has the thing you need to get off the island, the crazy old lady, the apparent friend who is actually the villain, the disabled knowledgeable one who spends all their time tinkering with your escape rout and the drunk.  May as well look at this games big point, the crafting. It's okay. I mean you can make things, cool yeah, okay. I never bothered with the potions. The guns? Didn't bother with them either. Melee weapons? Rusty machete all the way. After I built the boomerang I just didn't need anything else. Nothing. And when I upped it with the red stone, nothing else would do. Now when I say I didn't bother with it, I mean I built every combination I could within 7 hours of play. Tested it, and still found the boomerang to be the best thing to use. Chainsaw? Nope. Flamethrower? Nope. Shotgun? Nope. Boomerang. Always.  Survival. Pha. My biggest problem surviving was diverting my attention from my current fetch quest to trek to water holes, or where I knew deer were or to safe houses when I got a little tired. Fuel for bombs was the biggest problem for my 'survival'. I had so much food on my person I could have fed all the survivors I had found with ease. Three jerry cans of water kept me going for days. Ammo was of no concern, I had stacks of the stuff on every island just sitting there.   The zombies, or should I say, 'Monsters'. Well there are ones you can kill in one hit, ones that have helmets, ones that have body armor, ones with both, tall ones, ones that come out at night, ones that explode, ones in fireman suits, zombie deers, zombie emus, and a big old boss thing. At first the ones that came out at night were a challenge but then, boomerang one hit kill. The ones with both pieces of armor are just annoying, takes two head shots to kill them, one to knock off the helmet and one to kill 'em. And when they're coming at you in a pack, it's just easier to run off and come back later. The firemen, hint hint, head shots, lots and lots of head shots unless you got yourself a means of using electricity. The deers just run at you, shoot twice, dodge, rinse and repeat. The boss thing is just like a very very weekend version of the Tank from L4D. They just seemed like distractions from the endless fetch quests.  Oh yeah side quests are, you guessed it, fetch quests. -_-  The camera angle is lovely, seeing the exploders just as they enter their area of effect is just dandy.  The lock on and head-shot system is good too, when your backing away from a group with your trusty boomerang out and it's just about to go red and another zombie bumps your target out of your aim and you gotta start over again. This is especially annoying against the fully armored zombies. Great.  The leveling system seems just, pointless, the most useful stuff is getting the upgrades that slow down your hunger, thirst and drowsiness. Everything else seems just like fluff to me.  The most fun I had in the game was going off doing the missions for the scientists on the islands where I was super-under prepared, there was a challenge, and it was ridiculous. Experiments that could change the magnetic fields surrounding the islands? Sounds good enough to me!  Is this game worth the price tag, maybe in co-op. Maybe. However would I play though those opening missions again? Hell no.

Housewife Simulator 1995  Seriously though, this game is not very good when compared to other adventure games of the era. Although the FMV is very entertaining and I quite enjoyed the cheesy acting, the gameplay is basic and highly repetitive, forcing you to search the same rooms inside and outside the mansion every single chapter. There are a limited number of items you'll find, no dialog choices, no complicated puzzles, and no different cursors you can use to interact with things. You have one context sensitive cursor that does everything.  And boy, is it a slowwwww burner. It takes a good long while for things to start getting really interesting and to see any actual horror. If the game had been well paced, I would've enjoyed this, but it's so incredibly slow right up until the ending, which throws so much at you at once, that it's quite jarring.  And in my mind, this is one of the most poorly designed endings I've seen in this genre. It actually deletes your savegame once it enters the ending sequence, and if you don't have all the items necessary to complete it (including one in a hidden area I never even knew existed) there's nothing you can do about it but start the chapter over. I got so frustrated with it I decided to watch it on YouTube instead, and shook my head at the sequence of things you're just supposed to figure out in a panic as you continue to watch the same intense FMV scenes over and over again.  Police Quest, Space Quest, Gabriel Knight, Day of the Tentacle, Monkey Island, King's Quest, Quest for Glory--pick any one of these '90s adventures and you'll have a way better time than Phantasmagoria. Props to the production team and the actors, but I have to say, this one's a dud.

review_text: Not good enough.

Unfortunately, I have to echo the sentiments of a lot of other reviewers here. XCom2 delivers a great core game, playing off of the strengths of its predecessors, but a few design flaws (and a LOT of bugs and graphical issues) have sunk the game experience for me.  People have already outlined those issues in detail, but a short list:  EVERYTHING IS ON A TIMER (EVEN THE WORLD MAP). The aliens are working on the 'Avatar Project' (which might as well be named the Deus Ex Machina project), which ticks forward, often out of your control. God help you if you want to try to get anything done, because you'll be spending all your time on...  RESOURCE DROPS. Yes, to get any supplies, you'll now have to sit over them, like a hen laying on an egg, to get any resources. However, you won't be able to do this, because...  EVERYTHING IS ALWAYS ALARMING ALL THE TIME. Missions pop up all the time and while you're sometimes given a choice as to which one to do, you damned sure better do ONE of them, and best of luck because...  YOU'RE S.O.L. IF YOUR A-TEAM DIES. I don't mind difficult games. In fact, I like the permadeath nature of XCom; it gives your missions and decisions weight and I'll admit having to fight the urge to save-scum when one of my favorite soldiers died. However, the random nature of your missions means that it's entirely possible to get three of those 'not mandatory but actually pretty mandatory' missions outlined above, and if you take any damage at all, you'll have to bring in fresh recruits. Even by mid-campaign, Squaddies and Corporals are effectively useless because...  THE COVER MECHANICS AND TO HIT NUMBERS MAKE NO SENSE. I take a reasonably seasoned corporal with a Tier 2 railgun, put him directly next to a muton with no cover at all under the effects of suppression, and my to hit is... 18%. What? Are XCom soldiers half-stormtrooper? A big giant unit with big giant HP and armor is apparently faster than a speeding bullet. You'll be surprised when a stun lancer hits you literally through a wall because...  THE COVER MECHANICS MAKE LITERALLY NO SENSE. Hitting through walls? a 20% to hit percentage difference on two of the same creatures literally right next to each other? Sure, why not. I don't care anymore.  I almost never buy anything on launch day, and like I said, I loved XCom EU/EW, and I was excited for this game. But there's a difference between difficulty and gimmick, and the challenge here is not winning the game by playing it; it's winning the game in spite of the game itself.  For now, I'm waiting on a patch to resolve the glitches and bugs (of which there are many), and to fine-tune the numbers, before I download some of the (excellent) mods and go back for another playthrough. There IS an excellent game here, I just hope Firaxis is able to squash enough of the bugs to create the game that should have been there at launch.

review_text: Eurotruck Simulator - Motion Sickness Edition  The game is perfectly controllable with keyboard and mouse, just make sure to change the default setting, which are useless. This guide helped me a lot:  - Change the mouse x axis from roll to yaw - Set Q/E to roll left/right - Set W/S/A/D to up/down/left/right thrusters - Set R/F to forward/reverse thrusters - Set your mouse wheel to control speed; what I mean by this is to click in the 'increase throttle' binding box and roll the mouse wheel up, and do the same with 'decrease throttle' and roll your mouse wheel down. - Set speed increments to 25%. You'll never need more fine grain control than that. (I also set shift/ctrl as secondary binds for these)

review_text: I bought this game back somewhere between late 2012 and the middle of 2013. The combat was excellent; probably one of the best melee systems I've seen in a game - and that was only bolstered by the fact that the gore was brutally fantastic. It was ineffably satisfying to hack someone's arm off with a huge, heavy two-handed axe and then come back around with another swing while they're still clutching at their socket and take their head off. Combat was heavy and precise, the maps were cool, and the music was pretty good. So why am I not recommending it?  You'll remember I bought this game late 2012-mid 2013. Basically nothing has changed about this game since then; there's been a few miscellaneous tweaks and fixes, but there's nothing  new . No new maps, no new weapons, no new music; they never fixed the fact that you can't change the time limit on your own dedicated server, so you have to deal with the map trying to switch itself every ten minutes. Also, something changed about the combat along the way; I can't exactly pinpoint it, but it doesn't feel as fluid and responsive as it used to.  At some point they decided to stop making big, important changes and additions to the game and start implementing new helmets, and new armors, and new weapons skins. Cool! So what's the catch? The catch is that you absolutely cannot acquire   any   of these by legitimately paying the game. See that fancy helmet? That'll be $5. See that cool skin for your zweihander? That'll be another $5. There's so many microtransactions in this game that I wouldn't be surprised if it was possible to drop over $100 on all this superfluous, superficial stuff that doesn't really make a difference to the overall experience. I understand TB has to make money, but there should be much more to every update than just more skins you can purchase and a few tweaks - it's pretty ridiculous at this point.   It's sad to see the game go this way, as I made some great memories with this game and enjoyed the experience, and I'd say I got my money's worth; but I'd probably unbuy it if I could. They even put out a Deadliest Warrior DLC, and I abstained from buying it since I knew it was just going to be abandoned as well.  If you're new and you're reading this review and you're considering purchasing this game, I'm not here to dissuade you. Chivalry is an excellent experience and still has some of the best melee combat I've ever seen in a game, and I've definitely had my fair share of good times with it. But if you're buying it and you expect the current core experience to grow and evolve, absolutely do not buy this. It hasn't changed in the past two years, and I suspect it never will.

** See my comment below   Single player only review.  Doom (2016) is truly worthy of carrying the legendary name. It feels like a spiritual successor to Doom 2, with a few bits of Doom 3 and Quake (Quad Damage baby!) thrown in.  This is in the same fine caliber of remakes/reboots as Wolfenstein: New Order, which is one of the best single player FPS games I've played in recent years.  The FPS mechanics are close to perfect. At first I frowned at the double jumping and ledge climbing, but soon realized how these mechanics helped keep the pace of the fights high, as your great freedom of movement on all 3 axis pretty much always leaves you somewhere to go, even when you're not familiar with the layout of the map.   The level design is actually pretty reminiscent of Doom 2, with both narrow corridors and large open areas. Plenty of secret areas to explore, which gets rewarded well.  The good old gang of monsters and weapons are back (with some additions), and they all look, sound and handle superbly. This is pure no-nonsense, balls to the wall, Doom action with just a bare minimum of story and cutscenes.   Generally you'll face fewer, but tougher versions of the hordes from hell. The 'Baron of Hell' is pretty fearsome now, so it feels better than ever to pick up a 'Beserk' powerup, put a grin on your face and blast your 1-hit death machine fist into his sorry face.  The game is impressively true to it's herritage, even though the old dog HAS learned a few modern tricks...   Health and ammo no longer has to come from traditional pickups. Damage a monster so it almost dies, and it staggers for some seconds, during which you can perform a 'Glory Kill' by using your melee attack. This triggers a badass gory kill animation, and results in some dropped health.  The trusty old chainsaw has become a fuel guzzler, but in return it makes the sliced foes drop ammo. The tougher the enemy is to cut through, the more fuel the saw consumes.  All weapons (except for the pistol) have 2 unlockable modifications, and each of these mods have 4 upgrades. The first three upgrades are unlocked by spending weapon upgrade tokens, which are rewarded by fulfilling challenges, finding secrets and having some sort of algoritm evaluate your performance at the end of each level. The final upgrade level of each gun is unlocked by completing a challenge related to the usage of that specific weapon mod. Some of the mods are friggin' kickass, and really help keep things fresh and varied as you progress.  Your suit too has upgrades. Four categories of them actually, and these are unlocked via Praetor tokens, which are found on the corpses of Elite Guards.  Even passive 'perks' are introduced in the form of 'Runes' which are unlocked on special challenge maps accessed through portals. These runes can also be upgraded by fulfilling challenges. You start with 1 rune slot, but can unlock up to 3 slots.  And finally your max health, armor and ammo capacity is upgraded by finding and collecting 'Argent Cells' which are spread across the maps.  At the end of each level you can see how many exploration items you missed.  Overall the presentation is great. The id Tech engine performs excellent, so the awesome detailed artwork never once stuttered on MAX settings in 1200p on my GTX 980ti. I loved the industrial/metal themed soundtrack, but I reckon it might annoy the more faint eared folks out there :) It sets the mood perfectly regardless.  One of my few complaints is that I would've loved a thick layer of badass cutscene driven story full of colorful characters. If it can be done in a Wolfenstein remake, it can be done in the Doom universe as well.  The game crashed to desktop 4-5 times during my 10 hour playthrough, which never happened for me with previous id Tech powered titles.  That aside, it's my pick for best single player FPS of the year. Well done id Software - you're still a force to be reckoned with.  93%

review_text: Note: I have played about 14 hours in total (about 64% through the game on my playthrough) however I played it mostly in Offline mode due to internet connection problems.  First of all: This game is remarkable; the graphics look stunning and the gameplay feels fluid and most of the times not staggered. The Parkour is alright to say the least, it isn't Mirror's Edge but it is tied in well enough to be fun most of the time - I have seen many people have problems with the port but I have to say that I have found no problems with any lag (Running on a GTX 760 4gb) and because I do not have an AMD card or any GTX 900 series cards - the main cause for concern with this title, I can only state that it should work fine with the 700 series.  The story is lackluster at best and leaves alot to be desired; it starts to pick up pace from about half way onwards but it still is nothing to write home about. The voice acting of Crane is monotone and at times obnoxious and later on he begins to put in more emphasis when more dramatic plot points come up but equally can't save it.  The combat is basically a more refined version of Dead Island's with more fluid animations and better feeling weapons that actually have a sort of weight behind them, when you decapitate a zombie with a knife or bash its head in with a pipe the Sound, Slow-Mo and X-Ray compliment it without it being in the way of actual combat.  Atmospherically this game has nailed it - being out at night is tense, especially at the start of the game when you have barely any decent weapons, and at day when you can see everything the city is eerie yet beautiful, nothing really seems out of place. I get a sort of Resident Evil 1 vibe from it at some points where I know something will happen, but I don't know when. The horror in the game is purely atmospheric, nothing will jump out at you and scream but the enviroments and lighting (or lack there-of) can be intimidating at times.  Finally, the tone is a massive contrast to Dead Island. Where Dead Island was colourful and vibrant, this game was dark and gritty; we talk about the infamous Dead Island trailer but at times I think that the trailer for that would fit this game better - still not perfect but much better because of the tone of the game.  In Conclusion; this game is incredible - it has it's flaws, as does everything, but it is already one of the best modern games that I have played and it will take alot to top it.

This game has a reliance on cheap and ineffectual horror tropes.  Jumpscares only startle people if they don't expect it coming. Horror is more than jump scares, it's anticipation and both well timed delivery and denial. Outlast isn't scary at all, it's jumpscares are predictible and dated. Jumpscares themselves have always been weak, at least to me. At least in my opnion, true horror is something that is patient, careful and slow to execute. The faster something happens the weaker the effect. Ultimately an ideal horror writer would want your own imagination to become your worst enemy. The environment is there to facilitate your own dread manifesting things that are not there. But your tension makes even the simplest thing seem magnitudes worse. I studied horror in literature, traditional art mediums, interactive (like games) and cinema when I was in university and I can say that I've got at least an academic understanding of the nature of horror.  As a game, outlast is functional (It's solid, easy to tweak, never crashes, has good load times and mecanically is fantastic!) if very, very tired. I've been trying to play through it to the end but...it's agonizingly boring. It delivers too much on what you expect to happen, the environment is stale (A 'haunted' asylum? *sigh*). I really, really want to enjoy good horror but what most developers and writers consider to be horror is actually just shock. What is truly scary is unknown, ambiguity and mystery. When you don't know what is pursuing you, when you feel something lurking around the corner. Something that darts by, out of sight, barely noticed. In a game many horror elements would need to be in places where you could easily miss them and if you do, well...you missed them. If they are geared to deliver in a place where you're sure to see them, they don't have any effect such as a window or a door that you're reaching for.  At least in my opnion, for something to be genuinely scary, it needs to create an atmosphere that is terrifying FAR beyond any scary entity (ghost, monster, demon, maniac, etc.). I should be more afraid to 'be there' than I am afraid anything that's dangerous. If I'm terrified of the environment than anything that happens (or better yet 'doesn't' happen, part of the anticipation) will be significantly more intense. Horror and fear is an art that is not well practiced by movies and games. In the end, for me anyway, this game is more an exercise in 'beating' it. Defeating the game at it's own game. I don't need weapons to defeat maniacs, only patience to evade or outlast (hah) them.   As a final note, if you think I may be rushing, I'm not. I like to take my time in a game, to appreciate every detail that went into it's design. Especially in a horror, I want to get immersed to make the scares more personal but this game is so painfully predictible and it's lack of any actual scary content or supernatural forces makes it no scarier than walking down a crowded street in a metropolis at noon (which for agoraphobics is scary, so I'll give it that). I spend a lot of time looking around and the enemies are something I casually dodge around while still idly exploring 'chase' scenes. It's like the whole game moves in slow motion.  Let's hope the second one actually makes a game about ghosts and scary stuff. Real monsters, not just maniacs.

review_text: Early Access Review

First impressions is It's a beautiful game with a great soundtrack, but frustrating and just not fun. I'm not going to complain about the common issues. Since everyone else has complained about stuff like the UI and clunkyness and everything, there's no point in doing so. DFS feels like it doesn't know if it wants to be an RPG or a Rogue-like. You save your game in town (and there's a bit of an auto-save feature when you leave town), you level up and get stats and allows you to use items of your level, and you generally do RPG-esk stuff. Then you go and do a quest, which is where the roguelike stuff comes into play. Standard turn-based, map stuff. You do stuff, then the monsters do stuff, whatever. It doesn't seem that terrible on the surface, but it's how these two elements mix together.  There's a backstab system, but for a good portion of the monsters, it's hard to determine where the back is (you just have to assume, sometimes). The inability to equip any weapon or time you come across can really mess with you, especially if the item in question would help you not die. Exploring the entire map seems to be a mistake (much like a rogue-like), but if you don't, you'll be underleveled and under-equipped for the next quest (and I haven't found a good way to grind yet). If you die, you are reverted back to your last save, losing everything you had gotten that far (no progress after death, which is very unlike a rogue-like) and you can spend upwards to 20 minutes on these boards, only to screw yourself, die, and then lose everything.  I'd like to give this game more time to develope, see if after a few patches some of the issues with it are addressed, but right now I can't recomend the DFS because, currently, it just isn't fun.

review_text: This seems to be an attempt at blending Chess and Warhammer 40k, two great games, but in reality, it falls apart. The tutorial, which you can't skip, just points you to a bunch of confusing on-screen icons without explaining what they mean or what they do. When you finally get to play a game, you find that the rules of chess sometimes apply, but sometimes not - one of the biggest flaws is that putting the enemy leader into checkmate doesn't actually do anything - the Warboss, despite being in checkmate by a Terminator, was perfectly content to sit there, gunning down my Librarian and Assault Marine until I finally felled him with a sustained barrage of bolter fire.   Yes, the cathartic joy of perforating Orks with rocket-propelled rounds is obtainable here, and the graphics are excellent. But the voice acting is minimal - I think they have two, maybe three people doing all the voices. And since the actual rules of chess seem to be applied randomly here, anyone looking for that aforementioned cathartic joy is much better off with the many older 40k games available.   At least it's cheap.

Honestly, I think I would only play this game with friends where we can constantly communicate, or literally are in the same room together. I wouldn't want to play this with strangers. Why? Well, because with friends it would be a fun activity. This game isn't designed in a way that makes level grinding (which is what you need to get your characters anywhere) fun.  Also.... Every hunter can dome now? What's up with that? It was hard enough before with that dome. Now everyone can drop one? There's no way the monster's going to be able to escape with that going on. I agree with some of the other commenters- this just takes stealth out of the game entirely, which is one of the things I liked most about playing the monster. Listening to my friends furiously search for me while I'm on the other side of the map eating some random wildlife was fun. Getting trapped in a dome constantly will not be fun. Also.... some of these new abilities and names are clearly stolen from other games. And that's without considering the losses some people faced when this game updated: the founders who spent hours and hours within this game, leveling up, only to have it erased at the update. I can totally understand their salt. Maybe at some point, this game will update to Evolve: Stage 3 and be worthwhile again.

A very straight-forward little shoot'em up. The most noteworthy thing about this game are certainly the looks, which I immediately liked. You can tell a lot of work went into the landscape, effects, and models, and it looks spectacular.   There is no way to heal your ship between levels (at least as far in as I got), which makes not getting hit the most important objective and can be a bit unforgiving in the beginning. The gameplay is not unfair, however, and chiefly about skill. Apart from this, the score/coin system is a fun game mechanic: The score points are also the in-game currency. This makes for an interesting dilemma after each level, because upgrading the ship decreases your score and because you get 10% interest on your score between levels. The keyboard and mouse controls are spot-on, no complaints here, although a gamepad might work even better.

I played this game from open beta and loved it. After Mirkwood came out a few things in my life had me put LotRO on hold. I came back about 2 months ago and it feels like a different game. That being said, it's not a bad thing, just different from what I remember. Not dramatically different but enough to make it interesting. It is still one of the best MMOs I've ever played (going all the way back to Ultima Online before EA or whoever got a hold of it and ♥♥♥♥ed it all up).   The game follows the books pretty closely (and not the movies). So if you like Tolkien and are looking to get into a MMO, this is the right place for you. Even if you haven't read the books and just seen the Hobbit and Rings trilogies, there is enought in the game for you to say, 'Ah, I remember that!'   The game is a little old but that doesnt take away from its charm. It being older means the the graphics are not all HD/3D/VR-esque but are not too bad in hindsight. The story and quests drive you to want to go to the next part of the game and leveling is not that bad. The quests make it interesting to the point that it doesn't feel like a constant grind. Not to say that there isn't a grinding element to it.....it is a MMO after all.  I'd give it a 9/10, just because I'm a fan of the books and they actually took the time to get it right.
