Look, look, I know what you're thinking. 'Euro Truck Simulator 2? Why the f*** would anyone pay money for that?' I get it. There are a lot of crappy sim games out there by a lot of shady little companies that crash two-out-of-three times and feature buggy physics or awful AI. ETS2 is not one of these games. Not even a little bit.  I should note here that I have no particular attachment to trucks; they're just things to me. I do not eagerly purchase trucks of one brand or another, I don't have Scania stickers on my computer case or posters of Volvo FH16s hanging over my bed.   To me, this game's attraction comes from how ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ RELAXING it is.  Seriously, just try driving down the German autobahn at night in the rain, with the game's fully-integrated internet radio playing. That radio, by the way, is what makes this game truly great -- having all sorts of radio stations from all over Europe available really helps the immersion. Sooner or later you'll hit a sort of trance where the highway and the radio lull you nearly to sleep -- deadly on the real road, but unbelievably enjoyable sitting comfortably at home.  If you don't enjoy being relaxed (why?) the game also features a pretty nice career mode and a great business simulation, so you can have fun with that if you're boring or something.  This is a game that shouldn't be great, I understand. But it really is.

review_text: Awesomenauts is a fast paced brawl type game you can play with up to 3 people in a 2d side scroller. The game overall is one of the most unique games i've seen yet. This game however has so many things wrong with it i just can not find anymore enjoyment. Over the amount of time i've played, i tried each character multiple times and found many things.  There are so many issues with Awesomenauts, it's horrid. There are normal complaints about the game like a typical moba would recieve, but this game gets special complaints due to it's aspect.  This game i would not reccomend if you are returning or first picking up. Please do not. Unless you want to get placed in unfair matches with people who know far more superior tactics than you, you'll be left behind spending hours upon hours just learning the game. The spawn, where you have to control a little ship to collect the currency which is called solar is fantastic, but new players have absolutely no idea what is going on. The patterns are different from map to map, but this also makes it even harder for new or returning players to pick up on. The starting solar allows you to get a what is basically worth a kill. When you die, you lose 30 solar which isn't a terrible amount, but it does really hurt. It effectively puts the enemy team ahead 160 solar ahead due to each player getting 30 solar for killing someone, then 40 extra solar to whoever killed that person, and finally the person that died loses that 30 solar which equals the 160 solar. After a couple kills, say about 5, your team is behind 800 solar. It's insanely difficult to ever come back in a game like this. Just the solar amount is so unbalanced, a single kill can honestly make or break the game.  The match making in this game is borderline OK. If it were to be any worse, i don't think this game would be playable. I'm constantly being placed in matches where people live many miles away, recieving a lot of ping. This causes host advantage. Basically if your not the host, or they aren't close, good luck. Especially with all the debuffs that can be applied to a person, you'll be so locked down, you might as well surrender. Also you can join a game where it will basically be ending. You can literally drop leagues so fast and it would be impossible to do anything about it.  The characters in this game however are fun and have their own playstyle, they're unbalanced. A lot of people disagree with this, but some characters are just a little OP. Characters like clunk, skolldir, and derpl have larger bodies so they have a larger body block potential. This makes large mass characters have a huge advantage, where as people like Vinnie, Gnaw, and Leon much harder to body block. Now i'm not saying Body mass is what makes a person OP, it's just it gives them an advantage. Some higher damage people like raelynn, leon, and froggy, all do massive amounts of damage, but some just do more in general. Vinnie has a very high damage dash that is an AOE which could effectively be x3 damage of it's base damage instead of just 1. Vinnie alone can reach insane amounts of damage. Leon is supposed to be a stealthy assassin, but he really doesn't do a whole lot of damage since almost any debuff he recieves destroys his playstyle. he heavily relys on his basic attacks which is basically leon in a nut shell, but he does about the same amount of damage with his basic attacks as a vinnie. I could honestly make a massive list of just all the unbalanced things, it'd take a long time to make.   TL;DR: Awesomenauts is unique, and fast. It's a 2d side scrolling brawl game supports up to 3 people on either team. New or returning players are heavily punished for not spending time in the game. Matchmaking, majority of the time is terrible, and no dedicated servers. Unbalanced match making, making it unfair for new and returning players to play. Unbalanced characters. A single target damage dealer does effectively 2x-3x less damage than A AOE target damage dealer

review_text: Early Access Review
Early Access Review
So borring !!!

The recent years have seen the rise of 'retro'-style games, which seemingly attempt to recreate the look and feel of old games. From this, we've gotten countless NES—er, '8-bit' style games, whether they were created to honestly bring back the feel of an '8-bit' game or just to have an 'excuse' to give for horrible artwork. Yet, very few designers have ever done the same with other consoles—or old computers. Daniel Remar (the creator of Iji) adds to this niche with MURI, a side-scrolling platformer which takes after the first Duke Nukem from the DOS shareware era of 1991 but adds and fine-tunes many features to make it its own game. Everything here feels authentic, from the PC speaker sounds to the choppy scrolling (which can be changed to be smooth from the Options menu if you feel like it) to the EGA palette (nothing in the game uses any more than the EGA card's standard 16 colors).  But it's a fun game, too, with movement that feels more fluid than you'd think it'd be and a difficulty balance that feels about right. The levels are of the exploratory kind instead of a linear sort, and it feels great when you find the way to an item that seems just out of reach. It's a rather short game, though, with five levels for each of the four episodes (a touch of the shareware era), even if they get longer as you progress.  Overall, it's a successful return to early 90s DOS and good way to spend ~2 hours of free time. The $3.99 price tag doesn't hurt, either!

Definitely a fun game, just not worth 50 USD (5,050 YEN). This game puts the difficulty setting (easy, normal, hard, really hard, and inferno) right in your face on every single level, which will change this game from a fun 'movie come to life' experience to a merciless grind for decent guns. Example: You do mission #20 on easy, you get level 1 guns. So, it's pointless to do any missions below the hard setting. Plus, any weapons you find, they can't be used in the middle of a mission. Yup, you gotta wait to use any weapons you find until the next mission. Finally, if you die before you kill EVERY enemy, you don't get squat (no saving mid-mission).  All of that is not the reason this game is not worth 50 bucks. I'll tell you why it's not worth the asking price: This game is sooo outdated. It's like the year 2002 all over again. I swear, if it were possible to download this game on a PS2, that console could run this game. People love the fact they can run this game on their crappy computers, as they should. --EDIT-- I can't explain the AMD issue. Hope the Devs fix it for y'all. But those of us with decent rigs won't benefit at all from the dumbed down graphics, the late 90's menu screens, and the 1st Gen UI. I guess ya gotta do what ya gotta do to get so much packed into 1 screen.  Alright, I'm done hating on this game. I really do like it. There is actually a lot of strategy to go along with the mindless killin'. Four different characters to choose from, each unique in their own way, adds much variety to finishing a mission. This game is an awesome co-op game (if you have friends who can afford the game), or get yer buddy a controller and play split-screen and blow up the whole living room!!!  This is undoubtedly a 'thumbs up' game, they just got the price wrong. Try Dragon's Dogma for 20 bucks cheaper.

review_text: Nostalgia... Anyone who played Vengence Of The Blood Angels on the Amiga/3D0/Sega Saturn will look at this with a sense of nostalgia, but ultimately thats the whole problem with Space Hulk.  This game is old skool. unless you are prepared for an old skool (with a k because its THAT old skool) turn based combat game that IS NOT FAIR, then yes, you will love it, but I think that todays gamers will see this as an inferior product to X:Com as they expect something more visually stimulating and engaging, and to be honest, I can understand the numerous comments about bolter jaming....  If anything, this is an incredibly faithful conversion of the old board game, of which I am a fan (40k and all its incarnations od spin offs are, in general, brilliant as a social past-time) but it shows not only its age, but the age of the actual board game with the limited rule system and the fact that its just not fair. The AI is meant to throw everything at you. No learning where they attack from, no, random encounters, multiple deaths and much swearing will ensue....  You would think someone (Games Workshop?) would come up with rules for development of a bolter that doesnt jam, or at least a method of investing points into keeping your equipment working. Simple touches like that would expand the playability of this. as faithful as it is, its showing its age. A much needed revamp by GW is needed before anyone considers trying to put it on a computer or console. I love space hulk for its atmosphere, and its nice to see some investment after the horrible THQ situation, but we want multiplayer FPS based missions with the ability to develop a character in the 40K universe, and this is just a nod to nostalgia.

review_text: Being completely new to the CoH franchise, I hope that my review differs from many other negative reviews in that I am not basing my assessment on a comparison to the CoH part 1, I am not basing it on the Russian community's dissatisfaction with the anti-Soviet theme of the campaign, and I am not basing my assessment on the facet of microtransactions regarding all the tedious little upgrades and units you must buy.    Pro- Having never experienced this combination of macro and micro management in an RTS before, I was very pleased with how the actual gameplay feels. I enjoyed very much placing my troops in cover and positioning the direction of my machineguns, I love calling down artillery strikes and smoke barrages in person, and I even enjoyed the unique system which allows vehicles to receive component damage. It combines the best elements of Command and Conquer, World of Tanks, and XCom into one Band of Brothers themed experience.    Cons- I purchased the original CoH2 game on a Steam sale to try it out. I figured that with all of the additional content available I would start with the base game and perhaps buy the extra DLC if I enjoyed it. Well whether you blame me for this ignorance or sympathise with my situation, you must agree that it is always frustrating anytime you purchase gaming products to find out that they are completely uneccessary. I would have purchased either one by itself if I knew you didnt need the base game to add them. Now I have a campaign and 2 forces I never want to use when I could have had the US.

I own about 95% of the Meridian 4 catalog, and picked up GT: Evil Heroes during that package. I later purchased GT2: Dungeons and Donuts during a sale. The Background  GT1 and GT2 share the premise of the player taking control of a moody sap (Drake) who overcomes personal challenges to become The Hero That Saves The World. The game is largely a parody of main-stream RPGs, with characters often throwing out asides and references to other games. The jokes are campy and are mostly of low-brow humor; some would say the designers are mocking how sexualized female characters are in other games, some would say the designers are part of the problem. Characters  Throughout the games, you get the opportunity to recruit new party members with different skillsets, for example: Angelina: Healer 'The Semi-Divine Holy Avatar Himself': 2 handed swordsman. Candy: Archer Each character has a relatively well fleshed out back story, as there is a LOT of dialogue to read. In GT2 they have actual voice actors to play each character. Skills  In GT1, skills are tied to your player level. Your character has an additional skill titled 'Obsession', which is kind of an automatic passive attack with unique abilities. Ex: Holy Avatar's obsession is the ability to talk everyone everyone to sleep in a 3x3 radius. Including your party members. In GT2, skills are tied to your 'Talent Tree' (which is not very well explained). When you max a bottom tier Talent, it unlocks your 3rd skill. A second tier talent unlocks your 4th skill, and a 5th tier talent unlocks your 'ultimate'. Additionally, GT2 skills have a timer, so you can't use them every turn. Your Obsession meter (more like an ultimate meter), builds up to allow you to cast the Ultimate skill. The ultimate skills in GT2 are actually pretty useful, with one of them doing massive AOE damage which is needed later in the game when there are seriously 20 monsters in a room. The Problems  The camera is terrible. It frequently locks onto random locations, and you have to reset the Follow toggle to return to your character. You might even feel sick from it. The Bloom doesn't help either, and may be part of the problem in inducing motion sickness in players. There is extreme camera smoothing in both games. Characters frequently get stuck. If I have a party member that stands in a doorway, and I try to talk to a NPC outside of that door way, I cannot pass through. My character will choose to run into the party member, and constantly try to move past. The controls are locked and I cannot disable the 'queuing' of the talk command. To fix this I had to use the cheat console to teleport Drake to the NPC. This happened at least 30 or 40 times. In GT1 there is a major gamebreaking glitch where the Holy Avatar would be completely disabled during a battle. The battle round timer would still be enabled, and you would be stuck in an infinite loop. This required a reload of an earlier save file to fix. In GT2 the quest journal offers only Italicized and Normal text views. It is unclear if a quest is Completed, In Progress, or Not Completable. That last detail is interesting, there are at least 5 quests in GT2 that if completed, disable your ability to complete other quests. Some of my quests that were 'completed' still remained in the journal, while some that were incomplete just disappeared. GT2 lacks progression logic. Finding your next mission requires you to talk to just about every NPC to figure out what you're doing next. The need to do so is not tracked in the quest log. There is also no notification that informs you to talk to a NPC. If you surf the Steam or Gamefaqs forums, many players ask 'WTH do I do next?' And the answer is usually 'Talk to everyone'. This was not as big of a problem in GT1 as the maps were not as large. Final Comments  I eventually used the cheats 'goto//god//healall' to complete both games. 'Healall' resets the movement tracker so you can move from one end of a map to the opposite end in one turn. This made progressing through the (really boring and tedious) fights much quicker. Even then, it still took about 10 hours to finish GT2 because of all the walking and talking you're required to do. I would estimate that GT2 would take over 30 hours without cheating. And let me tell you the 10 second ending is not worth the effort[1] . Humblebrag: This post here is more comprehensive than anything you'll find online. Nobody wants to write a walkthrough or a guide for these games because GT sucks. If you're stuck on a mission/quest, leave a post and I'll see if I remember how I did it.

review_text: As both a Warhammer enthusiast and a panzer corp fan, I absolutely loved this game. The vast majority of complaints appear to be people whining about the turn limit or the absolutely ludicrous amount of unit variations. I can understand the time limit stance, but if you think about it, if you are fighting orcs you need to stay mobile, if you are in a single field of combat for too long you WILL be over run, so the turn limit makes sense. Any dedicated warhammer fan will be able to understand the differences between the different Leman Russ patterns and Infantry units, and in my opinion it is one of the positive points of the game. The versatility of the Steel Legion is clearly demonstrated by this large selection of units, and only a small number of units wouldn't do them justice. It's not a perfect game, and there are definitely missions I wish I had a turn or two extra for, (LOOKING AT YOU HIVE INFERNUS,) but that's just Warhammer for you.

review_text: After playing a couple of dozen hours now, and having read much of the other reviews here, i just can say  buy the game it´s totally worth it´s money with patch 12 being out.  Many of the negative reviews here are just silly hateboy drivvel, some of them are just plain wrong and outright ridiculous.  So get things straight:   1. Compared to Shogun 2, its a lot more stable; i kept having the famous autosave crash during the whole shogun 2 series , even with fall of the samurai every second autosave. Just once( !!!!) in rome 2, using the cloud for save.  2. Some people complained that the game engine obviously comes from Empire, and wasnt therefore suited to melee warfare. Well, pure hogwash, Shogun 2 had the same Empire engine, and melee worked just fine!  3. The premade tactical formations in battles work extremely good, and are very realistic. Frankly 80 % of all real battles in antiquity worked like phalanx in the front and cavalry on the flanks, skirmisher screen in front. If the main line budged, or the flanks got lost, battle was lost. The game here pretty much works the same way, it´s astonishing how well the engine emulates that logic of ancient phalanx/maniple warfare.  4. The new region system saves you a lot of micromanagement and is frankly more realistic. On the downside, you cant fortify every border town anymore, but that just makes proper strategic planning and gaining client states or satrapies more important, since you cant defend each settlement.  Well, the naval battles are quite crappy, but heh, no cannon broadsides without cannons, and ancient sea battles resembled a crazed ramming and boarding contest anyway. And rest assured, as soon as you got ships with on deck artillery, the transport rush thing is over. Besides, the often mentioned super transports can barely survive 3 rammings, whereas a quinquireme or somesuch can easily take a dozen hits. And transports tend to burn down real quick, therefore you just do need a real navy after the opening phase of the campaign.  So yeah, the game is totally worth it´s money now, and i´d dare say it´s one of the best in the series in her current patched state( 12 patches lol, was the original qualitiy assurance done by drunken monkeys?) with more content coming up every 2 months or so.  Haven´t played the Gaul and Hannibal campaign yet, gonna buy them with the next steam sale, and will write probably a review about that too.  p.s. patch 14(!!!!) out , a real big one commited just on artificial dumbness for siege battles. The comp attacking your walled cities was one of the weak points of this game; I kept butchering 4k plus barbarian armies with just a few units behind the walls repeatedly for example. So a real good game probably just got a lot better.

review_text: it do not work for me

If we were on DeviantArt, we would find ourselves astonished and amazed: no doubt. Paintings are great. But we're on Steam, talking about games: a more complex form of art. In a game you expect entertainment through animations, music, sound effects, engaging storyline, good dialogues, playability and longevity. Tormentum disappoints in all these aspects.   The first impression I got is that, maybe, they thought of making a hidden object game or a visual novel, but they would have had to write many more dialogues and a more solid story... so they have decided to make a some kind of game with almost no interaction, and almost no story.   There's been a lot of chatter about moral choices and alternative endings, well... there are just ten choices and two endings (a short cutscene, matter of seconds!). Much much little, even for a game that lasts about three hours. A soundtrack, in FLAC format, would be the only thing that could justify the price, because it's the second and final positive note of the game; needless to say: it's not included.  In conclusion: Torment is a very good sort-of-game for the mobile market (play it there) and, like many of these, not worth more than 4 euros

review_text: A speedrunners dream, but unfortunately the elitism bleeds into the game design - this is a tough but fun platforming game, with occasionally unresponsive/glitchy controls and fast-paced parkour and fps elements. When it works, it works great. However, the developers included lives and finite checkpoints so that, until you absolutely master a level, you won't be able to progress.  This game plays great both as a speedrunner and a platformer, but the devs don't want you playing it like a platformer and punish you for taking more than 2 seconds to think about what to do next - the homing, unkillable bombs where the only way to avoid instant death is to keep moving spring to mind.  Overall its a good game flawed by the inclusion of some clunky and unwanted game mechanics like lives, their being there only to further put off anyone other than the most hardcore from this game (this coming from someone that plays a lot of Dustforce, a similarly styled speedrun focused game in 2D).

review_text: Space Hulk was one heck of an experience to have. Looking in the grand scheme of things, you are paying anywhere from $15-25 to buy a virtual game based on a board game that costs well over $90 to sometimes up to $200 in amazing conditions. People interested in buying this product should realize you are buying a scenario based board game set in the WH40k universe. If you like WH40K and enjoy strategy games, this is something you should invest in. If you are thinking this is going to be something like WH40k Space Marine or Dawn of War, you should probably turn away from this game. It gives a clear visual interpretation of the board game Space Hulk, with decent sounds and visual effects. You'll be entranced trying to figure out how to place all of your space marines so that they can cover each other and complete your objective. And with so many missions, DLC, AND Online Multiplayer, this game has a lot of replayability; especially if you have a friend who is just as terminator crazy as you are.

review_text: Overall this game is fast paced and extremely fun to play with or without friends, Here are somethings you might be worried about when downloading the game. Common Concerns: 1. HRV, you might be concerned that everyone is running around with 'wall hacks', well it's not extremly OP it has a relatively long recharge time between uses and everyone has the ability to use it evening out the playing field. 2. P2W, although you can buy ingame currency known as ZEN with real money to get new Perm weapons extremly easy, you can also just play the game long enough and get enough GP (Free currency) to buy weapons Perm without wasting a dime. 3. Hit Detection, BLR im almost certain uses hit scan detection meaning there shouldnt be a problem when you aim at someone and pull the trigger. 4. PC Strain (Graphics), What if you only have a laptop? Well when my brother had a laptop he was able to run BLR at the lowest settings and it was playable. If you have a gaming PC then bump the graphics on maximum and be amazed by the quality of the graphics. Tips: 1. The starting pistol is not bad for finishing off your opponent but don't try to use it as your only weapon, its damage output is to low. 2. Aiming is key, Hip fire although can be used is almost impossible to get kills with when using the starting rifle, the more accurate you are, the more shots that are gunna land, and the faster you're going to kill your enemy. 3. Need to heal? Need more ammo? Just head to one of the supply stations during battle and pick up some health and ammo. 4. You can infact customize your starting rifle to be a sniper rifle, SMG, LMG, battle rifle, & many more, just make sure you have enough GP to buy the mods perm.  I hope this review helps you decide wether to download and play the game or not.

review_text: Mordheim: City of the Damned is a Masochist wet dream.  This game is possibly the funnest time you will have getting your a** handed to you on a silver platter especially late game. This game has a entire list of just painful factors that'll leave you rage quiting after every game you play, but like an abused girlfriend, you'll just keep coming back for more hoping to pull off those tight decisive victories and bloody battles that make you cheer alongside your warband.  Now Mordheim is a game that doesn't go easy on you and one bad mistake could cause your entire warband to be brutally defeated and lose you the game. Just to list a few, AI is tough and they will hammer as many soldiers as they can into a battle line just to take you down. Not a problem, but you'll be fighting against harsh RNG that definitely favors the AI in most fights. Criticals could knock out warriors leaving them completely vulnerable to even more hits, the AI will dodge what feels like every other hit you make before delivering their own blows that'll have your guys hurting or REALLY hurting. If you think that's cruel, the enemy doesn't have to kill everyone, only enough of your warband to bring your moral down till you route, which can be typicall 3-5 of your guys in a warband of 6-8.  Good news though, the enemy warband can also break, instantly winning you the battle. The Bad news is if the enemy breaks, you lose out on completing any optional objectives that are vital for leveling up your warband. 'Complete optional objectives before you fight' I hear you say, which would work except you forgot about the brutal AI enemy that'll block, charge, and murder your warband if you try to run around them, or would route before you could kill enough enemies to get on to completing the objectives.  After a battle, you'll have to deal with managing your warband, paying for their service and any medical bills from them getting injured, outfitting them with better gear, customizing them a bit, training new abilities. If a warrior is injured, they could be out of service for days which means you won't be able to fight with them, which is painful if you're leader becomes injured.  And to put icing on this hot-tamali cake, you have a timer! Throughout the campaign you will be required to pay your Warbands sponsor/leader/murder-lord shipments of Wyrdstone which you will collect during missions and receive a bit after a mission. And not small shipments, HUGE shipments of over a hundred pounds of Wyrdstone must be filled in the manner of 2 weeks, and every shipment will be larger than the last. If you fail to fill these shipment requests (I believe twice), you lose the game. Game over, Warband disbanded, please try again.  ....oh ya. And the game auto saves so no loading an old save if you lose, cheater.   NOW for AAALLLL of that...it's still a really good game and very, very rewarding when you win. The RNG is effected by a lot of skills you earn from leveling up that can swing things more in your favor. A clever player can usually lock down enemies and swoop around with faster troops to gather the wyrdstone and I've noticed wyrdstone piles seem to grow more fruitful as the game progresses so fulfilling those requests for your sponsor/leader/murder-lord never trully get's impossible. If you manage skills right and make sure to pull your soldiers out if they're hurting, you can avoid a lot of problems down the line. Optional objectives are still tough but smart planning (and a little luck) can achieve you a decisive victory.  Probably what I have to say though most for the fun factor is that it's tough, but never impossible. The feeling you get from just having enough gold to pay for one more battle, to just barely pay off the request with one more day to go. To pull of a clutch victory from the grasp of defeat or see you're warriors beat down a powerful enemy who seems so impossing is so satisfying. It's what makes the game so good. It isn't being defeated and restarting from a checkpoint.  This game isn't about how hard you hit, but how hard you can get hit and keep moving forward. How much you can take and keep moving forward. That's how winning is done!

review_text: Part of me would just like to let this game go unreviewed, but, apparently, that part has been stifled and gagged by the other part writing this review. And I guess that's a proper diagram for how this game makes me feel: Split down the middle.   Having just recently (re)played Loom, I could draw the obvious parallel there. But Loom A) didn't have an inventory system and B) only used 8 notes in a way that forced you to learn the spells along with Bobbin. Yes, it may be nitpicky for me to slam the inventory system and (mostly unused) music notes in this game, but a majority of it was unnecessary.   Finding Teddy gives the player an alphabet with which to 'communicate' with the world and get real feedback from it sometimes. You've already given the player a voice and immersed them into the experience. Why cluttler it up with items and allies? If the inventory was simplified or even removed (and absolutely scrap the cat and the fly. They're cute, but only perform two or three tasks the whole game), leaving only the alphabet, the game would have been so much more intuitive. Instead of hopelessly using every item in your inventory on every seemingly interactive thing in the game (in ADDITION to typing musical messages at them, or maybe using the cat or maybe using the fly), the player would have nothing but their words. It's a welcome limitation.  Actually, let's expand on that: The whole experience feels like a game that could be strictly text interactions, but has inventory items and allies shoe horned in. Instead of giving the text-based genre new life, it just causes a loss in momentum when you have to open your inventory on every screen. It causes the game to fall flat by having too many options.   Don't get me wrong, I did enjoy this game. But I feel like text based games and point and click adventures are most rewarding when you hit on those 'Ah Ha!' moments, of which this game has very few *because* of its varied mechanics. I do ultimately recommend it, however simply because of the graphics and ambient music.   It's too bad that it's an insult to your visual and music talents when the core mechanics are convoluted and sometimes frustrating. No spoilers, but I wanted the whole game to be just like the ending. Hell, the ending itself could have gone on a lot longer. Adventure games are all about using your key mechanics to keep the player forgetting that they're actually playing a game. And it felt like the ending put me in that immersive stranglehold only to let me go after a short time.  Final word: Never leave your player wanting to be strangled more. Let's hope the sequel brings out more of the positive aspects of this game and reduces the detritus.

review_text: Early Access Review

Polarity is a fairly simple 3D first-person puzzle platformer. It's rather short; I beat the single player game in one sitting in an afternoon. The game also contains a split-screen co-op mode and a set of bonus levels.  It lacks a lot of polish that makes similar puzzle games great: the menus are rather old looking, although the level select in the background is nice; there are a few clipping errors here and there, but nothing overt; the textures are a little flat, but there are is a nice shadow effect around the edges of things that make everything look a little bit nicer.  The single player puzzles are rather easy, and the co-op puzzles seem to be a bit harder. However, it seems that the co-op mode has even less polish than the main game, with the cube spawn sites not even having the fancy model that the single player campaign uses.  I'd rate this game as a fairly good entry into the genre for someone who hasn't played much. But for anyone looking for a well polished game or much of a challenge, it's best to look elsewhere.

review_text: I've played this a few times few at this point, attempted to compare different endings, but as an interactive fiction/visual novel, the cons outweigh the pros unfortunately.  I appreciate what this game attempts to do for the awareness of depression, but it fails to do so effectively.  At first opening the game, the music tipped me off with the thought, “oh hey, that’s nice. It’s kind of sad. This may help put me in the mood and let me understand what depression is for some.”  I’ve dealt with depression in the past, and no, it’s not fun. It’s tiresome and… depressing (Lol, kind of like this review). But depression is a bit different from one person to another, so knowing how it was like for myself and some other people I’ve been close to, I was curious about how this game would portray it.  First and foremost, the writing is decent. Fortunately for the player, there aren’t any serious flaws that derail the story completely. BUT, there definitely could’ve been more elaboration on the character’s past(s) or relationships with others. There was no real detail about the player’s workplace, or even their boss. Having that relationship explored more could’ve greatly helped the story, even if it was for a short moment.   Halfway through the game, I just stopped caring about the outcome. Which is weird for me, since I’ve trudged through some pretty boring interactive fictions in the past, including those old school text adventures very few people would ever want to play (unless you’re into that sort of thing like me).  Technically, I found this game irritatingly lazy. There’s no save function. I know this seems trivial, but even with the most homebrew of interactive fictions through platforms like Gargoyle or the InfoCom reader, there’s usually a save function. If you find yourself bored wanting to quit to carry on later… YOU CAN’T. You have no other choice but to start from the beginning.  The “branching” story is misleading in the description above. This game is marketed as an interactive fiction with decent options to change the ending, but in reality, there isn’t. Most people aren’t going to play this more than once to realize this, but there is only one ending. Sure a couple words and things may be switched around or replaced, but it’s a lie. Sure your “stats” may be a little better or worse, but it doesn’t change the context of the game greatly enough to be considered truly interactive.  And yes, I understand the crossed out options are options that would be available if you weren’t depressed, but since you’re given so little options, the variance is negligible. Therefore, replay value is at an all-time low. And even though I didn’t want to, I replayed this sucker more than I wanted to. I even went with the worst options available to me, singling me out to depressing options (no pun intended), to see if I would have a super terrible ending… it never came.  From personal experience with depression, I faced myself at the edge of a bridge considering suicide since I’m a terrible swimmer. The choice of suicide never came in this game.  I know this sounds absolutely terrible and I apologize to anybody that may be offended, but when you think the world is better off without you in it, you only drag the people around you down, that God has either forsaken you or isn’t even there, or if you killed yourself the world wouldn’t skip a beat, then yes… suicide, regardless of the stigma, is a viable option to somebody that is depressed, regardless of how terrible of a choice it may be.  An option for this could’ve been suicide, then showing the aftermath of what it would do to the family and friends of the player.  Unfortunately if this choice was made into the game, you never get acquainted well enough with your family and girlfriend enough to actually care. They overall seemed paper thin in character, and sure this detail may be thrown aside as a viewpoint of somebody that's depressed. I get that. But if you want to really grind at the readers views and heart, you need to connect them to the characters and their feelings, including the supporting characters. This may seem pretty basic, but the supporting characters are just as important as the main. And no matter how crappy this sounds, suicide could’ve opened up another heartbreaker end game scenario in the fact that your new kitten would die of starvation because you killed yourself and nobody knew about it for, I dunno... let's throw out some arbitrary numbers: 4-5 days to a week at most.  So yes, I found this game lazy in mechanic and programming. From the lack of options, lack of ending, and lack of a save function. I felt like it always ended with a sigh with no resolution. Which is funny, since the writers at the end of the game thank you for playing stating, “there’s no real end to depression.” Which may be true, but I just found lazy. For a good story, there could still of been a resolution of what happened, even if it’s, “Oh, nothing happened. You stayed depressed and went into a deeper spiral of darkness. Lost your job, and found yourself homeless.” Or, “Your girlfriend Alex, wanting to help you, excorted you to go see a therapist.” Or, “Your family, admitted you to a psyche ward, because they realized your depression got so bad, you weren’t able to function in day to day tasks.” OR, “Your IM friend Attic, realized you didn’t go see the therapist, attempted to get you help in person. You denied. So he called a Suicide help line.” OR, “ANYTHING beyond the same thing, nearly every time.”  The music was okay at first… then it got annoying. And in the end it seemed to actually add to the boredom I found with this game. It definitely didn’t help the more times I played through it. Luckily there’s a mute function in the menu, or you can always just mute your computer audio.  BUT here are the pros…  I appreciate what the game attempts to do.  The pictures getting fuzzier and turning black and white was a nice touch.  The music getting a little distorted the more you get depressed was kind of a nice touch.  I found no typos (unlike this review)  Final verdict: This game should be Pong and Forgotten. 3.7/10 – Not worth your time unless you’re really curious. OR, you’re a diehard interactive fiction novel gamer and want a taste of everything at some point, despite whether or not you’d like it or not. Which unfortunately for me, I fell under this category.  P.S. I'll probably make a video review of this on my YouTube Channel. I know this is shameless advertising, but idc. It'll probably get me some views and subs because of the whole gamergate crap. Also, It'll knock off anybody thinking that I plagerized my own material since my Youtube name doesn't have the underscore. lol

review_text: Masochisia  is a point-and-click horror game about a man battling his inner demons on his way to becoming a psychotic killer. It's a completely story-driven experience. You take on the role of Hamilton and wander through the game's painting-like world and talk with the people and creatures you encounter (and yourself). The six chapters take approximately two hours to complete.  On the plus side, the artwork is excellent and disturbing as is the musical score.  Did I feel creeped out? Yeah, the story is harsh, and the warnings about this game are serious.  Not for everyone.  Particularly if you are squeamish. It's based on a true story. I found a Wikipedia article that recounted the actual events and people involved, and no names had been changed. The game is remarkably faithful to the source material, but the real-life story is even worse. If you plan on playing this game I'd avoid looking it up until after as it will give away various plot elements.  But is it a good game?  That's debatable. I would be more inclined to call this a visual novel, well ... novella, due to length. It reminded me mostly of   Year Walk  , another creepy Steam title with several design similarities. However that game has puzzles in it, so it takes some brain work to finish. In this game there is only one puzzle, and it's not terribly hard to figure out if you are paying attention to the clue being dropped.  Of course horror novels exist purely to creep out their audience. That's a part of their allure. Horror games do this too, but in this case I'm inclined to think it's not enough to make this rise above being a mediocre experience because it only challenges you on how tolerant you are to the scenes being depicted. Maybe that's because it stays too close to the source material? Real life events might be notorious but that doesn't mean they make a good game. By the end the only thought I was left with was, 'there are some sick people in the world who are f---'d up beyond all hope.' The end.   In short, if you want to walk through a deeply disturbing and horrific short story and you have two hours to kill (heh), then play it. Otherwise there isn't much else here.   Rating: 5/10 Great visuals and atmosphere, an OK story, but little else. Not for the faint of heart, but might please a fair number of horror fans.

review_text: Taught me the word ♥♥♥♥♥♥ for only two pennies 13/10

I remember a while back when I stumbled across the webpage for this game. I remember reading the little synopsis, looking at the art and what I've been playing seems very different from what it was originally conceived as.   Before (As I recall) you were going to be a male Lilim bartender that people would come in and dump their emotional problems on. However being an AI that doesn't experience emotions in the same way and without a fear of death he would have a hard time offering advice so he'd try to help in the best way he knows: Bartending.   There was an acknowledgement of it being about getting to know strangers and that the player would have to approach people differently in asking the right questions or getting them drunk to open up. Then the question would come about if that is even okay to do even with the best intentions?   (A la Kim who is calmed by a drink but then sleeps in from her job the next day, but is unavoidable in the story now.)   There seemed to be a greater focus on shmoozing and boozing as I think a remnant of the system is still in the game with the ability to crank up the booze when a drink calls for 'Optional Karmotrine' but there is 0 effect on the characters. No drunken dialogue   (save for a few secret drunken dialogues)   That aspect mostly only plays in if you need to leave it out completely for the rare occasion that someone doesn't want alcohol.   It seemed like it was going to be a bit more tongue in cheek to have characters come in with a story and then have the robot bartender be like 'Hm, what drink goes with sadness?' or 'They seem to have something on their mind, better spike their drink so they can get it off their chest.' It gave me the impression that it would be a bit more gameplay driven in terms of choices to let someone be and not get involved or to be heavily involved in terms of getting their gossip/drama.   The game we have here is cool mostly because I love the art, but it's severely lacking in meaningful choices and gameplay. It's a visual novel for the most part which isn't necessarily bad but it did fall below my expectations of what I thought the game would be based on looking at the website some years ago. When you get to serve a drink it is straightforward and easy to pull off, which isn't bad but without any payoff it feels pointless. Do you like to put WAY too much Karmotrine? Well that's fine, booze away! But know that it will have no affect on the conversation or your relationship with the character in front of you. 'Thanks' is as good as it gets and then you really get the feeling that what little you do has very little impact on the whole. Your character will just do things without indication to the player of what's going on until it's brought up in the conversation.   (Like when there is a raid on your apartment building and you bring your cat to work. Nothing gives you any idea that anything is going on and you don't make that decision, it just happens.    The city is in turmoil with protesting, rampant hackers, a terrorist attack and the elite law enforcers are crumbling and your bar is closing... and no one is that concerned. Sure, there are questions and some overlap with the people coming and going but other than that everyone is so jaded that it barely affects conversation except with two of the characters.   Sei and Stella.   'Yeah, it kinda just sucks here.' But it's one of the most interesintg aspects of the game. In fact the subplots are the most interesting parts of the game and if you're like me you will hang onto every detail you can get because they are so few and far in between. The things I wante to learn about the most were carefully tucked away with the biggest glaring problem of the game.  Jill, the protagonist. If I had a nickel every time the main character got in the way of the actually good stuff. Jill is likeable at first. She's sarcastic and emotionally invested in the people around her, but her problems and motivations are oatmeal. It leaves a Mary Sue taste in my mouth when characters come in and while Jill is being unfirendly all around, they like her anyway and keep coming back. Jill lives in an apartment with her cat, reads blogs and lurks knockoff ♥♥♥♥♥ threads. She drinks beer alone and smokes in bed. Basically, a very boring person. Despite her need for freedom and to make her own decisions to build her life as she sees fit, she does nothing. She works, drinks and sleeps and even says that she'd stay in the bar until she died. So she's not doing anything with herself and when the game tries to make you take a closer look at her life by dropping an emotional bomb in the mix and it left me apathetic. Who is this person all of a sudden? She's talking about another person that we've had no interactions with in the game.   An ex-girlfriend who lived and died off screen therefore making no connections to the player in any shape or form suddenly needs to be important. Jill then gets over it. Sure she says 'I feel like a piece of poo' every once in a while, but the point is that I didn't believe her. Her routine doesn't change in any way in the wake of a death that we are supposed to feel had a huge impact on her life.   Jill's problems are not interesting and because they are emotionally intangible. As a player there's no room for self insert or empathy because everything is already spelled out. How does someone feel? Read on to find out! Also you rarely even get to look at Jill. She's mostly portrayed through text boxes which doesn't do much to bridge the disconnect between me and her. It's easier to empathize when you can see the person you're meant to empathize with. She wasn't built well enough to be a story vehicle.   Anyway, I feel like the game pivoted somewhere for something much less interesting when it comes to interactions and storytelling. It did engage me for the first few hours but as people came and went and it became apparent that making the drinks only affected how much money I made that day I began signing off. Much more interesting things were going on outside of the bar and by the time it came around I had nothing left for Jill. My eyes started rolling in my head, 'Oh okay, because you needed a problem too.' The pacing is a little weird as well. Some of the crucial points of a characters arc are interrupted with conversations about bust sizes or sexual partners. (Alma, Dorothy...) So which is it? Are we mourning the death of a loved one or are we just insecure and hopelessly romantic? Was that dead person--- ah who cares, I didn't know them anyway.   All in all, the game is fine to look at and if you only want to read then sure, pick it up. As someone who followed the game for a while this isn't what I was expecting in terms of execution or storytelling. Gameplay is minimal and pointless except to get back to the conversation and then save. You are not in charge of what you take away as the unimportant details are hamfisted to your brain and the more interesting bits are hidden away to leave you wanting more.    EDIT: The original featured art had characters more similar to Betty and Deal before Jill. Now the Betty and Deal art is on the Prologue title's front.

review_text: Early Access Review

The Witcher 3 is an art in terms of graphics. The story line however, doesn't catch your attention for too long. There are thousands of dialogs which eventually,the only reason not to skip them is so you can admire the beautiful and detailed graphics.  If you don't have a job and don't like your social lifet, get ready for a countless number of main quests, side quests, treasure huntings, contracts and BUGS.  That's right! This game has so many bugs and glitches!  Don't take me wrong, It's a great game, but sometimes it makes you feel like it is unfinished. Instead of simply whining, let me give you a few examples:  There's a quest in which you go to a graveyard to hunt a gear called viper. If you go there before activating the quest and end up closing the gate inside the chapel, that's it, you cannot complete the quest anymore.  I got distracted once and ran until the end of the map. It automatically made me turn around and walk to the other way, suddenly, I was flying and had to restart from the last check point, because no commands would respond anymore.  I tried to get into a boat, and for some reason, the screen showed myself flying and falling on it to death.  The list could extend, but I'll just stop here.   A patient person will hear some dialogs and go for some side quests, but if you're like me, you'll see yourself picking all of them and skipping every single dialog to simply follow the map and complete whatever has to be done.  For the first few days, I saw myself losing a few nights of sleep to play. After like 300 quests mixed between main and side (Believe me, 300 quests are nothing compared to how many quests this game has), I felt like I was not getting anywhere. The progress in the character is evident. But only in the character. Now 5 or 6 quests a day seems enough to bore me so I can go to bed.  The good thing is that I really feel like playing every day, but it's not like those games that made me live a second life more intensely than my real life.  Overall, a good game and I recommend. I will leave a few things in my summary below that I did not comment above:  Pros:  Stunning graphics; Awesome combat dynamics; Mind blowing crafting system with thousands of different loot to collect.  Cons:  Boring story line; Too many bugs and glitches.

review_text: Forgetting all the hype, rumors, conjecture, and flat out bullcrap that was was flying around about this game, it must be said that the game delivers EXACTLY what was promised. The Universe is mind-bogglingly huge, and drab. The sense of scale and space makes one feel completely insignificant.   But this isn't a detraction--it's what was promised, and it's what makes finding something truly bizarre or cool that much more rewarding. In fact, if I have any specific complaint about the game itself, it's that the universe is perhaps a bit TOO populated; I was expecting more barren rocks, but life abounds everywhere I've been yet.   The game is what you make of it--no more, no less. It's lonely as hell in space, which makes getting mauled by a deer with antlers growing out of it's ♥♥♥ all the more interesting.   That all said, I have to be honest when I say that the game wasn't ready for release. I have been lucky enough not to have the stability/crashing/launch issues many, MANY others have reported, but I do have several specific gripes: - The User interface is abysmal. Like, REALLY bad. Crafting is clumsy, everything is counter-intuitive.  - The galactic map is practically unusable. there is no sense of scale, and distances between objects have no reference so might as well be arbitrary. Planning routes is best done by closing your eyes and throwing a suction dart at your screen for all the UI will help you - The Atlas is useless. Mouse-over a 'discovery' to see a static animation of your discovery that's in the worst possible angle. You can't examine, rotate or otherwise inspect it. You might as well start collecting Windows 95 desktop icons and show them off to your friends.   Really though, the game is a spectacle, and well worth a look. review_text: Te best way to describe 'Operation Kursk K-141' is to call it a missed opportunity.  I realy hate it when adventure games are bad, because just making the effort to make an adventure game confines its creator to a very select niche.  The most part of the budget was clearly devoted to the graphics and animation part, which might not be exceptional, but is above the average 2D, pixelated indie efforts. However, after finishing the game and realizing they must have run out of money at the end, I wish they had gone the 2D route and invested more in story development.  Puzzles are the bread and butter of any adventure gamer. If they are good, they keep you invested in the story. If they are bad, they ruin everything.  While there are some good, smart puzzles in the game, they are mere exceptions to the 'WTF' reactions to the step-by-step walkthrough one has to use in order to finish the game. Radom objects apearing and disappearing without reason in different rooms - check; uninteresting, unidimensional and useless characters - check; combining objects in illogical ways - check; have to click objects several times in order to be able to use them - check; open, unsatisfying ending - check and so on.  So don't get this game unless it's on sale and you are desperate for adventure.

review_text: The 'shock' series was my all time favourite franchise and this installment marks its death. Instead of evolving the game the developers cut everything that made systemshock and bioshock special in order to appeal to the mainstream.  Aesthetics: There are no advanced rendering techniques employed here but everything from large buildings to small items is very well crafted. The problem is that there is only one visual theme present and its impossible to tell different areas apart. Sound is competently done, nothing to really praise or complain about.  Gameplay: This is where the game has fallen the furthest in my opinion. Instead of having a great mix of puzzle solving, sneaky combat and rpg like character progression we get the stock brainless military shooter model.  In previous installments you could build your arsenal carefully with a large arsenal of weapons at your disposal, you could mix powers, mods and various augmentations to fit your own playstyle. Infinite says ♥♥♥♥ that you get to carry two guns, you can upgrade your weapons slightly but since you can only carry two and ammo is scarce you'll end up using what the game gives you.  There is essentially only one type of weapon, a generic hitscan gun with maybe eight different variations with negative correlation between fire rate and damage.  To complement the measley arsenal you get powers and where historically they used to fill a variety of functions in and outside of combat they now do exactly one thing, stun enemies and do a bit of damage. Puzzle solving is reduced to let your npc companion solve it, no really.  Story: This is where most people say the game redeems itself and well, I disagree. First let me say that bioshock infinite deals with quantum mechanics and to quote Richard Fenynman 'If you think you understand quantum mechanics, you don't understand quantum mechanics', and this certainly holds true for the script writers.  The pseudo intellectual story is spoonfed to the player in a very disjointed way in order to create a false sense of mystery. After the mandatory plot twist all the pieces suddenly fit together to reveal the keystone of the story and well, it is just doesn't work in any sensible way.

Star Wars Republic Commando is a unique experience for a Star Wars game. You don't play as a Jedi or a Bounty Hunter or any of the things in between. Instead you get plopped directly into the head of a Clone trooper and thrown onto the battlefields of Episode Two.  As a clone trooper you don't get much back story or indeed any motivation beyond the occasional communication pop-up on your UI that tells you where to go next. Indeed you were bred for combat and unquestioning loyalty and that's what you do. This rather unique aspect does a lot of alleviate many of the problems that usually plague Star Wars games and makes Republic Commando a rather enjoyable experience.  You are not just any Clone Trooper however, you are a squad leader and this is where the gameplay of Republic Commando comes into play. With you have 3 additional commandos, each with their own area of expertise. A sniper, a demolitions expert and a hacker. As you go through the missions you will be able to order your team around, into cover, mounting gun positions, or performing specific tasks suited to their specialty through a context sensitive command system. You basically just point at an object and click and one of your squad members takes the appropriate action. This all works surprisingly well and trying to play the game as a lone wolf tends to end badly as the game features some rather impressive AI, both on the part of your teammates and the enemies.  The game is very pretty and in particular the background deserves notice as there is always something else going on there which often looks every bit as intense as the fight you are distracted from.  The voice-acting is pretty good for a game but you will quickly realize that the developers took a little shortcut here as the many different voices and accents the clones have makes you wonder just how similar they really are.  Star Wars Republic Commando is an attempt to take the Star Wars franchise into the squad based tactical genre and it comes away from the attempt quite nicely although it is a game that has it's flaws, most notably the campaign is extremely short and ends with a cliffhanger that's never resolved as this is the only game in the franchise. Still all in all it's one of the best Star Wars games out there and one of those that can be held up as a good game in it's own right.

review_text: I've been asked by many friends what the difference is between the Shadowrun Returns DLC for Dragonfall and the Director's Cut. I'm going to write from that perspective; there's already plenty of reviews telling about the game itself and I will be using spoilers. Let the reader beware.  First off, the overall plot reminded me a lot of Baldur's Gate II. Divine being-type entity split from its divinity and that problem becomes the player's problem, the player is required to raise a ridiculous amount of money to chase their adversary, along the way the player gets to know their companions better if they care to, and the player is offered side missions for each of their companions that gives each of them a slight boost. Despite all the similarities, I very much enjoyed the Shadowrun take on the story idea; enough to play it through several times and grab every achievement - as there are many 'hidden' achievements tied to how the game ends, that's saying something.  Where the Director's Cut differs from the DLC is mostly the inclusion of the companion missions, the Black Lodge audition, and missions on behalf of some of the Kreusbazar vendors [the majority of these are in both games, but I know the mission for Ezkibel was missing from the DLC for sure]. There are some other noticeable, but minor differences like less dialog options when dealing with Doctor Ezkibel after taking care of the sewers, but they do not really impact the game at all.  Edit: playing the DLC after the Director's Cut allowed me to notice that for some reason there are some dialog triggers and conversations that somehow failed to fire in the Director's Cut. For example, meeting Audran for the first time in the Director's Cut he just starts shooting when you meet him. Playing the DLC, he actually explained why the player's intrusion could not be forgiven and finally some companion lines made more sense as they reference that later.  I found that the missions and dialog included in the Director's Cut greatly enhanced the story and gave it a lot of depth that the player wouldn't know was missing until they experienced it as the DLC is not incomplete without it - it's just more complete with it.  The display and inteface is also slightly different between Dragonfall Director's Cut and the base Shadowrun Returns. While both games made use of keyboard shortcuts for things like inventory items, spells, and personal skills only in Dragonfall are they labeled on the display - playing the base game I never even knew those shortcuts existed and they enable the player to enjoy a quicker and smoother combat experience.  I also like how the weapon display was laid out in Dragonfall over the base game with all the information made available at a glance and number hot keys to switch to specific weapons. The only thing I would consider missing from Dragonfall that was present in the base game was the 'F' key to cycle through weapons when my attention was too focused on the scene to look up what number would correspond to whatever weapon, especially for a character whose loadout changes or an unfamiliar character.  Another item that is an improvement in Dragonfall in my opinion is the ability to lend not just consumable items to your team, but also weapons. In my first playthrough, for example, I made use of rifles and shotguns so whenever I could afford to upgrade my own gear I was able to lend my previous weapons to Eiger as they were better than her in built gear for a while. It also gave me the ability to outfit her with an assault rifle while the Shadowrun Returns Dragonfall DLC does not allow that to happen.  I'll be honest, I backed Shadowrun Returns on Kickstarter so the Director's Cut didn't cost me anything additional, but having played both I would gladly pay for the extra content and story that the Director's Cut offers and consider it money well spent.

Similiar titles in the genre include things like Spaz and Drox Operative (definitely check both of those out for similiar (but different) experiences. VoidExpanse gives you a nice-looking sandbox to play in, but doesn't give you all the toys necessary to have a good time. The skeleton is all here, but the experience feels empty a few hours in.   There simply isn't enough breadth/depth to what you can do to (or with) your character. The classes and ship options are fairly one-dimensional, and the quests are still pretty rinse and repeat. I'd love to see more dynamic quests involving attacks on space stations, some kind of hacking interface/skill tree, true smuggling runs, more interaction (even the ability to join) with the pirate faction, etc. The game just doesn't do interesting things with it's own universe yet.  I love this game for what it is, and for the price point I'm still happy with my purchase, I'm just hoping that they take this brilliant foundation and build something atop it. I know there are countless ideas floating around the community - let's hope some of them see the light of day.

This game was a fun title. I enjoyed playing it so I figured I would give it a decent review.    While I do recommend the game, I will start with the games short comings first. Cons:  Buggy - Their are definitely bugs in the game. The game is not as polished as it could but, but thats not from a lack of trying (Read Pros later) This is probably the games biggest fault, and that unfortunately has scared alot of people away from the game.  Vague - The game does not hold your hand, you are dumped into a world, and your expected to figure things out on your own, their is very little in the ways of tutorials for alot of the game features. And their are actually alot of features.   Underdeveloped - It seems their could be alot more added.   Pros:  Unique: Between the three games in the Data Hacker series, a unique world, with a decent amount of story and logic put into its creation. While many parallels could be made between this and a couple other story ideas out there, their is enough in the story and plot of the entire series that sets this apart.  Improvements: Writing, and Characters, have all noticbly improved since the first game. The character you get are decentlly interesting, fairly unique, and if you have played the other two games in the series, I do believe that things are heading in an upward direction.  Invested Developer: The Developer has been fairly active on the Discussion Boards, and has dialogued back and forth with almost every comment.  Potential: This game would really shine, if and when it gets just a little bit more polish.   Overall Opinion:  The game was worth the Price of $5 Having gotten 30+ hours out of something that cost me less than eating at a Fast Food resturant. I'm pretty sure most $8 meals have not given me 30 hours of enjoyment. So at its regular price I think its a good deal.  Overall what I would like to see in the future is simply More. More Polish, More Content, More Character Development, More Side Quests, More Character related side quests. (okay, their might not be a need for More classes, the game already has a done, but definitely more development to make them all unique.) I definitely look forward to more content, maybe a DLC, or even a Squel to continue to story.

*click*click*click*  That's what you're going to be doing ALOT of in Torchlight.  The game is an old school hack-and-slash RPG in the same vein as the Diablo series and all the clones that have spawned from that. Players who enjoy those kinds of games and/or are loot and stat junkies will absolutely adore Torchlight however everyone else will probably run out of steam once the main 'story' is completed.   There's tons of loot ranging from common stuff to rare items and sets of items to track down, you have three character types to choose from (the basic three: warrior, rogue, and wizard who each have three skill trees), you've got a pet that will carry loot and even transform into different things when you feed it fish, the different sections of the dungeon are artistically well done, and the range of enemies is definitely substantial.   Other than being a solid dungeon crawler though there's not much else to attract those who aren't quite fans of that genre. The story is paper thin, theres absolutely no characterization in regards to your own avatar or the very few NPCs, the sidequests are uninspired (get this, kill that, go to a different dungeon and get this) and the whole thing just feels like a time sink - one that will definitely appeal to some but will leave others bored and ready to move on to something else after a couple of hours.  The best way to really describe Torchlight: you're going to love it or hate it. Unfortunately I tend to put a good deal of emphasis on narrative, the game world, etc and this game just straight up doesn't have that.

Early Access Review

This game... this game.... Where do I even begin?  A peculiar, remote steppe town is ravaged by a terrible plague. Three healers in the town strive to save its people and defeat the disease. Simple enough, right?  Wrong. With three totally different perspectives, 12 in game days to survive and solve mysteries, over 70 hours of total gameplay time, the situation is much more complicated than you think. It is not just the epidemic that has more than meets the eye, but the entire situation surrounding it. With each character, you have almost two weeks to experience their part of the story before concluding the tale with one of the toughest choices to ever make in a game.  On the surface, this game is rough, crude, illegible, and utterly alienating. With outdated graphics, mediocre mechanics, seeminly indecipherable dialogue, and the lack of a proper tutorial, this game is really hard to get into. It is not impossible to get past the initial footslog however, and once you do, you not only get used to everything, but start understanding its magnificence. It may take a while, but considering how massive the game is, only a small percentage of gameplay time will pass before you get to the diamond in the rough.  As you cut deeper, you start identifying the game's main puzzle pieces. Your understanding of the world around you begins to form definite chuncks, from what it takes to survive to understanding the lore. Eventually, you start putting things together into a bigger picture. What was once seen as disordered fragments of puzzle pieces becomes the wholesome, organic anatomy that the game truly is.  *Pros: - Story and lore is rich, thick, and thought provoking. - Very immersive in making you roleplay as your character, feeling the weight of the crisis on your head, and manipulating your perspective on the world around you. - Atmosphere fits really well: bleak, strange, and surreal.  *Cons: - Bare bones mechanics and gameplay is unflavorful and unexciting (if you ignore the story); lots of text and walking around town. - Survival is tough and the game does not hold your hand.  *Some cons might by considered pros and vice versa.  And if you are intrigued by the story but are too discouraged due to the other factors of the game, there is a remake in the works that is bound to be a vast improvement in almost every way.

review_text: Ok, so this game has a lot of hate. I understand why with all the issues and the bugs. The game runs realtively OK for me. Im running windows 10 Pro with an AMD FX-8320 Eight-Core Processor, 8.0GB RAM, and a NVIDIA GeForce GTX 960 and I can run most of it on max with 60fps.  CONS: -Game runs like ♥♥♥♥ in a blender without a top on for some people. -World graphics are nice but some textures look like a child was shooting paint out of their nose, could just be me though.  PROS: -The community isnt bad at all really, though havent found much people willing to use mics unless you speak up first. -The Zombies is literlly slam jam, so much ♥♥♥♥ to do I cant wait for DLC. -I usually play Zombies but I found the Multiplayer actually enjoyable and found myself playing it more and more. -Supply Drops make it so you feel rewarded when you play Multiplayer -Supply Drops dont make your weapon stronger, cosmetics only! -Supply Drop skins look awesome.  I am not judging the Campaign yet because I havent played it all that much. Zombies has been a very enjoyable roller coaster ride though. The easter eggs are limitless, so much new ways of playing, fast pace decision making, well thrown out story (If I could just figure it out.). I have encountered FPS drops mainly in Zombies in really weird places. Just looking outside of a window and it drops. Multiplayer as I have said is VERY enjoyable, even for a Zombies only fan like myself. If you dont want to take a slight risk of Activision/Treyarch fixing the technical issues and worried that your PC cant run it, maybe you shouldnt buy it and wait it out. I do recommend this game overall though.  Campaign ?/10 Multiplayer 7/10 Zombies 9/10   Once they fix the issues with FPS drops the game is golden. UPDATE: The DLC has fixed some FPS issues. Buy this game.

review_text: Early Access Review

Metro 2033 is one of these games, that could almost be perfect, but miss. The Story is one of the best I have ever seen in a shooter, and the atmosphere is perfect. Many parts - mostly all those where you fight against nazis, communists or human enemies - are great. But unfortunately, the game also features lots of mutant enemies that are quite like dogs except that they have waaaaaay too many hitpoints, and as always in a shooter, these parts suck. If these mutants were rare, special enemies one might still enjoy the game, but they are more frequent than humanoids and, in the end, are even the only opponents.  The length of the game is decent for a shooter - it took me 13 hours to finish and I am far from finding all secrets and exploring everything - but unfortunately only about half of that is actually enjoyable. Therefore, I would not recommend to pay too much for this unless you are an absolute hardcore shooter fan.  However, if you can get it for cheap (for example in the Humble Bundle, where I got it myself) I would still recommend to get it and play it once for the sake of the story.
